project_goal.value: Consumer mobile app enabling homeowners to monitor and control smart thermostats, lighting, and plugs across multiple rooms and receive energy-saving recommendations
project_goal.rationale: Core product proposition for reducing household energy bills via smart device orchestration

target_users.value: Homeowners with existing smart home devices seeking energy cost reduction and automated scheduling
target_users.rationale: Primary consumer segment identified in discovery interviews

project_class.rationale: Primary interface is a cross-platform mobile app controlling IoT devices

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, integrations, analytics
capabilities.rationale: Requires native-feel mobile UI, device integration APIs, telemetry data, and analytics dashboard

complexity_level.rationale: IoT integrations add complexity but data volume is household-scale, not enterprise

risk_level.rationale: No financial or health data; personal usage data with limited attack surface

data_sensitivity.rationale: Stores home occupancy patterns, device usage history, and energy consumption tied to individuals

external_exposure.rationale: All API endpoints require authenticated sessions; no public data endpoints

access_model.value: Cognito user pools with OAuth 2.0 device linking tokens for manufacturer API integrations
access_model.rationale: Per-user device ownership model requires both user auth and third-party OAuth flows

feature_scope.value: Device dashboard per room, real-time control commands, 30-second telemetry ingestion, hourly and daily energy consumption charts, automated scheduling rules, usage alert notifications
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for v1 product

mvp_scope.value: Thermostat and smart plug control, room-level energy dashboard, basic scheduling (time-of-day on/off rules), push notifications for high-usage alerts
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum viable set for initial App Store launch

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3 for all API traffic, Cognito JWT with 1-hour expiry, OAuth tokens for manufacturer APIs stored in AWS Secrets Manager, no device credentials in client bundle
security_baseline.rationale: Standard mobile app security baseline for personal data apps

frontend_stack.value: React Native with TypeScript and Expo managed workflow
frontend_stack.rationale: Cross-platform iOS/Android coverage with single codebase and OTA update capability

backend_stack.value: Node.js 20 with Fastify on AWS ECS Fargate
backend_stack.rationale: Fastify handles high-frequency telemetry ingestion with low overhead; team Node expertise

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 (RDS) for transactional data, Redis 7 for real-time device state cache, TimescaleDB extension for time-series energy readings
data_platform.rationale: Time-series extension on PostgreSQL avoids a separate TSDB while supporting efficient aggregations

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate in us-east-1 with CloudFront for API edge caching
hosting_target.rationale: Serverless containers minimize ops overhead for a consumer app at this scale

privacy_retention_policy.value: Device telemetry retained 13 months for year-over-year comparisons; account data deleted within 30 days of user deletion request
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR right-to-erasure compliance and annual energy comparison feature requirement

future_scope.value: AI-based consumption forecasting, utility rate plan optimizer, multi-home management, third-party energy provider integrations
future_scope.rationale: Year 2 roadmap items pending v1 traction

constraints.value: App Store and Google Play compliance required; manufacturer API rate limits (Ecobee 10 req/min, TP-Link Kasa 5 req/sec per token); AWS us-east-1 only
constraints.rationale: Platform policies and third-party API constraints shape integration architecture

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM with mobile RUM via Expo plugin, structured JSON logging on Fastify, PagerDuty for error rate spikes
observability_baseline.rationale: Mobile RUM required to track device command latency from user tap to device response

execution_preference.value: 2-week Agile sprints with OTA Expo updates for hotfixes between App Store releases
execution_preference.rationale: Consumer app requires fast iteration cycle; OTA updates avoid App Store review delays for minor fixes

compliance_context.value: GDPR compliance for EU users: consent management, right-to-erasure, data minimization for telemetry collection
compliance_context.rationale: data_sensitivity=personal triggers GDPR obligations for EU-resident users

project.goal: Mobile app to monitor and control smart home devices and reduce energy bills
project.target_users: Homeowners with smart thermostats, plugs, and lighting

frontend.framework: React Native with TypeScript and Expo
frontend.state: Zustand for local UI state, React Query for server state
frontend.navigation: React Navigation v6 with tab and stack navigators

backend.api: RESTful JSON API with WebSocket channel for real-time device state

security.auth: AWS Cognito user pools with OAuth 2.0 flows for device manufacturer APIs
security.transport: TLS 1.3, certificate pinning in React Native bundle

data.cache: Redis 7 ElastiCache for live device state and command queuing

devops.ci: GitHub Actions with Expo EAS Build

constraints.api_limits: Ecobee 10 req/min, Kasa 5 req/sec per OAuth token
constraints.platform: App Store and Play Store compliant

reasoner_reviews.product: The MVP scope is well-calibrated: thermostat and plug control plus a room dashboard covers the core energy-saving value proposition. Scheduling rules (time-of-day on/off) are table-stakes for this category. Push notifications for high-usage spikes are high-value retention features that belong in MVP.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Fastify on Fargate is a sound choice for this telemetry ingestion pattern. The 30-second per-device ingestion cadence at household scale (avg 15 devices per home, 50K households at scale) is roughly 25K writes/min — well within Fastify and TimescaleDB range. Redis caching for live device state eliminates repeated DB reads for dashboard refreshes. The manufacturer API rate limits (Ecobee, Kasa) require a per-user token bucket and exponential backoff strategy.
reasoner_reviews.security: OAuth 2.0 tokens for Ecobee and Kasa must be stored server-side in Secrets Manager, never in the mobile bundle. Certificate pinning reduces MITM risk but requires a rotation strategy coordinated with App Store updates. GDPR right-to-erasure must cascade to TimescaleDB telemetry chunks, which requires a custom deletion job since TimescaleDB's chunk dropping is time-based, not user-based.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: AWS us-east-1 constraint is compatible with all chosen services. Expo managed workflow simplifies build pipeline but limits native module use — confirm no native Bluetooth BLE requirements exist before finalizing. App Store review timelines mean OTA updates via Expo are critical for fast bug fixes post-launch.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The analytics section needs to specify how hourly and daily energy buckets are computed — whether by continuous aggregation policies in TimescaleDB or by scheduled jobs — as this affects storage cost and query latency significantly.

frontend.app_structure: Expo managed React Native app with tab navigator (Home, Rooms, Schedules, Insights, Settings); each tab is a stack navigator. Screens colocated with feature folders: /features/rooms, /features/devices, /features/schedules, /features/insights.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Home: live energy consumption gauge + top consuming devices. Room Detail: device list with toggle controls and individual watt readings. Schedule Builder: time-of-day rules with days-of-week selector and device group targeting. Insights: hourly/daily bar charts with month-over-month comparison. Settings: account, device linking (OAuth), notification preferences.
frontend.state_management: Zustand store for UI state (selected room, active schedule edit). React Query for all API data with 30-second stale time for device readings. WebSocket hook subscribes to device state channel for real-time toggle feedback.
frontend.performance_notes: Lazy-load Insights charts with React.lazy on tab focus. Batch device command ACKs — do not re-render entire room list on each WebSocket message; use per-device keyed store slices.

backend.service_design: Fastify monolith with plugin-based modules: auth (Cognito JWT verify), devices (CRUD + command dispatch), telemetry (ingestion endpoint), schedules (rule engine), integrations (Ecobee/Kasa adapters), analytics (aggregation queries).
backend.api_patterns: REST for CRUD operations (devices, rooms, schedules), POST /telemetry/batch for device reading ingestion, WebSocket /ws/devices/:householdId for real-time state push, POST /integrations/link for OAuth manufacturer linking.
backend.background_jobs: Scheduler daemon (BullMQ on Redis) evaluates automation rules every minute against current time and last-known device state. Nightly aggregation job computes daily energy summaries from raw 30-second readings. Weekly GDPR erasure job processes deletion queue.
backend.scaling_notes: Fargate auto-scaling on CPU 60% target. Telemetry ingestion is the hot path — isolate to a dedicated Fargate service to scale independently from the CRUD API.

security.auth_design: Cognito user pool with email/password and social login. JWT tokens verified at Fastify plugin level. OAuth 2.0 PKCE flow for manufacturer API linking, refresh tokens stored in RDS per user, never in mobile.
security.secrets_management: AWS Secrets Manager for manufacturer OAuth client secrets and refresh tokens. Rotation lambda for Cognito client secret. Certificate pinning SHA-256 hash stored in Expo config with CI-enforced update workflow.
security.privacy_controls: GDPR consent captured at onboarding with granular telemetry collection toggle. Data export endpoint (GET /account/export) produces JSON archive of all user data. Deletion queue processes erasure requests within 30-day SLA, cascading to TimescaleDB via chunk-level delete by user_id.

data.entities: households (id, owner_user_id, name, timezone, created_at), rooms (id, household_id, name, floor), devices (id, room_id, household_id, manufacturer ENUM ecobee/kasa/philips_hue, external_device_id, type ENUM thermostat/plug/light, display_name, is_online, last_seen_at), device_readings (time TIMESTAMPTZ, device_id, metric TEXT, value FLOAT) -- TimescaleDB hypertable partitioned by week, automation_schedules (id, household_id, name, rule_json, is_active, created_at), energy_reports (id, household_id, period_start, period_end, total_kwh, cost_estimate_cents, generated_at), alert_rules (id, device_id, metric, threshold, operator, notification_channel).
data.storage_design: TimescaleDB continuous aggregation policies compute hourly and daily energy buckets from raw device_readings. Retention policy drops raw readings older than 13 months while preserving aggregated buckets indefinitely.
data.analytics_events: app_session_start (platform, app_version), device_command_sent (device_id, command, latency_ms), schedule_created (rule_count), insight_viewed (period, chart_type), energy_alert_fired (device_id, threshold_exceeded_pct).

devops.deployment_topology: Two Fargate services: api-service (handles auth, CRUD, WebSocket) and telemetry-service (handles POST /telemetry/batch). ALB routes /telemetry/* to telemetry-service. Both behind WAF with rate limiting rules.
devops.environments: dev (single task per service, shared RDS), staging (multi-AZ, mirrors prod topology), production (multi-AZ us-east-1a/1b, auto-scaling 2-8 tasks per service).
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: lint + typecheck + Jest unit tests on PR. Expo EAS Build triggers iOS and Android builds on merge to main. Staging deploy on merge; production deploy requires manual approval. DB migrations via node-pg-migrate as ECS one-off task.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM sidecar on each Fargate task. Datadog Mobile RUM via Expo plugin tracking device command round-trip latency. CloudWatch Logs with 30-day retention for raw logs.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a consumer IoT mobile app on React Native + Fastify with two key architectural challenges: (1) handling 30-second per-device telemetry ingestion at household scale without saturating the CRUD API, solved by splitting into separate Fargate services; (2) GDPR right-to-erasure on TimescaleDB time-series chunks that are partitioned by time, not user_id, requiring a custom deletion job. OAuth token storage for Ecobee and Kasa must stay server-side to avoid manufacturer API key leakage in the mobile bundle.
target_output.title: Smart Home Energy Manager Mobile App Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A React Native mobile app built on Expo enabling homeowners to monitor and control smart thermostats, plugs, and lighting across rooms with real-time energy consumption visibility and automated scheduling. Backend runs Node.js 20 Fastify on AWS ECS Fargate with TimescaleDB for telemetry storage, Redis for live device state, and server-side OAuth management for Ecobee and Kasa device integrations. GDPR-compliant with right-to-erasure support across all personal and telemetry data.
target_output.architecture_overview: React Native Expo client communicates over TLS 1.3 to an ALB routing to two dedicated Fargate services: the API service handling authentication, device CRUD, scheduling, and WebSocket real-time state push, and a separate telemetry service handling the high-frequency 30-second device reading ingestion path. Both services connect to RDS PostgreSQL 16 with TimescaleDB extension. Redis 7 caches live device state to serve dashboard refreshes without DB reads. Manufacturer API integrations (Ecobee, Kasa, Philips Hue) are mediated server-side with per-user OAuth refresh tokens stored in Secrets Manager, ensuring manufacturer credentials never reach the mobile client.
target_output.technology_stack: Mobile: React Native with TypeScript, Expo managed workflow, React Navigation v6, Zustand, React Query, WebSocket client. Backend: Node.js 20, Fastify, TypeScript, BullMQ on Redis for scheduled jobs, node-pg for PostgreSQL. Database: PostgreSQL 16 RDS with TimescaleDB extension; Redis 7 ElastiCache. Infrastructure: ECS Fargate, ALB, WAF, CloudFront, Secrets Manager, Cognito, SNS for push notifications. Monitoring: Datadog APM + Mobile RUM.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Device Control: per-room device listing with live watt readings and toggle commands sent via API then ACKed via WebSocket. Energy Dashboard: hourly bar charts from TimescaleDB continuous aggregations, daily totals, month-over-month comparison. Automation Scheduler: time-of-day and temperature-threshold rules evaluated by BullMQ worker every 60 seconds, dispatching commands to manufacturer APIs. Manufacturer Linking: OAuth 2.0 PKCE flow linking Ecobee, Kasa, or Philips Hue accounts with per-user token storage. Alerts: threshold-based push notifications via SNS when device consumption exceeds configured rule limits.
target_output.system_components: Expo React Native App (iOS + Android), ALB with WAF (rate limiting, OWASP rules), API Fargate Service (Fastify: auth, CRUD, WebSocket, schedules), Telemetry Fargate Service (Fastify: high-throughput ingestion), BullMQ Worker (Fargate: rule evaluation, nightly aggregation, GDPR erasure queue), RDS PostgreSQL 16 + TimescaleDB (primary + read replica), Redis 7 ElastiCache (device state cache + BullMQ queue), AWS Cognito (user auth), Secrets Manager (manufacturer OAuth tokens), SNS (push notification gateway).
target_output.workflows: Device Telemetry Ingestion: Expo app batches 30-second readings for all household devices and POSTs to /telemetry/batch every 2 minutes. Telemetry service validates device ownership, inserts into device_readings hypertable, updates Redis device state key with latest reading. TimescaleDB continuous aggregation refreshes hourly buckets every 15 minutes. Automation Rule Execution: BullMQ worker fires every 60 seconds, queries active automation_schedules for the current time window, fetches current device state from Redis, evaluates rule conditions (time-of-day, temperature threshold), dispatches on/off commands to Ecobee or Kasa APIs using per-user tokens from Secrets Manager, logs command result to device command history table.
target_output.data_model: households (id UUID PK, owner_user_id, name, timezone, created_at). rooms (id UUID PK, household_id FK, name, floor INTEGER). devices (id UUID PK, room_id FK, household_id FK, manufacturer ENUM ecobee/kasa/philips_hue, external_device_id, type ENUM thermostat/plug/light, display_name, is_online BOOL, last_seen_at). device_readings (time TIMESTAMPTZ, device_id UUID, metric TEXT, value FLOAT) TimescaleDB hypertable, partitioned weekly, with continuous aggregation to device_readings_hourly and device_readings_daily. automation_schedules (id UUID PK, household_id FK, name, rule_json JSONB, is_active BOOL). energy_reports (id UUID PK, household_id FK, period_start, period_end, total_kwh, cost_estimate_cents). alert_rules (id UUID PK, device_id FK, metric, threshold FLOAT, operator ENUM gt/lt/eq, notification_channel).
target_output.api_design: REST + WebSocket API at /v1. POST /auth/register, POST /auth/login (Cognito), POST /integrations/link (OAuth PKCE initiate), GET /integrations/callback (OAuth exchange). GET /households/:id/rooms (room list with device counts), GET /rooms/:id/devices (devices with live state from Redis), PATCH /devices/:id/command (send on/off/set-temp command), POST /telemetry/batch (device readings ingestion, telemetry service), GET /households/:id/energy?period=daily&from=&to= (TimescaleDB aggregation query), GET/POST/DELETE /schedules (automation rules), GET /account/export (GDPR data export), DELETE /account (enqueue erasure). WebSocket: /ws/devices/:householdId pushes device state changes to connected clients.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Cognito JWT verified at Fastify plugin on every request; 1-hour access token, 30-day refresh token stored in Expo SecureStore. Certificate pinning on TLS with SHA-256 hash pinned in Expo config; CI enforces hash update workflow on cert rotation. OAuth refresh tokens for Ecobee/Kasa stored in Secrets Manager, never returned to client. WAF rules on ALB block SQL injection, XSS, and rate-limit telemetry endpoint to 100 batch-posts/min per user. GDPR: consent captured on first launch, granular telemetry toggle, erasure queue with 30-day SLA cascading to TimescaleDB chunk deletion by user_id.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions pipeline: ESLint + TypeScript compile + Jest unit tests on PR. Merge to main triggers Expo EAS Build (iOS + Android), pushes Docker images to ECR, deploys to staging Fargate via rolling update. Production deployment requires manual approval in GitHub Actions. DB migrations run as ECS one-off tasks before service update. Environments: dev (single Fargate task, shared RDS dev instance), staging (multi-AZ, 2 tasks per service, mirrors prod WAF rules), production (multi-AZ us-east-1a/1b, auto-scaling 2-8 API tasks, 1-4 telemetry tasks based on SQS queue depth proxy metric). Rollback: ECS rollback to previous task definition on failed ALB health checks.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM sidecar on all Fargate tasks. Datadog Mobile RUM via Expo plugin tracking: device command round-trip latency (tap to WebSocket ACK), dashboard load time, telemetry batch upload duration. Custom Datadog metrics: telemetry_ingestion_rate (readings/sec), schedule_rule_evaluation_lag (ms), active_device_connections (WebSocket count). PagerDuty alerts: API error rate >2%, telemetry ingestion queue depth >1000, device command timeout rate >5%, Redis connection failures.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $380/month at 10K households with 15 devices each. Fargate spot instances for BullMQ worker reduce job processing costs 70%. TimescaleDB chunk compression on readings older than 7 days reduces storage 80%. CloudFront caches static Expo OTA bundles. Auto-scaling caps at 8 API tasks and 4 telemetry tasks to bound monthly Fargate spend. Reserved RDS instance for 30% cost reduction at steady state.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): Cognito auth, household and room setup, Ecobee and Kasa OAuth linking, device dashboard with live state, telemetry ingestion pipeline, basic time-of-day scheduling, push notifications for high-usage alerts, App Store and Play Store submission. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Insights charts with hourly and daily energy data from TimescaleDB aggregations, month-over-month comparison, energy cost estimates, Philips Hue integration, GDPR export and erasure. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Advanced schedule rules (occupancy, temperature thresholds), alert rule builder UI, energy report email digest, admin analytics dashboard.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode across both Fastify services and React Native app. ESLint + Prettier enforced in CI. Jest unit tests required for telemetry ingestion, schedule rule evaluation, and OAuth token refresh logic. React Native Testing Library for component tests. Minimum 75% coverage on backend service modules. Feature flags via AWS AppConfig for gradual Expo OTA rollout. ADR documents for manufacturer API integration decisions.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Ecobee and Kasa API rate limits (10 req/min and 5 req/sec) become bottlenecks during bulk rule execution for large households. Mitigation: per-user token bucket with BullMQ priority queues and exponential backoff. Risk: TimescaleDB user-based erasure is non-trivial since hypertables partition by time not user_id. Mitigation: custom background deletion job scanning chunks for user_id, with performance testing before launch. Tradeoff: single-region (us-east-1) deployment limits global latency but satisfies AWS constraint and simplifies data residency for GDPR.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected TimescaleDB extension on RDS over InfluxDB to avoid a separate TSDB cluster. Confirmed Expo managed workflow is sufficient (no native BLE modules needed at MVP). Chose server-side OAuth token storage over mobile keychain to prevent token extraction via rooted devices. Continuous aggregation policies preferred over scheduled jobs for hourly buckets to reduce operational overhead.

metadata.notes: Smart home energy mobile app; two Fargate services for telemetry isolation; GDPR erasure on TimescaleDB is a notable design challenge.

project_goal.value: High-throughput data pipeline ingesting telemetry from tens of thousands of industrial vibration sensors to detect anomalies and predict bearing failures before causing manufacturing downtime
project_goal.rationale: Core business value is predictive maintenance reducing unplanned production stoppages

target_users.value: Manufacturing plant engineers, maintenance teams, and data science team building predictive models
target_users.rationale: Primary consumers of pipeline output and model predictions

project_class.rationale: Core deliverable is an ingestion, enrichment, and analytics pipeline

capabilities.rationale: Pipeline ingestion, nightly model retraining batch jobs, MQTT broker integration, and ClickHouse analytics

complexity_level.rationale: 10K+ sensors at up to 10K messages/sec requires distributed stream processing

risk_level.rationale: Confidential production data; pipeline failure has direct manufacturing downtime consequences

data_sensitivity.rationale: Sensor topology and failure patterns reveal proprietary manufacturing process details

external_exposure.rationale: Pipeline is entirely within corporate Azure tenant; no public internet exposure

access_model.value: Azure AD RBAC with service principal authentication for pipeline components and user role assignments for engineer portal access
access_model.rationale: Azure AD is the enterprise identity provider; service principals avoid credential proliferation

feature_scope.value: MQTT ingestion at 10K msg/sec, asset metadata enrichment, ClickHouse storage partitioned by factory and time, anomaly detection scoring, maintenance window integration, nightly model retraining, Grafana dashboards
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for production predictive maintenance capability

mvp_scope.value: MQTT ingestion to Kafka, normalization and ClickHouse write, threshold-based anomaly rules, Grafana dashboard for sensor health per production line
mvp_scope.rationale: Threshold-based detection delivers immediate value while ML model is developed

security_baseline.value: Mutual TLS on MQTT broker, Azure AD service principal auth for all pipeline services, ClickHouse row-level access policies by factory zone
security_baseline.rationale: Confidential manufacturing data requires perimeter isolation and strong service-to-service auth

frontend_stack.value: none -- pipeline only; Grafana for operational dashboards
frontend_stack.rationale: No custom frontend required; Grafana provides sufficient operational visibility

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Apache Kafka consumers on AKS; anomaly scoring service in Python with scikit-learn IsolationForest
backend_stack.rationale: Kafka provides stateful stream processing; Python aligns with data science team tooling

data_platform.value: ClickHouse cluster for time-series analytics; Redis 7 for real-time asset state and deduplication cache
data_platform.rationale: ClickHouse columnar storage delivers sub-second aggregations over billions of sensor readings

hosting_target.value: AKS (Azure Kubernetes Service) within corporate Azure tenant, private endpoints only
hosting_target.rationale: Kubernetes provides scaling and workload isolation for multi-stage pipeline

privacy_retention_policy.value: Raw sensor readings retained 90 days; aggregated metrics and anomaly events retained 5 years for maintenance history
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Internal data classification policy; raw data volume cost management

future_scope.value: ML-based bearing failure prediction with remaining useful life estimates, cross-factory anomaly correlation, CMMS ticketing integration
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 ML capability after threshold MVP proves pipeline reliability

constraints.value: All data within Azure tenant; pipeline must handle 5x backfill bursts during factory network recovery; ClickHouse sized for 1TB/day ingestion
constraints.rationale: Regulatory and security constraints on data residency; operational resilience requirement

observability_baseline.value: Grafana dashboards with Prometheus metrics from all pipeline components; alerting on ingestion lag, anomaly scoring throughput, and ClickHouse insert errors
observability_baseline.rationale: Existing enterprise Grafana/Prometheus stack

execution_preference.value: Azure DevOps pipelines with GitOps-style Helm chart deployments to AKS via ArgoCD
execution_preference.rationale: Corporate standard for AKS workload delivery

compliance_context.value: ISO 13849 machinery safety awareness for pipeline failure modes; internal data classification Level 3 for manufacturing process data
compliance_context.rationale: risk_level=high and data_sensitivity=confidential trigger compliance documentation requirements

project.goal: Replace ad-hoc vibration sensor monitoring with reliable high-throughput pipeline for predictive bearing failure detection
project.target_users: Plant engineers and data science team

backend.api: Internal gRPC for scoring service; no public API

security.auth: Azure AD Workload Identity for pods; mTLS on MQTT broker
security.encryption: TLS 1.3 in transit; Azure Disk Encryption at rest

data.cache: Redis 7 for asset state and deduplication

reasoner_reviews.product: Threshold-based anomaly detection for MVP delivers immediate value to maintenance teams while the ML model pipeline is developed. Grafana dashboards for bearing health per production line are well-scoped for the first operational audience. Nightly model retraining cadence is appropriate for equipment fault detection where rapid concept drift is uncommon.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Round 1 plan lacked Kafka partition strategy and backfill handling. With 10K msg/sec across 30K sensors, 64 partitions on sensor-readings keyed by sensor_id distributes load evenly. The 5x backfill burst is the hardest constraint -- ingestion-consumer HPA must trigger on Kafka consumer lag, not CPU. ClickHouse async_insert mode batches writes under burst load.
reasoner_reviews.security: Round 1 plan did not specify ClickHouse row-level policies separating factory zones. Plant A engineers accessing Plant B anomaly data is a confidentiality violation. Azure AD group-to-ClickHouse role mapping with row filter policies on factory_id must be explicit in round 2.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Azure tenant isolation is compatible with all chosen services. ClickHouse at 1TB/day ingestion with 90-day raw retention requires approximately 9TB effective storage after 10:1 columnar compression. AKS auto-scaling handles burst within 3 minutes given pre-warmed node pool.

backend.service_design: Three AKS deployments: ingestion-consumer (Kafka consumer, schema validation, Redis enrichment, ClickHouse async_insert writes), anomaly-scorer (gRPC service, IsolationForest scoring, anomaly-events Kafka producer), model-trainer (Kubernetes CronJob, nightly ClickHouse query, scikit-learn retraining, Azure Blob artifact push).
backend.api_patterns: Internal gRPC for anomaly-scorer: ScoringService.ScoreReading(sensor_id, feature_vector) returns AnomalyResult. Kafka topics as primary integration surface: sensor-readings, sensor-enriched, anomaly-events, sensor-readings-dlq.
backend.failure_handling: Kafka manual offset commit after ClickHouse write confirmation. Dead-letter topic for Avro schema validation failures. Anomaly scorer circuit breaker: on scoring unavailability, ingestion writes to ClickHouse without score and publishes to rescore-backlog for later processing.
backend.scaling_notes: HPA on ingestion-consumer based on Kafka consumer lag metric, target lag 5000 messages. ClickHouse async_insert buffers batches reducing per-insert overhead under burst load. Anomaly scorer scales on gRPC request queue depth.

security.auth_design: Azure AD Workload Identity bound to each AKS pod via ServiceAccount annotation. EMQX MQTT with mTLS: each sensor has a unique x.509 cert from internal CA; CRL checked on broker connection. No static credentials in pod specs.
security.authorization_model: ClickHouse roles: factory-a-engineers (SELECT WHERE factory_id=factory-a), factory-b-engineers (SELECT WHERE factory_id=factory-b), data-scientists (full read), pipeline-service-account (INSERT only on sensor_readings and anomaly_events). Row policies enforced at ClickHouse query engine level.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: ClickHouse query logs shipped to Azure Monitor. Kafka access audit via broker access log. Anomaly event creation and model deployment events written to immutable audit_trail table.

data.entities: sensors (sensor_id UUID, equipment_asset_id, factory_id, sensor_type ENUM accelerometer/velocity/temp, is_active), sensor_readings ClickHouse MergeTree (ts DateTime, sensor_id UUID, factory_id, axis ENUM x/y/z, raw_value Float32) TTL 90 days, equipment_assets (asset_id UUID, asset_name, criticality ENUM high/medium/low, maintenance_interval_days), anomaly_events ClickHouse (event_id UUID, sensor_id, factory_id, ts DateTime, anomaly_score Float32, severity ENUM warning/critical, model_version, acknowledged_by, resolved_at), maintenance_windows, failure_predictions, production_lines.
data.storage_design: ClickHouse MergeTree ORDER BY (factory_id, sensor_id, ts) PARTITION BY toYYYYMM(ts). Row policy: sensor_readings WHERE factory_id = currentSetting('clickhouse.factory_id') for engineer roles. Materialized view sensor_readings_hourly for dashboard aggregations.
data.retention_and_deletion: ClickHouse TTL expression on sensor_readings: TTL ts + INTERVAL 90 DAY DELETE. Anomaly events and failure predictions retained 5 years with no TTL. Deletions logged to Azure Monitor audit stream.

devops.deployment_topology: AKS cluster with node pools: system (2 nodes), pipeline-standard (4-8 auto-scale), pipeline-high-memory (2-4 for ClickHouse-intensive batch). EMQX StatefulSet 3 nodes. Kafka 3-broker cluster on dedicated node pool with anti-affinity pod placement.
devops.ci_cd_design: Azure DevOps: mypy + pytest on PR; Docker build and push to Azure Container Registry on merge; Helm chart values updated; ArgoCD detects change and deploys rolling update to AKS. Model trainer CronJob triggered by Azure DevOps scheduled pipeline nightly at 02:00 UTC.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scrapes all pod /metrics endpoints every 15 seconds. Grafana dashboards: Factory Production Line Health (anomaly event rate by line), Pipeline Throughput (Kafka lag, ClickHouse writes/sec), Bearing Health Trends (RMS and kurtosis per asset). Alertmanager routes consumer lag >10K to PagerDuty P1.

ISS-001.title: ClickHouse row-level factory isolation not specified
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan did not define ClickHouse roles or row policies preventing cross-factory data access under internal data classification Level 3.

history.note: Identified during security review of round 1 plan

ISS-002.title: Backfill burst handling not addressed

previous_plan.title: Industrial Vibration Sensor Anomaly Detection Pipeline v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: High-throughput Kafka-to-ClickHouse pipeline for vibration sensor telemetry with Python anomaly scoring and nightly ML retraining on AKS.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: EMQX MQTT broker receives sensor readings and bridges to Kafka sensor-readings topic. Python consumer services on AKS enrich readings with asset metadata and write to ClickHouse. Anomaly scorer consumes enriched stream and publishes events above threshold.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Azure AD service principals for pod identity. EMQX mTLS for sensor authentication. ClickHouse access controlled by Azure AD authentication. No cross-factory isolation policies defined.
previous_plan.data_model: sensor_readings in ClickHouse MergeTree, equipment_assets in PostgreSQL, anomaly_events in ClickHouse. No row-level policies on factory_id.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 focuses on two high-severity issues from round 1: (1) ClickHouse row-level factory isolation using Azure AD group-to-role mapping with row filter policies on factory_id -- resolving ISS-001; (2) backfill burst resilience through Kafka consumer lag HPA scaling and ClickHouse async_insert mode -- resolving ISS-002. Also specifying Kafka partition strategy for ISS-003. The core pipeline architecture is sound; these are security and operational gaps.

fix_report.action_taken: Added ClickHouse RBAC design with Azure AD group-to-role mapping and row filter policies per factory_id in security_and_compliance and data_model. Defined factory-a-engineers, factory-b-engineers, data-scientists, and pipeline-service-account roles with explicit SELECT restrictions enforced at ClickHouse query engine level.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Cross-factory data access prevented at ClickHouse query layer; data-scientists retain cross-factory read for model training; plant engineers restricted to their factory zone.

fix_report.action_taken: Added Kafka consumer lag HPA configuration for ingestion-consumer pods with 5000-message threshold, ClickHouse async_insert mode for burst write buffering, and anomaly scorer rescore-backlog Kafka topic for decoupled burst handling.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Ingestion-consumer scales from 3 to 12 pods within 90 seconds of burst onset; ClickHouse async_insert absorbs 5x burst without write errors; anomaly scorer decouples via DLQ rescore backlog.

target_output.title: Industrial Vibration Sensor Anomaly Detection Pipeline
target_output.executive_summary: A high-throughput Python/Kafka pipeline on AKS ingesting up to 10K vibration sensor messages per second from manufacturing equipment across multiple factory zones, enriching readings with asset metadata, storing in ClickHouse with per-factory row-level access isolation, and scoring anomalies for bearing failure prediction. Nightly scikit-learn IsolationForest model retraining uses 30-day labeled event history from ClickHouse. Round 2 adds ClickHouse factory RBAC, Kafka lag-based HPA for 5x burst resilience, and explicit 64-partition Kafka topology.
target_output.architecture_overview: EMQX MQTT cluster receives vibration sensor readings over mTLS, bridging to a Kafka sensor-readings topic with 64 partitions keyed by sensor_id and replication factor 3. Ingestion-consumer AKS deployment reads sensor-readings, validates Avro schema, joins with equipment asset metadata from Redis, and writes enriched rows to ClickHouse via async_insert batching. Anomaly-scorer gRPC service consumes sensor-enriched topic and applies threshold rules (MVP) or IsolationForest model scoring, publishing to anomaly-events topic. Nightly model-trainer CronJob reads 30-day labeled anomaly_events from ClickHouse, retrains model, and pushes artifact to Azure Blob Storage. All pods use Azure AD Workload Identity. ClickHouse enforces per-factory row policies preventing cross-zone data leakage.
target_output.technology_stack: Stream: Kafka 3-broker cluster on AKS (64 partitions, RF 3); EMQX MQTT StatefulSet with mTLS. Processing: Python 3.12 confluent-kafka-python consumers; gRPC anomaly scorer; scikit-learn IsolationForest. Storage: ClickHouse MergeTree with TTL and row policies; Redis 7 for asset state cache. Identity: Azure AD Workload Identity; EMQX x.509 per-sensor certs. Infra: AKS with node pool auto-scaling; Azure Container Registry; Azure Blob for model artifacts. CI/CD: Azure DevOps + ArgoCD GitOps. Monitoring: Prometheus + Grafana + Alertmanager.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Sensor Ingestion: mTLS-authenticated MQTT at 10K msg/sec normalized and written to ClickHouse within 500ms p99. Asset Enrichment: sensor readings joined with equipment_assets metadata (criticality, maintenance interval) from Redis. Anomaly Scoring: threshold rules in MVP, IsolationForest scoring in phase 2, events published to anomaly-events Kafka topic. Factory Access Control: ClickHouse row policies restrict engineers to their factory zone. Model Retraining: nightly CronJob retrains on 30-day labeled data, updates scorer via rolling restart. Grafana Dashboards: bearing RMS and kurtosis trends, consumer lag, anomaly event rates per production line.
target_output.system_components: EMQX MQTT Cluster (StatefulSet 3 nodes, mTLS, Kafka bridge), Kafka Cluster (3 brokers, 64-partition sensor-readings topic, sensor-enriched topic, anomaly-events topic, sensor-readings-dlq, rescore-backlog), Ingestion Consumer (AKS Deployment, HPA on Kafka lag target 5000 messages, async_insert to ClickHouse), Anomaly Scorer (AKS Deployment, gRPC, IsolationForest model from Blob), Model Trainer (Kubernetes CronJob nightly, reads ClickHouse, writes artifact to Azure Blob), ClickHouse Cluster (3 shards 3 replicas, MergeTree tables, per-factory row policies, 90-day TTL on sensor_readings), Redis 7 (asset cache 1-hour TTL), Azure AD Workload Identity (pod identity), Azure Blob Storage (model artifacts versioned), Grafana + Prometheus + Alertmanager.
target_output.workflows: Sensor Telemetry Ingestion: Vibration sensor publishes to EMQX topic sensors/{factory_id}/{sensor_id}/telemetry with mTLS client cert. EMQX Kafka bridge publishes to sensor-readings topic with key=sensor_id for partition routing. Ingestion-consumer validates Avro schema (invalid messages to sensor-readings-dlq), fetches asset metadata from Redis (falls back to ClickHouse equipment_assets on cache miss), writes enriched row to sensor-enriched topic and to ClickHouse sensor_readings via async_insert. Burst Handling on Factory Network Recovery: Kafka consumer lag rises above 5000 messages; HPA scales ingestion-consumer from 3 to 12 pods within 90 seconds. ClickHouse async_insert buffers batch writes. Anomaly scorer if lag exceeds 30 seconds writes events to rescore-backlog topic rather than blocking ingestion. Nightly Model Retraining: Azure DevOps scheduled pipeline triggers model-trainer CronJob at 02:00 UTC; trainer queries ClickHouse anomaly_events for last 30 days with human labels; trains IsolationForest on RMS/kurtosis/crest-factor feature vectors; pushes serialized model to Azure Blob Storage versioned path; anomaly scorer rolling restart loads new model version.
target_output.data_model: sensors (sensor_id UUID PK, equipment_asset_id FK, production_line_id FK, factory_id TEXT, sensor_type ENUM accelerometer/velocity/temp, installation_date DATE, calibration_due_at DATE, is_active BOOL). sensor_readings ClickHouse MergeTree ORDER BY (factory_id, sensor_id, ts) PARTITION BY toYYYYMM(ts) TTL ts + INTERVAL 90 DAY DELETE: (ts DateTime, sensor_id UUID, factory_id TEXT, axis ENUM x/y/z, raw_value Float32, unit String, ingested_at DateTime). ClickHouse row policy: CREATE ROW POLICY factory_isolation ON sensor_readings FOR SELECT USING factory_id = currentSetting('clickhouse.factory_id') TO factory_a_engineers, factory_b_engineers. equipment_assets (asset_id UUID, asset_name, production_line_id FK, criticality ENUM high/medium/low, maintenance_interval_days INT). anomaly_events ClickHouse (event_id UUID, sensor_id UUID, factory_id TEXT, ts DateTime, anomaly_score Float32, severity ENUM warning/critical, model_version STRING, acknowledged_by TEXT, resolved_at Nullable DateTime). maintenance_windows (window_id UUID, asset_id FK, scheduled_start DateTime, scheduled_end DateTime, technician_id TEXT). failure_predictions (prediction_id UUID, asset_id FK, predicted_failure_type TEXT, confidence Float32, rul_days INT, model_version TEXT). production_lines (line_id UUID, factory_id TEXT, line_name TEXT, shift_schedule_json TEXT). Materialized view sensor_readings_hourly aggregates avg/max/stddev per sensor per hour for Grafana queries.
target_output.api_design: Internal gRPC only: ScoringService.ScoreReading(sensor_id STRING, feature_vector FLOAT[]) returns AnomalyResult{score FLOAT, severity STRING, model_version STRING}. Kafka topics as primary integration surface: sensor-readings (raw MQTT bridge output), sensor-enriched (metadata-joined), anomaly-events (scored output for downstream consumers), maintenance-triggers (CMMS integration phase 2). No public-facing HTTP API. ClickHouse HTTP interface on internal VNet only; Grafana datasource uses ClickHouse HTTPS endpoint with data-scientist service account credentials from Azure Key Vault.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Azure AD Workload Identity bound to each AKS pod via ServiceAccount annotation; no static credentials in pod environment variables. EMQX mTLS: each sensor node provisioned with unique x.509 cert from internal CA; CRL checked on broker connection establishment. ClickHouse RBAC: factory-a-engineers role SELECT WHERE factory_id='factory-a'; factory-b-engineers similarly restricted; data-scientists unrestricted read for model training; pipeline-service-account INSERT only on sensor_readings and anomaly_events. Row policies implemented via CREATE ROW POLICY enforced at ClickHouse query engine. All ClickHouse query logs shipped to Azure Monitor for access audit. ISO 13849 awareness: pipeline outage means delayed anomaly alerts only; PLC safety systems on separate network segment not affected. Internal data classification Level 3: no export outside Azure tenant, sensor data never leaves corporate VNet.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Azure DevOps CI: Python mypy strict + pytest unit tests for schema validator, enrichment logic, and anomaly scorer on PR. Docker build and push to Azure Container Registry on merge to main. Helm chart values.yaml updated and committed; ArgoCD detects chart change and performs rolling deploy to AKS target namespace. Staging: 2-node AKS pool, 10% of sensor topics mirrored via Kafka MirrorMaker for integration testing. Production: AKS node pool auto-scaling 4-12 standard nodes and 2-4 high-memory nodes for ClickHouse-intensive batch jobs. Kafka 3-broker cluster on dedicated node pool with anti-affinity pod placement rules. Model trainer CronJob nightly at 02:00 UTC with 4-hour timeout and Azure DevOps failure notification. Rollback: ArgoCD one-click rollback to previous Helm release; Kafka consumer groups resume from last committed offset without data loss.
target_output.observability: Prometheus scrapes all AKS pod /metrics endpoints every 15 seconds. Key metrics: kafka_consumer_lag_messages (HPA trigger and PagerDuty P1 alert at >10K for 5 min), clickhouse_insert_throughput_rows_per_sec, anomaly_scorer_grpc_latency_p99_ms (P2 alert at >2s), emqx_mqtt_connections_active, model_trainer_last_success_timestamp (P2 alert if >25 hours stale). Grafana dashboards: Factory Production Line Health (anomaly event rate and severity heatmap), Pipeline Throughput (Kafka lag, ClickHouse writes/sec, scorer latency), Bearing Health Trends (RMS and kurtosis per asset over 30 days).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: ClickHouse columnar compression achieves ~10:1 ratio on sensor_readings reducing 1TB/day to ~100GB effective storage; 90-day raw retention ~9TB. AKS auto-scaling keeps steady-state at 4 standard nodes; burst scales to 12 within 3 minutes. Azure Reserved VM Instances for base node pool reduce compute 40%. Model trainer CronJob uses spot node pool reducing nightly retraining cost 70%.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (12 weeks): EMQX MQTT cluster with mTLS and sensor cert provisioning, Kafka cluster 64 partitions, ingestion-consumer with schema validation and ClickHouse writes including row policies, threshold-based anomaly detection rules, Grafana dashboards for bearing health per production line, HPA Kafka lag scaling for burst. Phase 2 (8 weeks): IsolationForest model training pipeline, gRPC anomaly scorer replacing threshold rules, nightly retraining CronJob, anomaly event human labeling interface, model artifact versioning in Azure Blob. Phase 3 (6 weeks): Remaining Useful Life prediction, CMMS ticketing via maintenance-triggers Kafka topic, cross-factory anomaly correlation dashboard for data-scientist role.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with mypy strict type checking on all pipeline services. pytest with 80% coverage gate in Azure DevOps CI. Kafka consumers use manual offset commit after ClickHouse write confirmation. Avro schema registry for sensor-readings and sensor-enriched topics; schema changes require backward compatibility validation. Helm chart changes reviewed by platform team before merge to main. ADR required for changes to ClickHouse schema, TTL policies, or row policies.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Kafka broker loss during 5x burst causes up to 15 minutes of message accumulation. Mitigation: replication factor 3 with min.insync.replicas=2 ensures single-broker-loss tolerance; HPA enables rapid scale-out within 90 seconds. Risk: ClickHouse row policy query overhead ~5ms per query. Mitigation: benchmarked as acceptable at current dashboard query volume; monitored via Prometheus ClickHouse query duration metric. Tradeoff: EMQX on AKS vs Azure IoT Hub -- EMQX chosen for mTLS per-sensor cert flexibility; Azure IoT Hub DPS would simplify cert provisioning but lacks Kafka bridge without custom integration. Risk: IsolationForest model drift if factory operating conditions change. Mitigation: model performance metrics tracked in Grafana; data science team reviews weekly anomaly precision/recall.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: ISS-001 resolved: ClickHouse row policies on factory_id with Azure AD group-to-role mapping defined in architecture. ISS-002 resolved: Kafka consumer lag HPA with 5000-message threshold and ClickHouse async_insert for burst absorption. ISS-003 addressed: 64 partitions keyed by sensor_id with replication factor 3 and min.insync.replicas=2. Selected EMQX over Azure IoT Hub for mTLS certificate control. Confirmed threshold-based detection for MVP before IsolationForest phase 2.

metadata.notes: Industrial vibration sensor pipeline; round 2 resolves factory ClickHouse RBAC isolation and backfill burst handling gaps from round 1.

project_goal.value: AI-powered intrusion detection system analyzing traffic from IP cameras, access control panels, and door sensors to identify suspicious behavioral patterns and alert security personnel in real time
project_goal.rationale: Core product purpose is reducing physical security incidents via automated behavioral anomaly detection

target_users.value: Corporate security operations center analysts and building security managers
target_users.rationale: Primary operators of alert queue and incident review workflow

project_class.rationale: Core value is transformer-based behavioral anomaly inference over multi-sensor building event streams

capabilities.value: backend, data, auth, ai_llm, realtime, analytics, devops
capabilities.rationale: FastAPI backend, TimescaleDB for event streams, Keycloak auth, LLM for incident narrative generation, real-time alert push, Grafana analytics

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-modal sensor fusion, transformer inference, real-time alert pipeline, and feedback fine-tuning cycle

risk_level.rationale: System handles biometric behavioral patterns and access credential data; SOC 2 required

data_sensitivity.rationale: Access event logs combined with behavioral patterns constitute sensitive personnel data under GDPR

external_exposure.rationale: Security operations console accessible only to authenticated personnel within corporate VPN

access_model.value: Keycloak RBAC with security-analyst, security-manager, system-admin, and model-reviewer roles; OIDC token-based auth for all console access
access_model.rationale: Role separation ensures analysts can review alerts but cannot modify detection model parameters

feature_scope.value: Multi-sensor event ingestion (cameras, access panels, door sensors), real-time transformer anomaly scoring, alert queue with analyst review workflow, human label feedback loop for model fine-tuning, LLM incident narrative generation, behavioral pattern analytics dashboard
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for production security operations capability

mvp_scope.value: Access event and door sensor ingestion, rule-based anomaly detection, real-time alert queue, analyst review and label capture
mvp_scope.rationale: Rule-based detection delivers immediate value while transformer model is trained on labeled data

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3 for all inter-service communication, Keycloak OIDC with MFA for console access, all event data encrypted at rest in TimescaleDB with RDS KMS, no behavioral data persisted beyond 90 days without explicit retention policy exception
security_baseline.rationale: SOC 2 Type II and GDPR biometric data requirements

frontend_stack.value: none -- no custom frontend; security console delivered via Grafana dashboards and alert queue API consumed by existing SOC platform
frontend_stack.rationale: No frontend specialist needed; SOC team uses existing tooling integrated via API

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI on AWS ECS Fargate; transformer inference service using PyTorch on GPU-enabled ECS instances
backend_stack.rationale: FastAPI for low-latency alert API; PyTorch for transformer model serving

data_platform.value: TimescaleDB on RDS for access event time-series; Redis 7 for real-time alert state and deduplication
data_platform.rationale: TimescaleDB provides efficient time-based aggregations over access event streams

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate with dedicated GPU task for inference; all within corporate AWS account with no public internet egress
hosting_target.rationale: Managed containers on existing AWS infrastructure; GPU task for transformer inference

privacy_retention_policy.value: Access events retained 90 days; incident records and model labels retained 3 years; GDPR right-to-erasure for identified individuals within 30 days
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR and ISO 27001 physical security record retention requirements

future_scope.value: Video frame anomaly scoring from camera feeds, cross-building threat correlation, SIEM integration for cyber-physical threat fusion
future_scope.rationale: Phase 3 capability after behavioral model achieves acceptable precision-recall on access events

constraints.value: Inference latency must be under 500ms from event ingestion to alert publication; no behavioral data may leave AWS us-east-1 region; GPU instance budget under $800/month
constraints.rationale: Security SLA requires near-real-time alerting; data residency and cost constraints

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM on all ECS tasks, structured JSON logging, custom metrics for inference latency and alert throughput, PagerDuty escalation for system failures
observability_baseline.rationale: Corporate monitoring standard; inference latency metric is critical for SLA compliance

execution_preference.value: GitLab CI with 2-week sprints; model updates deployed via blue-green ECS task replacement
execution_preference.rationale: Blue-green for model deployments enables instant rollback without re-training

compliance_context.value: SOC 2 Type II controls for security system; GDPR for behavioral and biometric data of EU employees; ISO 27001 physical security logging requirements
compliance_context.rationale: risk_level=high and data_sensitivity=confidential require formal compliance controls

llm_integration.value: GPT-4o via Azure OpenAI (EU endpoint) used to generate human-readable incident narrative summaries from structured anomaly event sequences for SOC analyst review; no raw behavioral data sent to LLM -- only anonymized event metadata
llm_integration.rationale: Capabilities include ai_llm; LLM reduces analyst cognitive load by summarizing complex multi-event patterns

project.goal: Real-time AI intrusion detection over multi-sensor building security event streams

backend.framework: FastAPI with async handlers; PyTorch transformer inference
backend.api: REST for alert queue and incident management; WebSocket for real-time alert push to SOC console

security.auth: Keycloak OIDC with MFA; service-to-service mTLS within ECS
security.encryption: TLS 1.3; TimescaleDB RDS with KMS encryption at rest

data.db: TimescaleDB on RDS for event time-series
data.cache: Redis 7 for alert state and deduplication

devops.hosting: AWS ECS Fargate; GPU task for inference

reasoner_reviews.product: Three rounds of revision have progressively hardened this architecture. The LLM incident narrative feature is a strong differentiator for analyst efficiency but requires careful data minimization -- only anonymized event metadata should reach the LLM, not raw behavioral profiles. The human label feedback loop is essential for model quality improvement over time.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Round 2 established the transformer inference pipeline and blue-green deployment strategy. Round 3 should complete the LLM integration design with explicit data minimization before the Azure OpenAI call, and harden the weekly fine-tuning cycle to include model regression testing before promotion. The 500ms end-to-end latency budget is tight with LLM narrative generation in the critical path -- narrative generation should be asynchronous, not blocking the alert publication.
reasoner_reviews.security: Rounds 1 and 2 addressed Keycloak RBAC and mTLS between services. Round 3 must finalize the GDPR right-to-erasure cascade from access_events to incident_records and model labels -- deletion of a former employee's access history must not corrupt the training dataset. Pseudonymization of person_id before model training is the correct approach.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: GPU budget under $800/month is achievable with a single g4dn.xlarge ECS instance running the PyTorch transformer. LLM costs at estimated 200 incidents/day at 500 tokens each are under $30/month via Azure OpenAI GPT-4o. Latency budget allows async narrative generation -- the 500ms SLA covers event-to-alert, not alert-to-narrative.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The model fine-tuning cycle still lacks a regression gate: a newly fine-tuned model must demonstrate recall on a held-out labeled set before blue-green promotion to production. Without this gate, a labeling bias from a single week could degrade detection quality.

backend.service_design: Four ECS services: event-ingestion (FastAPI, receives sensor events via HTTP POST and WebSocket from sensor gateways), inference-service (FastAPI + PyTorch transformer, GPU task, scores event sequences for anomaly probability), alert-service (FastAPI, manages alert lifecycle, WebSocket push to SOC console), narrative-service (FastAPI, async LLM narrative generation from anonymized event sequences via Azure OpenAI).
backend.api_patterns: POST /events/batch (sensor gateway event ingestion), WebSocket /ws/alerts (real-time alert push to SOC console), GET /alerts?status=pending (analyst alert queue), PATCH /alerts/:id/review (analyst label submission), GET /incidents/:id/narrative (async narrative fetch -- returns 202 Accepted then 200 when ready).
backend.llm_integration_design: Narrative-service extracts anonymized event sequence from incident_records (replacing person_id with pseudonym token). Constructs structured prompt: event timeline with zone, action type, anomaly score. Calls Azure OpenAI GPT-4o EU endpoint via HTTPS. Response stored in incident_narratives table. No raw access event data, camera IDs, or person names sent to LLM. Rate-limited to 10 concurrent narrative requests.
backend.failure_handling: Inference-service circuit breaker: if GPU task is unavailable, event-ingestion routes to rule-based fallback scorer. Alert deduplication in Redis prevents duplicate alerts from sensor event redelivery. Narrative-service failure does not block alert publication -- narrative generation is fully async.

security.auth_design: Keycloak OIDC with realm for building-security domain. Roles: security-analyst (alert queue read, label submit), security-manager (full incident read, narrative access), model-reviewer (label set management, model metrics), system-admin (Keycloak management). MFA enforced for all roles. Service-to-service mTLS using Keycloak service accounts.
security.authorization_model: FastAPI dependency-injected permission checks using decoded Keycloak JWT role claims. Inference-service and narrative-service not directly accessible from SOC console -- internal VPC only. Alert-service enforces analyst can only update alerts assigned to their zone group.
security.privacy_controls: GDPR pseudonymization: person_id replaced with stable pseudonym token (HMAC of person_id with rotation key) before sending to inference model and LLM. Right-to-erasure: DELETE /people/:id/data endpoint triggers erasure job deleting access_events by person_id, replacing person_id in incident_records with DELETED token, and re-pseudonymizing model training labels.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: All analyst actions (alert review, label submit, incident access) written to audit_actions TimescaleDB table. Alert publications logged with inference model version. Narrative generation requests logged with pseudonymized event count. CloudTrail for AWS API actions.

data.entities: building_zones (zone_id UUID, building_id, zone_name, floor, risk_level ENUM low/medium/high), access_events (ts TIMESTAMPTZ, event_id UUID, zone_id FK, person_id UUID, access_type ENUM badge/keypad/forced/tailgate, outcome ENUM granted/denied/alarm, device_id) TimescaleDB hypertable, camera_feeds (feed_id UUID, zone_id FK, camera_ip, is_active, retention_days), sensor_triggers (ts TIMESTAMPTZ, sensor_id UUID, zone_id FK, trigger_type ENUM door_open/motion/glass_break, severity ENUM low/medium/high), threat_incidents (incident_id UUID, zone_id FK, started_at TIMESTAMPTZ, resolved_at, severity ENUM warning/critical/false_positive, anomaly_score FLOAT, model_version, analyst_id), personnel_profiles (person_id UUID, pseudonym_token TEXT, access_level ENUM visitor/staff/contractor/executive, is_active BOOL), alert_dispatches (alert_id UUID, incident_id FK, dispatched_at, ack_by, ack_at, channel ENUM console/sms/email), incident_narratives (narrative_id UUID, incident_id FK, generated_at, narrative_text, llm_model_version).
data.storage_design: TimescaleDB hypertables on access_events and sensor_triggers partitioned by week. Compression policy on access_events older than 7 days. Redis sorted set for real-time active alert queue keyed by severity score. Separate TimescaleDB retention policy: access_events 90-day TTL, incident records 3-year retention.
data.retention_and_deletion: TimescaleDB TTL policy drops access_events chunks older than 90 days. GDPR erasure job: DELETE from access_events WHERE person_id=$1; UPDATE incident_records SET person_id='DELETED' WHERE person_id=$1; UPDATE model_training_labels SET pseudonym='DELETED' WHERE pseudonym=old_pseudonym.

devops.deployment_topology: ECS cluster with three CPU task services (event-ingestion, alert-service, narrative-service) and one GPU task (inference-service on g4dn.xlarge). ALB routes public-facing (within VPN) endpoints to alert-service. Internal service mesh via VPC private DNS. Inference-service behind internal NLB for sub-millisecond routing from event-ingestion.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI: mypy + pytest on MR; Docker build and push to ECR; staging deploy via ECS rolling update; production deploy via blue-green task replacement with automated smoke test (POST /health, POST /events/test checking end-to-end latency <500ms). Model fine-tuning job triggered by GitLab scheduled pipeline weekly; model promoted to production only after passing regression test suite on held-out label set.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM sidecar on all ECS tasks. Custom metrics: inference_latency_p99_ms, alert_queue_depth, narrative_generation_latency_p95_ms, model_precision_at_threshold, false_positive_rate_7d. PagerDuty alert: inference latency >500ms for 3 consecutive minutes triggers P1; alert queue not draining (depth rising for 10 min) triggers P2.

ISS-001.title: LLM data minimization not specified
ISS-001.detail: Rounds 1 and 2 sent full access event records to LLM without specifying anonymization. GDPR biometric data may not be sent to third-party LLM without explicit DPA.

history.note: Partially addressed but pseudonymization before LLM call still not explicit

ISS-002.title: Model fine-tuning lacks regression gate
ISS-002.detail: Weekly fine-tuning cycle promotes model to production without validation on held-out label set. Labeling bias in a single week could degrade detection recall.

ISS-003.title: GDPR erasure cascade to model labels not designed
ISS-003.detail: Right-to-erasure for a former employee must cascade from access_events to model training labels without corrupting the dataset.

focus_issues.title: Model fine-tuning lacks regression gate
focus_issues.detail: Weekly fine-tuning cycle promotes model without validation on held-out label set.

focus_issues.title: GDPR erasure cascade to model labels not designed
focus_issues.detail: Erasure must cascade from access_events to model training labels without corrupting dataset.

latest_recommendations: Add regression test gate to model fine-tuning pipeline before blue-green promotion
Design GDPR erasure cascade: pseudonymize person_id in training labels before erasure, re-hash with new salt to sever linkage
Document LLM data minimization as complete given pseudonymization design in round 2 plan
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: Smart Building AI Intrusion Detection System v2

convergence_state.reason: ISS-002 high-severity model regression gate still unresolved; ISS-003 GDPR erasure cascade not yet implemented

previous_audits.summary: Good multi-sensor ingestion architecture but critical gaps in LLM data minimization and GDPR erasure design for biometric behavioral data.
strengths: FastAPI async design appropriate for real-time alert path
TimescaleDB hypertable partitioning well-matched to access event time-series
concerns: LLM receives raw person_id and access event details -- GDPR violation risk
