target_output.thinking_summary: This is round 3 of an AI system plan for acoustic fault detection on power grid infrastructure. Two issues remain: the model promotion gate was described but lacked numeric precision thresholds per fault class, and the 72-hour audio deletion CronJob was never specified. Both are resolved: promotion gate now specifies arcing >0.92, insulator_crack >0.88, conductor_galloping >0.85 precision thresholds with automated evaluation; audio deletion CronJob runs nightly at 01:00 UTC with PagerDuty alert on failure. Plan quality is strong given the infrastructure complexity and NERC CIP constraints.

fix_report.action_taken: Added explicit precision thresholds to model promotion gate: arcing >0.92, insulator_crack >0.88, conductor_galloping >0.85. Fine-tuning CronJob now includes evaluation script that checks per-class precision against these thresholds before updating model_version ConfigMap in production namespace via ArgoCD image update PR.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Model promotion is now gated on objective per-class performance metrics, preventing under-performing model versions from reaching production sensors.

fix_report.action_taken: Defined audio deletion AKS CronJob: schedule 0 1 * * * UTC, executes az storage blob delete-batch against the raw-audio Azure Blob container with --if-unmodified-since 72h, logs deletion count to ClickHouse audio_deletion_log, PagerDuty alert if job exits non-zero.
fix_report.expected_outcome: 72-hour audio retention policy is automated and monitored, satisfying NERC CIP-011 information protection requirements for raw sensor data.

target_output.title: Power Line Acoustic Fault Detection System - NERC CIP Edition
target_output.executive_summary: A high-scale real-time AI system detecting arcing faults, insulator cracking, and conductor galloping on high-voltage transmission lines by processing 48 kHz acoustic emission audio from up to 2,000 concurrent sensors. Built with Python 3.12 microservices on AKS in Azure Government Cloud, using ONNX Runtime GPU inference on 500ms spectral windows via Kafka, ClickHouse for fault analytics, and ArgoCD GitOps for compliant deployments. Alert dispatch within 45 seconds of sensor event. NERC CIP-007/009/011 compliant with 7-year audit trail.
target_output.architecture_overview: Event-driven ML inference pipeline with four Python services: audio-ingestor consuming MQTT from sensor VLAN and producing to Kafka audio.frames topic; feature-extractor consuming audio.frames, running librosa mel-spectrogram extraction on 500ms windows with range partition assignment ensuring per-sensor frame ordering, publishing to audio.features topic; inference-engine consuming audio.features, batching 64 windows per ONNX Runtime TensorRT call on A100 GPU nodes, publishing FaultDetection events to audio.detections topic; alert-dispatcher consuming audio.detections, checking Redis alert dedup window, dispatching PagerDuty and ClickHouse writes. Model promotion via weekly fine-tuning CronJob with per-class precision gates: arcing >0.92, insulator_crack >0.88, conductor_galloping >0.85. Audio deleted by nightly CronJob at 72-hour TTL. AKS in Azure Government Cloud with ArgoCD GitOps for NERC CIP change auditability.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, confluent-kafka-python, librosa for spectral feature extraction, ONNX Runtime 1.17 with TensorRT execution provider. Inference hardware: Azure Standard_NC24ads_A100_v4 GPU nodes. Data: ClickHouse MergeTree for fault analytics; Redis 7 for live sensor state and alert dedup. Queue: Azure Event Hubs Kafka-compatible endpoint (3 brokers, 32 partitions on audio.frames and audio.features topics). Storage: Azure Blob Storage for raw audio with lifecycle policy; Azure Container Registry for Docker images. Infrastructure: AKS with node auto-provisioner, ArgoCD for GitOps, Azure DevOps for CI. Monitoring: Prometheus + Grafana on AKS, PagerDuty for SLA alerting.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Audio Ingestion: audio-ingestor validates sensor mTLS client certificate, deserializes 48 kHz PCM frames from MQTT, publishes to Kafka audio.frames with sensor_id partition key. Spectral Feature Extraction: feature-extractor computes 128-bin mel-spectrogram over 500ms Hanning-windowed frame (24,000 samples at 48 kHz), normalizes to dB scale, serializes as float32 array to audio.features. Fault Classification: inference-engine batches 64 feature vectors per GPU call, runs ONNX transformer encoder forward pass, returns 4-class softmax scores (arcing, insulator_crack, conductor_galloping, normal), publishes FaultDetection with sensor_id, fault_class, confidence, model_version, window_start. Alert Dispatch: alert-dispatcher checks Redis sorted set for duplicate alerts from same sensor within 5-minute dedup window; on new high-confidence event dispatches PagerDuty incident with GPS coordinates and line segment name; writes to ClickHouse fault_detections. Operator Annotation: REST API allows grid_operators to annotate fault_detections as confirmed/false_positive/upgraded, writing to event_classifications and queueing sample for model retraining.
target_output.system_components: AKS node pools: system (3 x Standard_D4s_v3), cpu-workers (4-16 x Standard_D8s_v3 for ingestors and feature extractors, auto-scale on Kafka lag), gpu-workers (2-8 x Standard_NC24ads_A100_v4 for inference engine, auto-scale on features topic lag > 500). Kafka: Azure Event Hubs Kafka-compatible, 32 partitions on audio.frames with range assignment per inference pod. ClickHouse: 3-node cluster on cpu-workers, fault_detections and event_classifications MergeTree tables partitioned by month, inference_audit retention 1 year, operator_audit retention 7 years. Redis 7: Azure Cache for Redis, sensor state HASHes and alert dedup sorted sets. Azure Blob Storage: raw-audio container with 72-hour lifecycle policy, model-artifacts container private to AKS managed identity. Model promotion CronJob: weekly Sunday 03:00 UTC, evaluates fine-tuned ONNX model against 5,000-sample held-out test set, promotes if arcing precision >0.92 AND insulator_crack precision >0.88 AND conductor_galloping precision >0.85. Audio deletion CronJob: nightly 01:00 UTC, az storage blob delete-batch --if-unmodified-since 72h.
target_output.workflows: Fault Detection Path: sensor transmits 48 kHz PCM audio via MQTT over TLS 1.3; audio-ingestor validates mTLS client cert, publishes frame to Kafka audio.frames with sensor_id as partition key; feature-extractor pod owning that partition key range consumes frame, computes mel-spectrogram, publishes to audio.features; inference-engine batches 64 features, runs ONNX GPU inference in ~2ms, publishes FaultDetection with confidence score; alert-dispatcher checks Redis dedup window, on novel arcing detection confidence >0.90 dispatches PagerDuty with sensor GPS and line segment ID within 45 seconds of sensor event. Model Promotion Path: AKS CronJob weekly-fine-tune pulls annotated samples from ClickHouse training_samples where event_classifications.operator_annotation='confirmed' since last training run; runs Python fine-tuning script on GPU node, evaluates per-class precision on held-out set; if all three fault class thresholds pass, uploads ONNX artifact to Blob Storage production path and creates ArgoCD image update PR updating model_version ConfigMap; human reviewer merges PR to trigger ArgoCD sync rolling inference pods to new model.
target_output.data_model: sensor_units (id UUID PK, line_segment_id UUID FK, gps_lat DOUBLE, gps_lng DOUBLE, install_date DATE, status ENUM active/offline/maintenance, mTLS_thumbprint TEXT). power_line_segments (id UUID PK, name TEXT, voltage_kv INT, start_substation TEXT, end_substation TEXT, length_km FLOAT, geojson_path TEXT). audio_streams (id UUID PK, sensor_id UUID FK, stream_start TIMESTAMPTZ, blob_path TEXT, sample_rate_hz INT DEFAULT 48000, duration_ms INT, deleted_at TIMESTAMPTZ nullable - ClickHouse). fault_detections (id UUID PK, sensor_id UUID FK, line_segment_id UUID FK, window_start TIMESTAMPTZ, fault_class ENUM arcing/insulator_crack/conductor_galloping/normal, confidence FLOAT, model_version TEXT, alert_dispatched BOOL, pagerduty_incident_id TEXT nullable, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ - ClickHouse MergeTree partitioned by toYYYYMM(created_at)). event_classifications (id UUID PK, fault_detection_id UUID FK, operator_annotation ENUM confirmed/false_positive/upgraded, annotated_by TEXT, annotated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). maintenance_dispatches (id UUID PK, fault_detection_id UUID FK, crew_id TEXT, dispatched_at TIMESTAMPTZ, resolved_at TIMESTAMPTZ nullable).
target_output.api_design: FastAPI REST /api/v1. GET /v1/fault-detections?segment_id=&start=&end=&fault_class=&min_confidence= (grid_operator and above). PATCH /v1/fault-detections/:id/annotate with body {operator_annotation, notes} (grid_operator, writes event_classifications). GET /v1/segments/:id/fault-summary?period=week (analytics summary per line segment). GET /v1/model/status (current model version, last promotion date, precision metrics per fault class). POST /v1/alerts/test (maintenance_dispatcher test-fires a synthetic alert for drill purposes). All endpoints: Azure AD JWT Bearer, RBAC enforced at route level, audit log written on every mutating request.
target_output.security_and_compliance: NERC CIP-007: all sensor-to-ingestor MQTT connections require mTLS with client certificates issued by internal CA; certificate revocation checked via OCSP on every connection. NERC CIP-011: raw audio classified as Protected Cyber Asset Information; Azure Blob raw-audio container restricted to AKS managed identity with no internet egress; ClickHouse data encrypted at rest with Azure-managed keys. NERC CIP-009: fault_detections and operator_audit retained 7 years with ClickHouse TTL policy; Azure Monitor captures all AKS control plane events for 7-year Log Analytics retention. SOC 2 Type II: every inference result written to inference_audit with model_version, confidence, and sensor_id; operator actions written to operator_audit. Azure AD Conditional Access: MFA plus compliant device required for grid_operator and maintenance_dispatcher roles. Audio deletion CronJob log written to ClickHouse audio_deletion_log with deletion count and job exit code; PagerDuty alert on non-zero exit.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: AKS in Azure Government us-gov-virginia. ArgoCD GitOps syncing from infrastructure-repo/clusters/prod/. Azure DevOps pipeline: pytest --cov 80% gate, Docker build, push ACR, update image tag in Helm values file, ArgoCD auto-sync triggers rolling deploy. GPU node pool minimum 2 nodes always running; scale-up at Kafka audio.features lag > 500. Environments: dev (single AZ, Azure Event Hubs Basic, no GPU - CPU ONNX inference), staging (multi-AZ, GPU pool 1 node, production Kafka config), production (3 AZ, GPU pool 2-8 nodes, ClickHouse 3-node). ArgoCD rollback on inference error rate > 5% in first 10 minutes. Model promotion CronJob runs Sunday 03:00 UTC; creates PR to infrastructure-repo on threshold pass; human merge triggers ArgoCD sync. Audio deletion CronJob: schedule '0 1 * * *', image: azure-cli:latest, command: az storage blob delete-batch --source raw-audio --if-unmodified-since 72h; Datadog log monitor alerts PagerDuty on non-zero exit code.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics scraped by Grafana: kafka_consumer_lag per topic and consumer_group (PagerDuty at lag > 2000 on audio.features for > 2 minutes), inference_latency_ms histogram (p99 target <50ms per 64-window batch), fault_detection_rate per fault_class per hour, alert_dispatch_latency_seconds (PagerDuty at p95 > 45s). ClickHouse analytics: weekly model performance report querying precision/recall per fault_class from event_classifications join fault_detections. Audio deletion CronJob writes deletion_count and job_duration to ClickHouse audio_deletion_log; Grafana panel shows 7-day deletion trend.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: ~$12,000/month at 2,000 concurrent streams: GPU nodes (2-8 A100) ~$5,000-8,000 variable, CPU nodes ~$2,000, ClickHouse cluster ~$1,200, Azure Event Hubs ~$600, Redis Cache ~$300, Blob Storage ~$200, Azure DevOps ~$100. Cost optimization: inference batching at 64 windows per GPU call reduces A100 utilization allowing 500 streams per GPU pod; auto-scaling keeps GPU pods at minimum 2 during off-peak hours.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (12 weeks): audio-ingestor and feature-extractor pipeline, arcing fault detection ONNX model, alert-dispatcher with PagerDuty, Redis alert dedup, ClickHouse fault_detections table, Azure AD RBAC, mTLS sensor authentication, NERC CIP audit logging, audio deletion CronJob, ArgoCD GitOps. Phase 2 (8 weeks): insulator_crack and conductor_galloping model classes, operator annotation API, model promotion CronJob with precision gates, model performance dashboard, maintenance dispatch tracking. Phase 3 (4 weeks): thermal imaging sensor fusion prototype, multi-week fault trend analytics, executive summary dashboard.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 strict type hints with mypy. pytest with 80% coverage gate; no skipped tests in CI. All Kafka consumer workers must log consumed offset on every batch commit for audit traceability. ONNX model artifacts must be versioned with a semantic version tag and SHA-256 checksum in the model registry manifest. ArgoCD ApplicationSets for per-environment configuration; no manual kubectl apply in production. ADR required for changes to Kafka partition count or ONNX inference batch size.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: GPU node scale-up latency (3-5 minutes) causes Kafka lag spike during sudden increase in active sensors. Mitigation: minimum 2 GPU nodes always running; KEDA Kafka scaler triggers at lag > 500 rather than waiting for CPU saturation. Risk: ONNX model accuracy drift as grid infrastructure ages and new fault signatures emerge. Mitigation: weekly precision monitoring in Grafana; model promotion gate enforces re-evaluation before any production rollout. Tradeoff: Azure Event Hubs Kafka-compatible endpoint over self-managed Kafka avoids AKS broker management at 15-20% higher per-message cost, acceptable given NERC CIP audit requirements for managed service SLAs.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed ONNX Runtime over TorchServe to satisfy NERC CIP requirement for air-gapped model inference without internet-accessible model repository. Selected range partition assignment over round-robin to maintain per-sensor audio frame ordering within 500ms windows. ClickHouse chosen over TimescaleDB for fault analytics because columnar storage handles the high-cardinality sensor_id GROUP BY queries 10x faster at billion-row scale.

metadata.notes: Round 3 strong-quality AI system for acoustic fault detection on power grid; resolved model promotion gate thresholds and audio deletion CronJob.

project_goal.value: Python SDK providing a unified API-first abstraction for provisioning, configuring, and monitoring LoRaWAN network servers, supporting The Things Stack and ChirpStack backends with a common interface for device registration and downlink scheduling
project_goal.rationale: IoT operators managing multi-vendor LoRaWAN deployments need a single programming interface instead of two incompatible REST and gRPC APIs

target_users.value: IoT platform engineers and system integrators deploying LoRaWAN networks for industrial and agricultural monitoring customers, using Python automation scripts and orchestration tools
target_users.rationale: Primary audience from LoRaWAN integration community using Python for network automation

project_class.rationale: Published as a PyPI package; no hosted service

capabilities.rationale: SDK integrates backend APIs of two LoRaWAN network server vendors and normalizes device and downlink data structures

complexity_level.rationale: Two vendor adapters with different transports (REST vs gRPC) and auth models require careful abstraction design

risk_level.rationale: Library with no hosted infrastructure; risk is limited to API contract breakage from vendor updates

data_sensitivity.rationale: DevEUI, AppKey, and device profiles are operationally internal data for IoT deployments

external_exposure.rationale: SDK is used by partner system integrators building LoRaWAN network automation on behalf of utility and agricultural customers

access_model.value: Published on PyPI under Apache 2.0 license; callers authenticate to their own network server instances using API key or OAuth2 credentials passed at SDK instantiation
access_model.rationale: Open-source distribution model; network server credentials are caller-owned

feature_scope.value: NetworkServerClient interface, TheThingsStackAdapter (REST), ChirpStackAdapter (gRPC), device registration with DevEUI/AppKey/device_profile, downlink scheduling with confirmed/unconfirmed delivery and ACK tracking, uplink frame subscription via async generator, device status and session key queries
feature_scope.rationale: Full SDK feature set for LoRaWAN network operations automation

mvp_scope.value: NetworkServerClient interface, TheThingsStackAdapter with device registration and downlink scheduling, DeviceRecord normalized type, 90% test coverage with mocked HTTP
mvp_scope.rationale: The Things Stack covers 70% of partner deployments; ChirpStack adapter in v1.1

security_baseline.value: API keys and OAuth2 tokens never logged or exposed in exceptions; HTTPS enforced for all vendor API calls; AppKey material redacted from DeviceRecord __repr__
security_baseline.rationale: LoRaWAN AppKeys are root session keys; exposure compromises device security

frontend_stack.value: none
frontend_stack.rationale: Library SDK with no frontend

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with httpx async client for TheThingsStack REST; grpcio and betterproto for ChirpStack gRPC; pydantic v2 for response validation
backend_stack.rationale: httpx provides async/sync duality; betterproto provides idiomatic Python gRPC dataclasses; pydantic v2 for fast response parsing

data_platform.value: none - SDK is stateless; callers manage device record persistence
data_platform.rationale: SDK normalizes data in memory and returns typed objects to callers

hosting_target.value: PyPI registry as lorawan-sdk; GitHub Packages as secondary distribution
hosting_target.rationale: Standard Python package distribution

privacy_retention_policy.value: SDK stores no data; AppKey material is accepted as input but never persisted or logged; security guide advises callers to store AppKeys in hardware security modules
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: LoRaWAN root keys require secure storage; SDK documents this responsibility to callers

future_scope.value: Actility ThingPark adapter, batch device provisioning from CSV, network server health monitoring polling, Terraform provider wrapping the SDK
future_scope.rationale: Post-v1.0 ecosystem expansion for enterprise partner use cases

constraints.value: Supports Python 3.10+; grpcio dependency optional (only installed with extras_require ChirpStack); no mandatory dependencies beyond httpx and pydantic; async-first API with sync wrappers via asyncio.run
constraints.rationale: Partner automation environments vary; optional gRPC dependency avoids forcing grpcio compilation on TTS-only users

observability_baseline.value: Codecov with 90% gate; GitHub Actions CI on Python 3.10, 3.11, 3.12; respx-based HTTP mock tests for TTS adapter; grpc-testing mock for ChirpStack adapter
observability_baseline.rationale: High coverage required for SDK handling device credentials and downlink scheduling

execution_preference.value: Semantic versioning with changelog; PR review from 2 maintainers; breaking changes in major versions with migration guide
execution_preference.rationale: Partner integrators depend on stable API contracts across vendor backend updates

project.goal: Unified Python SDK for LoRaWAN network server automation across TTS and ChirpStack
project.target_users: IoT platform engineers and system integrators
project.timeline: v1.0 TTS adapter in 8 weeks, ChirpStack adapter v1.1 in 4 more weeks

security.credential_handling: API keys and OAuth tokens never logged; AppKey redacted in __repr__

data.vendor_apis: TTS REST API v3, ChirpStack gRPC API v4

devops.distribution: PyPI with Trusted Publishing via OIDC

reasoner_reviews.product: The unified NetworkServerClient interface is the primary value. Partner integrators write device provisioning scripts once and run against any supported backend. Downlink ACK tracking is critical for confirmed downlinks since LoRaWAN confirmed delivery requires the device to acknowledge receipt within a Class A receive window.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The async generator approach for uplink frame subscription is correct: TTS provides a Server-Sent Events stream and ChirpStack uses gRPC server streaming, both of which map naturally to AsyncIterator. The extras_require pattern for grpcio keeps installation lightweight for TTS-only users. Pydantic v2 model_validator can normalize vendor-specific field names to DeviceRecord at parse time.
reasoner_reviews.security: AppKey is a 128-bit root key equivalent to a device master secret. It must be excluded from logs, error messages, and __repr__. The SDK security guide must advise callers to use AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, or hardware HSMs for AppKey storage. API key rotation should be supported without re-instantiating the client.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Round 1 plan defined the NetworkServerClient interface and TTS adapter but left ChirpStack gRPC transport design underspecified: the betterproto codegen process, the proto file versioning strategy, and how gRPC channel lifecycle is managed across concurrent calls were all absent.

backend.service_design: Package lorawan_sdk with modules: client.py (NetworkServerClient abstract base), adapters/tts.py (TheThingsStackAdapter using httpx), adapters/chirpstack.py (ChirpStackAdapter using grpcio channel, optional import), models.py (pydantic v2 DeviceRecord, DownlinkFrame, UplinkFrame, DeviceProfile), errors.py (LoraSdkError hierarchy).
backend.api_patterns: NetworkServerClient interface: async register_device(dev_eui, app_key, device_profile_id, application_id) -> DeviceRecord. async schedule_downlink(dev_eui, payload_hex, confirmed, port) -> DownlinkFrame. async get_device(dev_eui) -> DeviceRecord. async subscribe_uplinks(dev_eui) -> AsyncIterator[UplinkFrame]. TTS adapter uses httpx.AsyncClient; ChirpStack adapter uses grpc.aio.insecure_channel or secure_channel based on server_tls flag.
backend.failure_handling: DeviceNotFoundError, DeviceAlreadyExistsError, DownlinkQueueFullError, NetworkServerApiError base class. Transient HTTP 5xx and gRPC UNAVAILABLE errors retried up to 3 times with exponential backoff via tenacity. API key expiry raises CredentialExpiredError to prompt caller to rotate.
backend.backend_risks: TTS REST API v3 field names differ from ChirpStack gRPC proto field names; normalization must be tested with actual vendor response fixtures. Betterproto-generated classes may lag behind ChirpStack proto updates; mitigation: pin betterproto codegen to a tested ChirpStack API version.

data.entities: DeviceRecord (dev_eui STRING, application_id STRING, device_profile_id STRING, name STRING, description STRING optional, created_at datetime, last_seen_at datetime optional, session_keys_available BOOL - AppKey excluded from representation). DownlinkFrame (id STRING, dev_eui STRING, port INT, payload_hex STRING, confirmed BOOL, ack_received BOOL, scheduled_at datetime, transmitted_at datetime optional). UplinkFrame (dev_eui STRING, port INT, payload_hex STRING, rssi_dbm FLOAT, snr_db FLOAT, frequency_mhz FLOAT, received_at datetime, fcnt INT). DeviceProfile (id STRING, name STRING, mac_version STRING, regional_params STRING, adr_algorithm STRING, supports_class_b BOOL, supports_class_c BOOL).
data.schema_notes: Pydantic v2 model_validator(mode='before') in each adapter-specific model converts vendor field names to normalized SDK names before Pydantic validation. TTS uses dev_eui as a string with dashes; ChirpStack uses devEui without dashes; normalization strips dashes and upper-cases.
data.data_risks: ChirpStack gRPC proto field naming uses camelCase while pydantic prefers snake_case; betterproto generates snake_case Python classes automatically, but any handwritten normalization must be tested against real proto stubs.

devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: matrix [python-3.10, 3.11, 3.12]. Jobs: lint (ruff + mypy strict), test-tts (pytest with respx HTTP mock, Codecov upload), test-chirpstack (pytest with grpc-testing mock, requires extras install), test-integration (scheduled weekly against TTS sandbox). PyPI Trusted Publishing via OIDC on version tag push, no API key stored in secrets.
devops.observability_stack: Codecov with 90% gate and branch coverage. mypy strict mode in CI gates type safety. Weekly integration tests against TTS Community Server sandbox to detect API drift.
devops.devops_risks: TTS Community Server sandbox rate limits may cause flaky integration tests. Mitigation: integration tests use a dedicated application with a small device quota; flaky tests retried once before failing CI.

ISS-001.title: ChirpStack gRPC channel lifecycle not specified
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan defined ChirpStackAdapter using betterproto but did not specify how the gRPC channel is created, how TLS is configured, how the channel is shared across concurrent async calls, or when it is closed.

ISS-002.title: Betterproto codegen process and proto version pinning absent
ISS-002.detail: The round 1 plan mentioned betterproto for gRPC bindings but did not describe how proto files are vendored, which ChirpStack API version is targeted, or how the codegen step is automated in the build pipeline.

focus_issues.title: ChirpStack gRPC channel lifecycle not specified
focus_issues.detail: gRPC channel creation, TLS config, concurrency, and cleanup not defined.

focus_issues.title: Betterproto codegen process and proto version pinning absent
focus_issues.detail: Proto file vendoring, ChirpStack API version target, and codegen automation not specified.

latest_recommendations: Specify gRPC channel creation using grpc.aio.secure_channel with server TLS; share single channel per adapter instance using asyncio.Lock for thread safety; close channel in async context manager __aexit__
Document proto vendoring: pin chirpstack-api v4 proto files in lorawan_sdk/adapters/proto/; run betterproto codegen in Makefile target generate-proto; commit generated files to repo
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: lorawan-sdk: LoRaWAN Network Server Python SDK v1

convergence_state.reason: One high and one medium issue unresolved after round 1

previous_audits.summary: TTS adapter design is well-specified. ChirpStack gRPC adapter is conceptually correct but lacks the operational detail needed for implementation: channel lifecycle and proto codegen automation are absent.
strengths: NetworkServerClient abstract interface cleanly separates TTS and ChirpStack concerns
pydantic v2 normalization approach for dev_eui field name differences is correct
respx mock test pattern for TTS adapter is well-described
concerns: No specification of how grpc.aio.Channel is initialized, whether TLS is used, or how it handles concurrent async calls
betterproto codegen mentioned but proto file sourcing and version pinning not described
blocking_issues: ChirpStack adapter gRPC channel design is underdefined for implementation
recommendations: Add gRPC channel spec: grpc.aio.secure_channel with root_certificates from cert bundle; single channel per adapter instance; use async context manager for channel lifecycle; document channel reuse across concurrent calls

Add proto codegen spec: vendor chirpstack-api v4 proto files in repo under lorawan_sdk/adapters/proto/; Makefile generate-proto target runs python -m grpc_tools.protoc piped through betterproto plugin; CI lint step verifies generated files match committed files

issue_updates.title: ChirpStack gRPC channel lifecycle not specified
issue_updates.detail: Channel initialization, TLS, concurrency, and cleanup not defined

issue_updates.title: Betterproto codegen process and proto version pinning absent
issue_updates.detail: Proto file sourcing and codegen automation not described

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 of the LoRaWAN SDK plan. Two issues from round 1 remain: the ChirpStack gRPC channel lifecycle was undefined (ISS-001, high), and the betterproto codegen process and proto version pinning were absent (ISS-002, medium). Both are resolved: ChirpStack adapter now uses grpc.aio.secure_channel as an async context manager per adapter instance with a single channel reused across concurrent calls; and proto files are vendored in lorawan_sdk/adapters/proto/ from chirpstack-api v4.8 with a Makefile generate-proto target and CI check verifying generated files match committed files.

fix_report.action_taken: Specified ChirpStack gRPC channel lifecycle: ChirpStackAdapter creates a grpc.aio.secure_channel in __aenter__ using ssl_credentials from the caller-provided CA bundle or system roots; channel is stored as self._channel and reused across all concurrent method calls within an async with block; channel is closed in __aexit__ via await self._channel.close(); all adapter methods create per-call stubs from self._channel rather than new channels.
fix_report.expected_outcome: ChirpStack adapter gRPC channel is unambiguously defined for implementation with correct TLS, concurrency, and lifecycle semantics.

fix_report.action_taken: Documented proto vendoring: chirpstack-api v4.8 proto files copied to lorawan_sdk/adapters/proto/ from github.com/chirpstack/chirpstack/api/proto; Makefile generate-proto target runs python -m grpc_tools.protoc with betterproto plugin, outputting to lorawan_sdk/adapters/_chirpstack_pb/; generated files committed to repo; CI lint job runs make generate-proto and git diff --exit-code to verify generated files match committed files, blocking PRs that upgrade proto version without regenerating.
fix_report.expected_outcome: ChirpStack gRPC bindings are reproducible, version-pinned, and CI-verified, preventing stale generated code from masking API changes.

target_output.title: lorawan-sdk: Unified LoRaWAN Network Server Python SDK
target_output.executive_summary: A Python 3.10+ SDK providing a unified NetworkServerClient interface for provisioning LoRaWAN devices, scheduling downlinks, and subscribing to uplink frames across The Things Stack v3 REST API and ChirpStack v4 gRPC API. Adapters normalize vendor-specific response shapes to shared pydantic v2 DeviceRecord, DownlinkFrame, and UplinkFrame types. The ChirpStack adapter uses grpc.aio.secure_channel as an async context manager with TLS and reuses a single channel per adapter instance. Proto files are vendored at chirpstack-api v4.8 with CI-verified codegen. Distributed via PyPI with grpcio as an optional extras_require dependency.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single-package library lorawan_sdk with three layers: NetworkServerClient abstract base class defining the async interface contract; vendor adapters TheThingsStackAdapter (httpx async REST client against TTS API v3) and ChirpStackAdapter (grpc.aio.secure_channel against ChirpStack gRPC API v4, betterproto-generated stubs); and pydantic v2 normalization models that convert vendor response shapes to SDK types at parse time. Both adapters implement async context manager protocol for connection lifecycle. Optional dependency: grpcio and betterproto installed via pip install lorawan-sdk[chirpstack]. Uplink subscription returns AsyncIterator[UplinkFrame] mapping TTS SSE stream and ChirpStack gRPC server streaming to a common async generator.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: Python 3.12. HTTP client: httpx 0.27 async. gRPC: grpcio 1.64 with grpc.aio, betterproto 2.0 for proto dataclasses (optional extras). Validation: pydantic v2 with model_validator for vendor normalization. Retry logic: tenacity for exponential backoff on transient errors. Proto source: chirpstack-api v4.8 vendored in lorawan_sdk/adapters/proto/. Testing: pytest-asyncio, respx for TTS HTTP mocking, grpc-testing for ChirpStack. Distribution: PyPI with Trusted Publishing via GitHub Actions OIDC. CI: GitHub Actions matrix on Python 3.10/3.11/3.12. Coverage: Codecov 90% gate.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Device Registration: register_device(dev_eui, app_key, device_profile_id, application_id) creates a LoRaWAN device on the configured backend; TTS adapter calls POST /api/v3/applications/{app_id}/devices; ChirpStack adapter calls DeviceService.Create and DeviceService.CreateKeys gRPC methods; both return DeviceRecord with AppKey excluded from repr. Downlink Scheduling: schedule_downlink(dev_eui, payload_hex, confirmed, port) enqueues a downlink frame; TTS uses POST /api/v3/as/applications/{app_id}/devices/{dev_eui}/down/push; ChirpStack uses DeviceService.Enqueue; returns DownlinkFrame with id and scheduled_at. Uplink Subscription: subscribe_uplinks(dev_eui) returns AsyncIterator[UplinkFrame]; TTS subscribes to GET /api/v3/events SSE stream filtered by dev_eui; ChirpStack calls DeviceService.StreamEventLogs gRPC server streaming. Device Query: get_device(dev_eui) retrieves current DeviceRecord including session_keys_available flag.
target_output.system_components: lorawan_sdk/client.py: NetworkServerClient ABC with abstract async methods register_device, schedule_downlink, get_device, subscribe_uplinks, and async context manager protocol __aenter__/__aexit__. lorawan_sdk/adapters/tts.py: TheThingsStackAdapter using httpx.AsyncClient initialized in __aenter__, closed in __aexit__; API key passed as Authorization header. lorawan_sdk/adapters/chirpstack.py: ChirpStackAdapter using grpc.aio.secure_channel created in __aenter__ with ssl_credentials=grpc.ssl_channel_credentials(root_certificates=ca_bundle); single channel stored as self._channel; DeviceServiceStub instantiated per method call from self._channel; channel closed in __aexit__. lorawan_sdk/adapters/_chirpstack_pb/: betterproto-generated Python dataclasses from chirpstack-api v4.8 proto files. lorawan_sdk/models.py: pydantic v2 DeviceRecord, DownlinkFrame, UplinkFrame, DeviceProfile with @model_validator(mode='before') normalizing TTS and ChirpStack field names. lorawan_sdk/errors.py: LoraSdkError, DeviceNotFoundError, DeviceAlreadyExistsError, DownlinkQueueFullError, NetworkServerApiError, CredentialExpiredError.
target_output.workflows: TTS Device Registration: async with TheThingsStackAdapter(server_url, api_key) as client: DeviceRecord = await client.register_device(dev_eui, app_key, profile_id, app_id); adapter calls httpx POST /api/v3/applications/{app_id}/devices with JSON body; on HTTP 409 raises DeviceAlreadyExistsError; on HTTP 401 raises CredentialExpiredError; response parsed through pydantic v2 TTSDeviceResponse model_validator normalizing ids.device_id to dev_eui. ChirpStack Downlink Scheduling: async with ChirpStackAdapter(host, port, ca_bundle) as client; adapter creates grpc.aio.secure_channel in __aenter__; await client.schedule_downlink(dev_eui, '0102', confirmed=True, port=2); adapter instantiates DeviceServiceStub(self._channel), calls await stub.enqueue(DeviceQueueItem(dev_eui=dev_eui, confirmed=True, f_port=2, data=bytes.fromhex('0102'))); on gRPC NOT_FOUND raises DeviceNotFoundError; on gRPC RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED raises DownlinkQueueFullError.
target_output.data_model: DeviceRecord: dev_eui STRING (uppercase no dashes), application_id STRING, device_profile_id STRING, name STRING, description STRING optional, created_at datetime, last_seen_at datetime optional, session_keys_available BOOL, _app_key_present BOOL private (AppKey value never stored in model). DownlinkFrame: id STRING, dev_eui STRING, port INT 1-223, payload_hex STRING, confirmed BOOL, ack_received BOOL DEFAULT False, scheduled_at datetime, transmitted_at datetime optional. UplinkFrame: dev_eui STRING, port INT, payload_hex STRING, rssi_dbm FLOAT, snr_db FLOAT, frequency_mhz FLOAT, received_at datetime, fcnt INT, data_rate STRING (e.g. SF7BW125). DeviceProfile: id STRING, name STRING, mac_version STRING (1.0.3/1.1.0), regional_params STRING (EU868/US915), adr_algorithm STRING, supports_class_b BOOL, supports_class_c BOOL. ApplicationRecord: id STRING, name STRING, description STRING optional.
target_output.api_design: Python async API. from lorawan_sdk import TheThingsStackAdapter, ChirpStackAdapter. async with TheThingsStackAdapter(server_url='https://tts.example.com', api_key='NNSXS...') as client. record: DeviceRecord = await client.register_device(dev_eui='AABB...', app_key='0102...', device_profile_id='profile-1', application_id='app-1'). downlink: DownlinkFrame = await client.schedule_downlink(dev_eui='AABB...', payload_hex='0A0B', confirmed=True, port=10). async for uplink in client.subscribe_uplinks('AABB...'): process(uplink.payload_hex). Full type stubs in lorawan_sdk/py.typed marker. Documented in Sphinx with autodoc and deployed to Read the Docs.
target_output.security_and_compliance: AppKey protection: AppKey is accepted as a constructor argument to register_device but never assigned to any model attribute; the pydantic DeviceRecord validator strips app_key from the normalized model; DeviceRecord.__repr__ shows dev_eui and application_id only. API keys passed via constructor keyword; never written to module-level variables or log output; LoraSdkError subclasses explicitly excluded from including auth material in message string. HTTPS enforced: httpx client verifies TLS certificates by default with no verify=False option exposed; ChirpStack adapter uses grpc.ssl_channel_credentials with explicit CA bundle or system roots; no plaintext channel option in the public API. Security guide in docs/security.md advises callers to source AppKeys from AWS Secrets Manager or HashiCorp Vault and avoid environment variables for production deployments.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: PyPI Trusted Publishing via GitHub Actions OIDC: no API key stored in repository secrets; release triggered by push of version tag matching v*.*.*, workflow calls pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish. Makefile generate-proto target: python -m grpc_tools.protoc -I lorawan_sdk/adapters/proto/ --python_betterproto_out=lorawan_sdk/adapters/_chirpstack_pb/ $(wildcard lorawan_sdk/adapters/proto/*.proto). CI proto-check job: runs make generate-proto followed by git diff --exit-code lorawan_sdk/adapters/_chirpstack_pb/; fails PR if generated files differ from committed files, enforcing that proto upgrades are always accompanied by regenerated bindings. GitHub Actions matrix: Python 3.10, 3.11, 3.12. Integration test job: weekly schedule against TTS Community Server sandbox, manual trigger via workflow_dispatch.
target_output.observability: Codecov with 90% gate and branch coverage blocking PR merge. mypy strict mode in CI; no ignored type errors. ruff lint with all rules enabled. Weekly integration test results posted as GitHub Actions job summary. Read the Docs build triggered on every release tag; docs coverage badge in README showing percentage of public symbols with docstrings.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero hosting cost: GitHub Actions free tier for open-source. Read the Docs free plan for documentation hosting. PyPI Trusted Publishing is free. Optional self-hosted TTS Community Server for integration tests runs on a $10/month VPS. Package bundle is lightweight: lorawan_sdk core installs only httpx and pydantic, approximately 2 MB installed. ChirpStack extras add grpcio at approximately 8 MB.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 v1.0 (8 weeks): NetworkServerClient ABC, TheThingsStackAdapter with httpx, DeviceRecord/DownlinkFrame/UplinkFrame pydantic models, AppKey protection, respx-based test suite, Codecov at 90%, PyPI Trusted Publishing workflow, Read the Docs. Phase 2 v1.1 (4 weeks): ChirpStackAdapter with grpc.aio.secure_channel, betterproto proto codegen Makefile and CI check, chirpstack-api v4.8 proto files vendored, grpc-testing mock test suite, uplink subscription via gRPC server streaming. Phase 3 v1.2 (3 weeks): Actility ThingPark adapter, batch device provisioning from CSV via register_devices_from_csv helper, network server health check polling method.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 type hints throughout with mypy strict. ruff for lint and format. pytest-asyncio for async tests with asyncio_mode=auto. All public methods documented with Google-style docstrings. AppKey must never appear in any string format, log call, or exception message; CI grep for 'app_key' in log calls enforced via pre-commit hook. Proto files in lorawan_sdk/adapters/proto/ must not be hand-edited; only update via official chirpstack-api release and re-run make generate-proto. Conventional commits with automated changelog via git-cliff.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: TTS REST API v3 field name changes breaking TheThingsStackAdapter normalization silently. Mitigation: pydantic strict validation rejects unexpected fields from vendor responses; weekly integration tests against TTS sandbox surface regressions within 7 days. Risk: ChirpStack gRPC API incompatible changes between chirpstack-api v4.8 and future v4.x releases. Mitigation: proto version pinned in Makefile; major API version changes result in a new adapter module (chirpstack_v4.py, chirpstack_v5.py) rather than in-place update. Tradeoff: betterproto over official grpcio codegen gives more Pythonic dataclasses at the cost of a less mature code generator; acceptable for v1.x given betterproto stability on v4 protos.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected grpc.aio over grpc (sync) for ChirpStack adapter to maintain the async-first API contract without asyncio.run blocking. Confirmed single shared grpc.aio.Channel per adapter instance over per-call channels for connection efficiency; gRPC multiplexing handles concurrent calls on a single channel safely. Chosen Sphinx over MkDocs for documentation to leverage autodoc and the established Read the Docs integration.

metadata.notes: Round 2 moderate-quality LoRaWAN SDK plan resolving gRPC channel lifecycle and betterproto codegen issues from round 1.

project_goal.value: Establish a zero-trust network overlay with mTLS certificate lifecycle management for IoT edge gateways across retail stores to prevent lateral movement on gateway compromise
project_goal.rationale: Edge gateways handle payment and inventory data; lateral movement risk is unacceptable

target_users.value: Platform security engineers and network ops teams managing retail store infrastructure
target_users.rationale: Internal operators responsible for gateway fleet health

project_class.rationale: Pure platform and environment architecture with no application UI

capabilities.rationale: Certificate rotation agent and platform tooling only; no frontend

complexity_level.rationale: SPIFFE/SPIRE identity mesh, Cilium policy enforcement, automated rotation across hundreds of stores

risk_level.rationale: Gateway compromise in a retail payment network is a PCI-DSS incident

data_sensitivity.rationale: Certificate material and network topology are confidential security assets

external_exposure.rationale: Management plane is air-gapped from public internet; store gateways reach control plane via private WAN

access_model.value: SPIFFE/SPIRE workload identity; human operators use OIDC-federated kubectl with RBAC; no shared credentials
access_model.rationale: Eliminates static credential exposure; every workload cryptographically attested

feature_scope.value: SPIRE trust domain per store cluster, SVID issuance, 24-hour certificate rotation, Cilium network policy enforcement, policy violation alerting, rotation audit log
feature_scope.rationale: Full zero-trust lifecycle from provisioning to rotation and audit

mvp_scope.value: SPIRE deployment on EKS, SVID issuance for edge gateway workloads, Cilium mTLS enforcement, rotation agent for 24-hour SVID refresh
mvp_scope.rationale: Core identity and mTLS before tackling advanced policy sets

security_baseline.value: mTLS mandatory for all east-west traffic; SVID validity max 48 hours; SPIRE server HA; etcd encrypted at rest; Cilium deny-all default policy
security_baseline.rationale: PCI-DSS and internal policy requirements

frontend_stack.value: none - infrastructure project with no browser UI
frontend_stack.rationale: Operators interact via kubectl and CLI tooling

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 custom certificate rotation agent; SPIRE server and agent; Cilium 1.15 CNI
backend_stack.rationale: Go suits long-running agents; SPIRE is the CNCF reference for SPIFFE

data_platform.value: etcd (via Kubernetes) for cluster state; Datadog Logs for rotation audit records
data_platform.rationale: No external DB needed; etcd stores SPIRE state; audit shipped to Datadog

hosting_target.value: EKS on AWS with Cilium CNI replacing aws-vpc-cni; store gateways run k3s with SPIRE agent
hosting_target.rationale: EKS for control plane; k3s lightweight for constrained store hardware

privacy_retention_policy.value: Certificate rotation logs retained 12 months; policy violation events retained 24 months for PCI audit trail
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: PCI-DSS requires 12-month audit log retention; security events extended to 24 months

future_scope.value: OPA policy engine for fine-grained authorization; federated trust domains across cloud regions; automated store onboarding pipeline
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 hardening after zero-trust baseline is stable

constraints.value: Store gateways run on constrained hardware (4 vCPU, 4 GB RAM); WAN links to stores are 100 Mbps with 30 ms latency; must not interrupt store POS operations during rotation
constraints.rationale: Hardware and network constraints drive agent design and rotation scheduling

observability_baseline.value: Datadog agent on EKS nodes; SPIRE metrics via Prometheus scraped by Datadog; PagerDuty alerts for rotation failures and policy violations
observability_baseline.rationale: Corporate monitoring standard; rotation failures must page on-call within 5 minutes

execution_preference.value: Infrastructure-as-code with Terraform and Helm; GitOps with ArgoCD for cluster state
execution_preference.rationale: Reproducible, auditable deployments aligned with IaC policy

compliance_context.value: PCI-DSS v4.0 Requirement 4 (encryption in transit) and Requirement 10 (audit logging); SOC 2 Type II CC6 controls; internal security policy limiting SVID validity to 48 hours
compliance_context.rationale: Retail payment network requires PCI-DSS; SOC 2 for enterprise customer trust

project.goal: Zero-trust mTLS infrastructure for retail IoT edge gateway fleet
project.target_users: Platform security engineers and network ops

backend.api: SPIFFE Workload API (gRPC)

security.transport: mTLS enforced by Cilium on all pod-to-pod traffic

data.audit: Datadog Logs for rotation and violation events

devops.environments: staging-cluster, production (per-region EKS + 200 store k3s clusters)

constraints.gateway_hardware: 4 vCPU, 4 GB RAM per store gateway

reasoner_reviews.product: The zero-trust mandate is clear and the SPIFFE/SPIRE choice is well-grounded. The 24-hour SVID rotation cadence is aggressive but achievable given the rotation agent design. The constraint on no POS disruption during rotation is the critical operational requirement that must be reflected in the rotation scheduling logic.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: SPIRE HA configuration needs at least 3 server replicas with Postgres backend for the trust domain database, not etcd, to survive EKS node failures. The rotation agent must implement a pre-rotation health check against the SPIFFE Workload API before hot-swapping the certificate material. Previous rounds missed the SPIRE server backend specification.
reasoner_reviews.security: Round 2 addressed Cilium default-deny policy but the SVID bootstrap attestation method for k3s store nodes was underspecified. Node attestation via AWS IID is not available for on-premises k3s; the plan must specify k8s_psat or join_token attestation with a per-store bootstrap token scoped to the store SPIFFE trust domain suffix.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: WAN latency of 30 ms is tolerable for SVID fetches but the rotation agent must implement exponential backoff with jitter to avoid thundering-herd SVID renewals from 200 stores hitting the SPIRE server simultaneously at the 24-hour mark.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Prior plan version had a gap in the policy_violations alerting path -- it described Cilium network policy enforcement but did not specify how Hubble flow logs route to Datadog for violation detection. This must be addressed in round 3.

backend.service_design: Go rotation agent runs as a DaemonSet on each k3s store node; fetches SVIDs via SPIFFE Workload API Unix socket; implements pre-rotation health check, hot-swap via symlink atomic rename, and post-rotation verification by dialing a local mTLS health endpoint
backend.api_patterns: Internal gRPC to SPIFFE Workload API for SVID fetch; REST to SPIRE Federation API for trust bundle exchange; Prometheus /metrics endpoint on port 9090
backend.failure_handling: On SVID fetch failure: exponential backoff 5s/10s/30s/60s then PagerDuty alert; certificate expiry within 2-hour window triggers emergency renew with bypass of jitter schedule
backend.scaling_notes: 200 store nodes each run one rotation agent; SPIRE server must handle 200 concurrent SVID renewals within a 30-minute jitter window; benchmark shows SPIRE 1.9 handles 500 req/s with 3 replicas

security.auth_design: SPIRE server issues SVIDs under trust domain spiffe://retail.internal; store nodes attest via k8s_psat attestor using service account tokens scoped per store namespace
security.authorization_model: Cilium NetworkPolicy deny-all baseline; explicit allow rules for gateway-to-inventory-service, gateway-to-pos-api, and agent-to-spire-server only
security.secrets_management: Bootstrap join tokens stored in AWS Secrets Manager; rotated per store provisioning event; SPIRE agent joins then derives all subsequent identity from SVID chain
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Hubble CLI exports Cilium flow logs to stdout; Datadog Agent DaemonSet ships logs; rotation events logged as structured JSON by the Go agent with store_id, svid_expiry, rotation_status fields

data.entities: spiffe_identities (spiffe_id, store_id, workload_selector, issued_at, expires_at), certificate_rotation_logs (id, store_id, gateway_hostname, old_svid_serial, new_svid_serial, rotation_status, rotated_at), policy_violations (id, src_spiffe_id, dst_spiffe_id, action ENUM allow/deny, flow_direction, detected_at, resolved_at), store_locations (store_id, region, k3s_api_endpoint, spire_agent_version)
data.storage_design: SPIRE server state in PostgreSQL RDS (not etcd) for HA; Kubernetes cluster state in etcd as standard; rotation logs shipped to Datadog Log Management with 12-month retention index
data.migration_notes: Prior plan used etcd for SPIRE state; migrating to PostgreSQL backend requires a SPIRE server upgrade and data export/import during a maintenance window

devops.deployment_topology: EKS control plane in us-east-1 running SPIRE server (3 replicas), ArgoCD, Datadog agent; per-store k3s cluster running SPIRE agent DaemonSet and rotation agent DaemonSet; Hubble relay on EKS for centralized flow log aggregation
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: Terraform plan on PR, Terraform apply on merge to main; Helm chart lint and diff via helm-diff; ArgoCD ApplicationSets generate per-store k3s manifests from a store inventory ConfigMap
devops.rollback_strategy: ArgoCD sync reverts to previous Helm chart revision; SPIRE server rollback via Terraform state; rotation agent rollback via DaemonSet image tag pin in ArgoCD app spec
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scrapes SPIRE server metrics (x509_svid_issued_total, svid_rotation_duration_seconds); Datadog Dashboards for rotation success rate per store; PagerDuty rules on rotation_failure_count > 0 in 5-minute window

ISS-002.title: k3s node attestation method unspecified
ISS-002.detail: Store k3s nodes cannot use AWS IID attestor; round 2 plan added k8s_psat attestation with per-store bootstrap tokens scoped to store SPIFFE trust domain suffix

ISS-003.title: Thundering herd risk on 24-hour SVID renewal
ISS-003.detail: 200 store agents renewing SVIDs simultaneously at the 24-hour mark will spike SPIRE server load; jitter strategy not implemented in rotation agent

ISS-004.title: Hubble flow log routing to Datadog unspecified
ISS-004.detail: Round 2 plan described Cilium policy enforcement but did not define how Hubble network flow events reach Datadog for violation alerting

previous_plan.title: Retail Edge Zero-Trust mTLS Infrastructure v2
previous_plan.executive_summary: Zero-trust infrastructure using SPIFFE/SPIRE with PostgreSQL backend on EKS; k3s store clusters attest via k8s_psat; Cilium enforces mTLS east-west; Go rotation agent refreshes SVIDs every 24 hours
previous_plan.architecture_overview: SPIRE server HA on EKS backed by PostgreSQL RDS Multi-AZ; SPIRE agent DaemonSet on k3s store clusters attesting via k8s_psat; Cilium enforces deny-all with selective mTLS allow rules
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: k8s_psat attestation with per-store bootstrap tokens from AWS Secrets Manager; Cilium default-deny NetworkPolicy; PCI-DSS audit log via Datadog

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 focuses on ISS-003 (thundering herd) and ISS-004 (Hubble log routing). The jitter fix is a deterministic per-store offset computed as store_id modulo 1800 seconds (30 minutes), ensuring 200 stores spread evenly. For ISS-004, Hubble Relay runs as a DaemonSet on EKS exporting Cilium flow logs to Datadog Agent via a local file_tail listener. Both fixes integrate cleanly into the existing GitOps deployment structure.

fix_report.action_taken: Added jitter_offset_seconds = store_id_hash % 1800 to rotation agent renewal scheduler; renewal fires at SVID issue_time + 24h + jitter_offset; offset is deterministic so it survives agent restarts
fix_report.expected_outcome: 200 store renewal events spread across a 30-minute window; SPIRE server peak load reduced from 200 concurrent requests to ~7 per minute

fix_report.action_taken: Added Hubble Relay DaemonSet to EKS cluster; Relay exports flow logs in JSON to /var/log/hubble/flows.json; Datadog Agent file_tail input ships to Datadog Logs index with dd_source=cilium tag
fix_report.expected_outcome: All Cilium policy_violations events visible in Datadog within 30 seconds; PagerDuty alert fires when deny action count exceeds 10 in 5-minute rolling window

target_output.title: Retail Edge Zero-Trust mTLS Infrastructure
target_output.executive_summary: Security-hardened Kubernetes infrastructure establishing SPIFFE/SPIRE-based workload identity and Cilium-enforced mTLS for a fleet of IoT edge gateways across 200 retail stores. SPIRE server runs HA on EKS backed by PostgreSQL RDS; store k3s clusters attest via k8s_psat with per-store bootstrap tokens; a Go rotation agent refreshes SVIDs every 24 hours with per-store jitter to prevent renewal storms; Hubble Relay ships Cilium flow logs to Datadog for policy violation alerting.
target_output.architecture_overview: Hub-and-spoke trust architecture: SPIRE server cluster (3 replicas) on EKS us-east-1 acts as the root trust domain authority for spiffe://retail.internal; each store k3s cluster runs a SPIRE agent that bootstraps via k8s_psat attestation with a per-store join token from AWS Secrets Manager, then receives X.509 SVIDs valid for 24 hours with store-scoped SPIFFE IDs (spiffe://retail.internal/store/{store_id}/gateway). Cilium CNI replaces aws-vpc-cni on EKS and acts as the mTLS enforcement point for all east-west pod traffic using SVID-derived identities. Hubble Relay centralizes Cilium network flow telemetry from all EKS nodes to a Datadog log pipeline. ArgoCD ApplicationSets generate per-store k3s Helm releases from a store inventory ConfigMap, enabling GitOps-driven fleet management.
target_output.technology_stack: Identity: SPIRE 1.9 server (3 replicas) + SPIRE agent DaemonSet; SPIFFE Workload API (gRPC Unix socket). Network: Cilium 1.15 CNI with Hubble Relay for flow telemetry. Rotation Agent: Go 1.22 DaemonSet with per-store jitter scheduling. State: PostgreSQL 15 RDS Multi-AZ for SPIRE datastore; etcd for Kubernetes cluster state. GitOps: ArgoCD 2.10 with ApplicationSets. IaC: Terraform 1.7 for EKS, RDS, VPC, Secrets Manager. CI: GitHub Actions. Observability: Datadog Agent + Prometheus; PagerDuty.
target_output.functional_feature_map: SPIFFE Identity Issuance: per-workload SVID provisioning scoped to store trust domain with 24-hour TTL. mTLS Enforcement: Cilium deny-all baseline with explicit allow rules for gateway-to-POS-API and gateway-to-inventory-service traffic lanes. Certificate Rotation: automated 24-hour SVID refresh with deterministic per-store jitter offset and hot-swap via atomic symlink rename. Policy Violation Alerting: Hubble flow events classified as deny actions routed to Datadog and PagerDuty within 30 seconds. Fleet GitOps: ArgoCD ApplicationSets generate per-store manifests from store inventory; store onboarding reduces to adding a ConfigMap entry.
target_output.system_components: SPIRE Server Deployment (3 replicas, EKS, PostgreSQL RDS backend, SPIFFE trust domain spiffe://retail.internal). SPIRE Agent DaemonSet (per k3s store cluster, k8s_psat attestation, Workload API on Unix socket). Go Rotation Agent DaemonSet (per store node, jitter_offset = store_id_hash % 1800s, hot-swap via atomic symlink). Cilium CNI (EKS and k3s, deny-all NetworkPolicy, mTLS via SVID identities). Hubble Relay DaemonSet (EKS, JSON flow log export to /var/log/hubble/flows.json). Datadog Agent DaemonSet (file_tail on Hubble logs, Prometheus scrape of SPIRE metrics, dd_source=cilium tag). ArgoCD Application Server (ApplicationSets for per-store k3s manifests from store-inventory ConfigMap). PostgreSQL RDS Multi-AZ (SPIRE datastore, us-east-1). AWS Secrets Manager (per-store bootstrap join tokens, rotated on store provisioning).
target_output.workflows: Gateway Provisioning: ops engineer adds store entry to store-inventory ConfigMap in Git; ArgoCD detects diff and applies a new ApplicationSet release to the store k3s cluster; SPIRE agent starts and performs k8s_psat attestation, submitting a service account token and cluster metadata to the SPIRE server; SPIRE server validates the token against the store k8s API server (registered as a trusted cluster), issues a node SVID, and the agent begins serving Workload API to local pods. Certificate Rotation: at hour 23 + (store_id_hash % 30 minutes), the Go rotation agent calls svid_stream on the Workload API, receives the new X.509 SVID, writes the cert and key PEM files to a staging path, validates the chain against the trust bundle, atomically renames the symlink to point to the staging path, then dials the gateway's local mTLS health endpoint to confirm the new certificate is live; logs rotation_status=success with old and new serial numbers to stdout for Datadog shipping. Policy Violation Response: Hubble Relay captures Cilium flow log with action=DROPPED for unauthorized src/dst SPIFFE ID pair; Datadog Agent ships log entry within 15 seconds; Datadog alert fires when deny_count > 10 in 5-minute window; PagerDuty incident created with src_store_id and affected workload SPIFFE ID for network ops triage.
target_output.data_model: spiffe_identities (spiffe_id TEXT PK, store_id VARCHAR, workload_selector TEXT, trust_domain TEXT, issued_at TIMESTAMPTZ, expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ, last_rotation_at TIMESTAMPTZ) -- stored in SPIRE PostgreSQL datastore. certificate_rotation_logs (id UUID PK, store_id VARCHAR, gateway_hostname TEXT, old_svid_serial TEXT, new_svid_serial TEXT, rotation_status ENUM success/failed/skipped, jitter_offset_seconds INT, rotated_at TIMESTAMPTZ) -- shipped to Datadog Log Management. policy_violations (id UUID PK, src_spiffe_id TEXT, dst_spiffe_id TEXT, action ENUM allow/deny, src_store_id VARCHAR, detected_at TIMESTAMPTZ, resolved_at TIMESTAMPTZ, datadog_event_id TEXT) -- reconstructed from Hubble flow logs in Datadog. store_locations (store_id VARCHAR PK, region TEXT, k3s_api_endpoint TEXT, spire_agent_version TEXT, last_seen_at TIMESTAMPTZ) -- stored in store-inventory ConfigMap.
target_output.api_design: SPIFFE Workload API (gRPC, Unix socket /run/spire/sockets/agent.sock): FetchX509SVID RPC returns X.509 SVID bundle for calling workload matched by Kubernetes pod selector. SPIRE Server Registration API (gRPC, internal EKS service): CreateEntry for new workload selectors; DeleteEntry on decommission. Rotation Agent Internal: no external API; emits Prometheus counter spire_rotation_total{status='success|failed',store_id} and gauge spire_svid_expiry_seconds{store_id}. ArgoCD API (HTTPS, internal): POST /api/v1/applications for ApplicationSet-driven per-store k3s app creation triggered by store-inventory ConfigMap change.
target_output.security_and_compliance: PCI-DSS v4.0 Requirement 4.2: all in-scope data paths use mTLS enforced by Cilium with SVID-derived identities; no plaintext east-west traffic permitted. Requirement 10.2: certificate_rotation_logs and policy_violations retained in Datadog for 12 and 24 months respectively; log integrity protected by Datadog archive with S3 Object Lock. SOC 2 CC6.6: per-store bootstrap tokens stored in AWS Secrets Manager with automatic rotation on provisioning; no long-lived static credentials. Internal policy: SVID TTL hard-capped at 48 hours; current 24-hour TTL satisfies this. Attestation integrity: k8s_psat tokens validated against per-store k8s API server; SPIRE server maintains an allowlist of trusted cluster API endpoints registered at store provisioning time.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: EKS cluster (us-east-1, 3 m6i.xlarge nodes for SPIRE + ArgoCD workloads, Cilium CNI replacing aws-vpc-cni). PostgreSQL RDS Multi-AZ db.r6g.large for SPIRE datastore. Terraform manages: EKS, RDS, VPC private subnets, Transit Gateway for store WAN connectivity, Secrets Manager policies. ArgoCD ApplicationSets: parent app in EKS watches store-inventory ConfigMap; for each store entry generates a k3s Helm release with SPIRE agent, rotation agent, and Cilium policies pre-configured with store_id values. GitHub Actions CI pipeline: Terraform fmt/validate/plan on PR; helm lint and helm-diff on Helm chart changes; Terraform apply via GitHub Actions OIDC role on merge to main. Environments: staging-cluster (single-node k3s emulating one store) for integration tests; production EKS + 200 store k3s clusters. Rollback: ArgoCD sync to previous Helm revision; SPIRE server rollback via Terraform state revert followed by database restore from automated RDS snapshot.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics scraped from SPIRE server: x509_svid_issued_total (rate of new SVIDs), svid_rotation_duration_seconds histogram, attestation_failures_total by attestor type. Rotation agent custom Prometheus metrics: spire_rotation_total{status,store_id}, spire_svid_expiry_seconds{store_id} (alert when < 7200 seconds). Hubble Relay JSON flow logs shipped via Datadog Agent file_tail with dd_source=cilium; Datadog Log facets on src_spiffe_id, dst_spiffe_id, action; saved search for action:DROPPED triggers PagerDuty when count > 10 in 5-minute window. Datadog Dashboard: per-store rotation success rate heatmap, SVID issuance rate, policy violation count by store. PagerDuty escalation: rotation_failure on any store pages network-security on-call within 5 minutes.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: EKS control plane ~$300/month; 3 m6i.xlarge nodes ~$450/month; RDS db.r6g.large Multi-AZ ~$250/month; Datadog Log ingestion for rotation and Hubble logs at ~50 GB/month ~$125/month. Total ~$1,125/month for 200 stores. Scale path: SPIRE server horizontal scaling to 5 replicas at 500 stores; RDS read replica for SPIRE registration API reads; ArgoCD shard for >500 ApplicationSets.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (5 weeks): Terraform EKS cluster with Cilium CNI, PostgreSQL RDS SPIRE datastore, SPIRE server HA deployment, k8s_psat attestation configuration for first 5 pilot stores, deny-all Cilium NetworkPolicy with POS-API allow rule. Phase 2 (3 weeks): Go rotation agent with jitter scheduling deployed to pilot stores, Hubble Relay DaemonSet with Datadog log pipeline, PagerDuty alert rules for rotation failure and policy violations, rotation audit log validation. Phase 3 (2 weeks): ArgoCD ApplicationSets for remaining 195 stores, store onboarding runbook, full PCI-DSS audit log validation across all stores, load test of 200 simultaneous near-miss renewals against SPIRE server.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go rotation agent: table-driven unit tests for jitter offset calculation and atomic symlink swap logic; integration test against a local SPIRE dev server. Terraform: modules for EKS, RDS, and store k3s registration; plan output reviewed in PR before apply. Helm charts: values schema validation with helm-schema-gen; diff shown in PR via helm-diff plugin. All SPIRE registration entries managed as code in Git; manual kubectl exec to SPIRE server forbidden in production.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: SPIRE server PostgreSQL RDS failure takes down SVID issuance; mitigation: RDS Multi-AZ with automatic failover; SVID 24-hour TTL provides 24-hour grace window before existing certificates expire. Risk: k3s API server unreachable during k8s_psat attestation at provisioning time; mitigation: SPIRE agent retries with exponential backoff; store can operate with existing SVIDs until connectivity restores. Tradeoff: Cilium CNI replacement of aws-vpc-cni requires EKS node group rolling update causing brief per-node traffic disruption; accepted during maintenance window. Tradeoff: deterministic jitter (store_id_hash % 1800) vs. random jitter -- deterministic chosen so jitter offset is predictable for operational scheduling of SPIRE server maintenance windows.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed k8s_psat attestation for k3s nodes over join_token to eliminate static secret exposure. Chosen PostgreSQL RDS over CockroachDB for SPIRE datastore due to SPIRE 1.9 native PostgreSQL support. Hubble Relay selected over Tetragon for network flow logging as it requires no kernel BTF dependency on store gateway hardware. Jitter strategy set to deterministic store_id_hash modulo 1800 seconds.

metadata.notes: Round 3 zero-trust mTLS infrastructure; resolves thundering-herd jitter and Hubble log routing gaps.

project_goal.value: Real-time monitoring dashboard for smart irrigation systems displaying live soil moisture, valve states, and rainfall readings from hundreds of field zones with manual overrides and automated schedule management
project_goal.rationale: Replace paper-based irrigation logs and radio-dispatched valve controls with a web dashboard

target_users.value: Farm managers and irrigation technicians at commercial farms
target_users.rationale: Primary operators who set schedules and respond to moisture anomalies

project_class.rationale: Requires a React dashboard UI and a Node.js backend with WebSocket for live sensor feeds

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, realtime, integrations
capabilities.rationale: Live sensor streams, valve control API, schedule engine, Firebase Auth, and sensor hub integrations

complexity_level.rationale: Real-time WebSocket fan-out but bounded user base and limited domain logic

risk_level.rationale: Incorrect valve actuation could damage crops but no financial or health data involved

data_sensitivity.rationale: Farm operational data is business-sensitive but not regulated

external_exposure.rationale: Firebase Auth required for all dashboard access; no public endpoints

access_model.value: Firebase Auth with email/password; farm-level tenancy isolation; RBAC with manager and technician roles
access_model.rationale: Multi-farm SaaS with per-farm data isolation required

feature_scope.value: Live zone dashboard with soil moisture gauges and valve state indicators, manual valve override, irrigation schedule editor, water usage reporting, alert rules for moisture out-of-range
feature_scope.rationale: Core feature set replacing manual field operations

mvp_scope.value: Live dashboard for 1 farm with up to 50 zones, manual valve override, basic scheduling, moisture and rainfall live readings
mvp_scope.rationale: Single-farm pilot before multi-tenant rollout

security_baseline.value: Firebase Auth JWT validation on all API routes; HTTPS only; PostgreSQL row-level security for farm tenancy isolation; no sensor data exposed without auth
security_baseline.rationale: Multi-tenant farm data must not leak across tenants

frontend_stack.value: React 18 with TypeScript
frontend_stack.rationale: Team expertise; React ecosystem suits dashboard-style real-time UI

backend_stack.value: Node.js 20 with Express and Socket.IO
backend_stack.rationale: Socket.IO simplifies WebSocket room management for per-farm subscriptions

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for operational data; Redis 7 for sensor state cache and Socket.IO pub/sub adapter
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL for schedule and audit data; Redis pub/sub fans out sensor updates to Socket.IO workers

hosting_target.value: DigitalOcean App Platform for API and frontend; DigitalOcean Managed PostgreSQL and Redis
hosting_target.rationale: Cost-effective managed services for small-to-medium farm scale

privacy_retention_policy.value: Sensor readings retained 2 years; irrigation actuation logs retained 5 years; no personal data collected beyond farm manager email
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Agronomic records needed for multi-season trend analysis

future_scope.value: Multi-farm portal with cross-farm analytics; predictive irrigation recommendations via weather forecast API; mobile app for field technicians
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 expansion after single-farm MVP validation

constraints.value: DigitalOcean App Platform budget under $200/month; field sensor hubs connect over cellular with intermittent connectivity; no on-premise infrastructure
constraints.rationale: Small-farm customer budget; cellular connectivity requires offline-tolerant sensor hubs

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for API and WebSocket metrics; structured logging to DigitalOcean Logs; alert on WebSocket disconnect rate and valve command failures
observability_baseline.rationale: Lightweight observability stack fitting the DigitalOcean budget

execution_preference.value: Agile 2-week sprints; GitHub Flow with feature branches and PR review
execution_preference.rationale: Small team iteration cadence

project.goal: Real-time irrigation monitoring and control dashboard

frontend.state: React Query + Zustand for WebSocket state

backend.api: REST + WebSocket

reasoner_reviews.product: The feature scope is well-matched to replacing manual irrigation dispatch. The valve override control is the highest-risk feature -- a mis-sent command could flood or drought a zone. Confirmation dialogs and actuation audit logs are essential and were thin in round 1.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Socket.IO with Redis pub/sub adapter correctly supports horizontal scaling of the API tier. The round 1 plan did not specify how sensor hub messages arrive at the backend; the ingestion path from cellular hub to Redis pub/sub needs to be explicit in this revision.
reasoner_reviews.security: Row-level security on PostgreSQL is the right tenancy isolation mechanism but round 1 plan did not specify the RLS policy predicate. The valve override endpoint must log the Firebase UID and timestamp for every actuation to provide an audit trail.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: DigitalOcean App Platform auto-scaling can handle burst WebSocket connections but Socket.IO sticky sessions must be configured; the round 1 plan omitted this configuration detail.

frontend.app_structure: React SPA with route-based code splitting; FarmDashboard, ZoneDetail, ScheduleEditor, WaterUsageReport, AlertRules pages
frontend.pages_and_flows: FarmDashboard shows a zone grid with live moisture percentage gauges and valve open/closed icons updated via Socket.IO events; ZoneDetail shows 24-hour moisture trend chart; ScheduleEditor allows defining target moisture range and override windows per zone
frontend.state_management: React Query for REST data (schedules, reports); Zustand store for WebSocket zone state keyed by zone_id; Socket.IO client connects on auth, joins farm_id room, updates Zustand on zone_update events
frontend.frontend_risks: WebSocket reconnection on cellular handoff must replay latest zone state to avoid stale valve indicators in the Zustand store

backend.service_design: Express app with REST routes and Socket.IO server sharing HTTP instance; Redis pub/sub adapter for multi-instance room broadcast; sensor ingestion via POST /ingest/:hub_id from field hubs using HMAC-SHA256 auth
backend.api_patterns: REST for schedules and reports; Socket.IO rooms for live zone state push; idempotent valve command API with last-write-wins conflict resolution
backend.failure_handling: Valve command failures logged to override_commands with status=failed; retry not automatic to avoid double-actuation; stale sensor detection via last_seen_at

security.auth_design: Firebase Admin SDK validates ID token on every Express route; WebSocket handshake authenticated with same Firebase JWT
security.authorization_model: RBAC: manager role can edit schedules and trigger overrides; technician role can trigger manual overrides but not edit schedules; farm_id claim in Firebase custom token scopes all queries
security.audit_and_logging_controls: override_commands table records firebase_uid, zone_id, action, timestamp for every valve actuation; readable by farm managers in audit view

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 fixes two blocking gaps: the sensor hub ingestion path and Socket.IO sticky session configuration. The ingestion endpoint uses HMAC-SHA256 per-hub shared secrets to authenticate field hubs without Firebase Auth, since field hubs cannot run a browser OAuth flow. Socket.IO Redis adapter resolves the multi-instance room subscription problem on DigitalOcean App Platform. Both fixes integrate cleanly into the existing PostgreSQL RLS and Firebase Auth security model.

fix_report.action_taken: Added POST /ingest/:hub_id endpoint authenticated by HMAC-SHA256 signature of payload and timestamp using per-hub secret from hub_credentials table; handler upserts sensor readings to PostgreSQL and publishes zone_update event to Redis channel farm:{farm_id}:zones
fix_report.expected_outcome: Field sensor hubs deliver readings over HTTPS with hub-level authentication; readings fan out to all dashboard clients subscribed to the farm room via Redis pub/sub

fix_report.action_taken: Added @socket.io/redis-adapter to Express Socket.IO server; DigitalOcean App Platform sticky session affinity enabled via platform configuration; Socket.IO rooms keyed by farm_id so any API instance can publish to the correct room via Redis adapter
fix_report.expected_outcome: Multi-instance API tier correctly fans out zone_update events to all clients in a farm room regardless of which instance they connected to

target_output.title: Smart Irrigation Real-Time Dashboard
target_output.executive_summary: React 18 dashboard with Socket.IO live zone state updates for commercial farm irrigation management. Node.js Express backend with Socket.IO and Redis pub/sub adapter handles real-time fan-out across two DigitalOcean App Platform instances. Field sensor hubs authenticate via HMAC-SHA256 and POST moisture and rainfall readings to a dedicated ingestion endpoint. PostgreSQL 16 with row-level security isolates farm tenancy. Valve override commands are audit-logged per actuation including Firebase UID.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-tier architecture: React 18 SPA served as a static DigitalOcean App Platform site communicates over HTTPS and WSS to a Node.js Express API running on two App Platform instances with @socket.io/redis-adapter for cross-instance room broadcasting. Field sensor hubs (cellular-connected) POST to POST /ingest/:hub_id with HMAC-SHA256 authentication; the ingestion handler upserts readings to PostgreSQL moisture_sensors and rainfall_gauges tables, then publishes a Redis zone_update message on channel farm:{farm_id}:zones. Socket.IO instances subscribed via Redis adapter receive the message and emit zone_update events to all authenticated clients in the farm room. Firebase Auth JWT validates all dashboard user sessions; PostgreSQL RLS policy enforces farm-level tenancy isolation at the database layer.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, React Query, Zustand, Socket.IO client 4.x. Backend: Node.js 20, Express 4, Socket.IO 4 with @socket.io/redis-adapter, Firebase Admin SDK 12, node-pg-migrate. Database: DigitalOcean Managed PostgreSQL 16 with RLS. Cache: DigitalOcean Managed Redis 7. Hosting: DigitalOcean App Platform (2 API instances + static site). CI: GitHub Actions. Monitoring: Grafana + Prometheus; DigitalOcean Logs.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Live Zone Dashboard: Socket.IO zone_update events update Zustand store; React renders moisture percentage gauges per zone with open/closed valve state icons; zones with last_seen_at > 10 minutes shown in stale warning state. Manual Valve Override: manager clicks override, confirms dialog, POST /api/v1/zones/:id/override with action=open or close; actuation logged to override_commands with Firebase UID. Irrigation Schedule Editor: form persists schedule rules via REST; scheduling engine evaluates zones hourly against target moisture thresholds. Water Usage Report: aggregated volume_liters per zone over date range, exported as CSV. Alert Rules: per-zone moisture_min/max bounds; server-side check on ingestion triggers email notification on breach.
target_output.system_components: DigitalOcean App Platform API service (2 instances, sticky session affinity cookie enabled, Node.js 20 Express). Socket.IO 4 with @socket.io/redis-adapter subscribing to farm:{farm_id}:zones Redis channels. Sensor Ingestion Handler (POST /ingest/:hub_id, HMAC-SHA256 validation with 60-second timestamp replay window, upserts moisture_sensors and rainfall_gauges, publishes to Redis). Hourly Scheduling Engine (Node.js cron, evaluates irrigation_schedules against current zone moisture, issues valve commands). DigitalOcean Managed PostgreSQL 16 with RLS policy USING (farm_id = current_setting('app.farm_id')::uuid). DigitalOcean Managed Redis 7 (Socket.IO adapter and zone state cache). Firebase Auth (JWT validation via Admin SDK middleware on all routes). GitHub Actions CI. Grafana + Prometheus.
