target_output.workflows: Sensor Reading Ingestion: field hub computes HMAC-SHA256 of JSON payload plus Unix timestamp using per-hub secret stored in hub_credentials; sends POST /ingest/:hub_id with X-Hub-Signature header; Express middleware validates signature and timestamp freshness within 60 seconds to prevent replay; handler upserts reading to moisture_sensors or rainfall_gauges; publishes {zone_id, moisture_pct, valve_state, ts} to Redis PUBLISH on farm:{farm_id}:zones; all Socket.IO instances subscribed via Redis adapter receive message and emit zone_update to clients in room farm_{farm_id}. Valve Override: farm manager clicks override on a zone, Zustand dispatches optimistic state update, POST /api/v1/zones/:id/override with Firebase JWT; API inserts override_commands row with status=sent, calls valve controller REST endpoint on field hub, updates status to confirmed or failed, logs firebase_uid and timestamp; Socket.IO broadcasts zone_update with new valve_state to all clients in farm room.
target_output.data_model: farm_zones (id UUID PK, farm_id UUID, name TEXT, zone_type ENUM field/greenhouse/orchard, target_moisture_min SMALLINT, target_moisture_max SMALLINT, valve_id UUID, enabled BOOL). irrigation_valves (id UUID PK, farm_id UUID, zone_id UUID FK, current_state ENUM open/closed/unknown, last_actuated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). moisture_sensors (id UUID PK, farm_id UUID, zone_id UUID FK, sensor_hw_id TEXT, moisture_pct SMALLINT, last_seen_at TIMESTAMPTZ). rainfall_gauges (id UUID PK, farm_id UUID, location_lat NUMERIC, location_lng NUMERIC, last_reading_mm NUMERIC, last_read_at TIMESTAMPTZ). hub_credentials (hub_id TEXT PK, farm_id UUID, hmac_secret TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). irrigation_schedules (id UUID PK, farm_id UUID, zone_id UUID FK, target_moisture_threshold SMALLINT, start_time TIME, duration_minutes SMALLINT, enabled BOOL). override_commands (id UUID PK, farm_id UUID, zone_id UUID FK, firebase_uid TEXT, action ENUM open/close, status ENUM sent/confirmed/failed, issued_at TIMESTAMPTZ). water_usage_logs (id UUID PK, farm_id UUID, zone_id UUID FK, volume_liters NUMERIC, period_start TIMESTAMPTZ, period_end TIMESTAMPTZ). All tables protected by PostgreSQL RLS policy using app.farm_id session variable set by Express middleware after Firebase JWT validation.
target_output.api_design: REST base /api/v1. GET /zones?farm_id returns zone list with current valve_state and last moisture reading. POST /zones/:id/override {action: open or close} triggers valve command, requires manager role Firebase claim. GET /zones/:id/readings?start&end returns moisture history. GET /schedules returns schedule list. PUT /schedules/:id upserts schedule rule. GET /reports/water-usage?start&end&zone_id returns aggregated volume. POST /ingest/:hub_id authenticated by HMAC-SHA256 (not Firebase) for sensor hub readings; publishes to Redis on success. WebSocket: zone_update {zone_id, moisture_pct, valve_state, rainfall_mm, ts} emitted to farm_{farm_id} Socket.IO room on each Redis pub/sub message.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Firebase Auth JWT validated on every Express route via middleware setting PostgreSQL app.farm_id session variable for RLS enforcement. HMAC-SHA256 hub authentication with 60-second timestamp replay window prevents hub impersonation; hub secrets stored in hub_credentials never returned via any API response. PostgreSQL RLS enforced at database session level; application query cannot bypass tenancy isolation even with SQL injection. HTTPS enforced on App Platform; WebSocket over WSS only. RBAC enforced via Firebase custom claims: manager and technician roles checked in Express middleware. override_commands table provides full audit trail of valve actuation including Firebase UID, zone, action, and timestamp.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: DigitalOcean App Platform: API service configured with 2 Basic instances (1 vCPU, 1 GB RAM each) with sticky session affinity enabled for Socket.IO client reconnect consistency. React build output deployed as static site on same App Platform project. Managed PostgreSQL 16 (primary plus hot standby, 1 GB RAM). Managed Redis 7 (1 GB). GitHub Actions CI: eslint, tsc --noEmit, jest with 80% coverage gate, Docker build and push to DO Container Registry, doctl app update on merge to main. node-pg-migrate runs automatically on API container startup for schema migrations. Rollback: re-deploy previous container image tag via doctl app update. Environments: dev (single API instance, local PostgreSQL and Redis via Docker Compose); production (2-instance App Platform with managed databases).
target_output.observability: Prometheus custom metrics exposed on /metrics by Express: socket_active_connections gauge per farm_id room, zone_update_events_total counter, valve_command_failures_total counter, sensor_hub_ingest_errors_total counter, schedule_engine_runs_total counter. Grafana dashboard showing live connections per farm, ingestion rate per hub, valve failure rate, and zone staleness count. DigitalOcean Logs receives structured JSON logs from Express via morgan with JSON formatter. Alert on valve_command_failures exceeding 3 in 5 minutes sends email to farm manager; alert on sensor hub last_seen_at stale beyond 15 minutes shows warning badge on zone card in dashboard.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: DigitalOcean App Platform 2 Basic instances ~$24/month; Managed PostgreSQL Basic ~$15/month; Managed Redis Basic ~$15/month; total ~$54/month well under $200 budget. Scale path at multi-farm growth: increase to 3 API instances with same Redis adapter for up to 500 concurrent WebSocket connections; add PostgreSQL read replica for water usage report queries without impacting transactional writes.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (4 weeks): sensor hub ingestion endpoint with HMAC-SHA256 auth, live zone dashboard with Socket.IO Redis adapter and sticky sessions, manual valve override with override_commands audit log, Firebase Auth JWT integration and PostgreSQL RLS tenancy isolation. Phase 2 (2 weeks): irrigation schedule editor and hourly scheduling engine, water usage report with CSV export, moisture alert rules with email notification via SendGrid. Phase 3 (2 weeks): multi-farm portal admin view, farm onboarding self-service flow, Grafana dashboard and PagerDuty alert rules, load test simulating 50 concurrent zones across 5 farms.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode for both React and Express. Jest unit tests for HMAC validation logic, RLS session variable setting middleware, and scheduling engine threshold evaluation. Cypress E2E for login, zone dashboard WebSocket connection, and valve override confirmation flow. PR requires passing CI and 1 review. node-pg-migrate migration files committed alongside code; never modify previously applied migration files. hub_credentials secrets never logged or returned in API responses.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: cellular sensor hub connectivity gaps cause stale zone readings; mitigation: last_seen_at staleness indicator on dashboard warns farm manager; scheduling engine skips zones with sensors stale beyond 15 minutes. Risk: Redis Managed instance failure breaks Socket.IO room broadcasts and zone state cache; mitigation: DigitalOcean Managed Redis has automated failover; Socket.IO degrades to HTTP long-polling if Redis adapter is unavailable. Tradeoff: HMAC-SHA256 over TLS client certificates for hub auth chosen for provisioning simplicity at farm scale. Tradeoff: hourly scheduling engine over event-driven for MVP simplicity; sub-minute scheduling addressable in phase 2.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: HMAC-SHA256 chosen over per-hub TLS client certificates for sensor hub authentication due to provisioning simplicity at scale. Socket.IO Redis adapter selected over managed WebSocket services to keep all infrastructure within DigitalOcean budget. Valve controller integration confirmed as REST API on the field hub accessed from backend over cellular. PostgreSQL RLS confirmed over application-layer tenancy filtering for defense-in-depth.

metadata.notes: Round 2 irrigation dashboard; resolves sensor hub ingestion path and Socket.IO multi-instance sticky session gaps.

project_goal.value: Desktop application for field engineers commissioning industrial IoT gateways on-site, providing device discovery via mDNS, API-driven parameter configuration, and one-click firmware OTA update workflows over local network
project_goal.rationale: Replace manual SSH-based commissioning with a guided GUI tool to reduce error rate and training time for field engineers

target_users.value: Field engineers and biomedical technicians commissioning and upgrading industrial IoT gateways on-site at customer premises
target_users.rationale: Primary operator persona; technically proficient but not software engineers

project_class.rationale: Runs on engineer laptop; no cloud hosting; local network only; requires native OS APIs for mDNS and file system access

capabilities.rationale: Tauri UI, Rust sidecar for mDNS and OTA, gateway REST API integration, custom JWT for session auth

complexity_level.rationale: mDNS discovery, OTA binary transfer, and device REST API integration are non-trivial but contained in scope

risk_level.rationale: Incorrect firmware flash or parameter misconfiguration could take a gateway offline at a customer site

data_sensitivity.rationale: Device credentials and commissioning sessions are internal engineering data not regulated

external_exposure.rationale: App operates entirely on local network; no cloud connectivity required during commissioning

access_model.value: Custom JWT issued at app login; engineer profiles stored locally in SQLite; no central auth server required on-site
access_model.rationale: App must function at customer sites without internet access; local auth is the only viable option

feature_scope.value: mDNS device discovery, device inventory list with firmware version, parameter configuration form, firmware OTA update with progress tracking, commissioning session log, engineer profile management
feature_scope.rationale: Complete on-site commissioning workflow from discovery through validation

mvp_scope.value: mDNS discovery, device list with firmware version display, parameter write via gateway REST API, OTA firmware push from local file
mvp_scope.rationale: Core commissioning actions needed for field pilot with 10 engineers

security_baseline.value: Custom JWT with 8-hour session expiry; firmware binaries verified against SHA-256 hash from vendor manifest before flashing; device API credentials stored encrypted in SQLite with AES-256; no credentials in logs
security_baseline.rationale: Prevent unauthorized device configuration and ensure firmware integrity before OTA push

frontend_stack.value: Tauri 2 with React 18 and TypeScript
frontend_stack.rationale: Tauri provides cross-platform desktop with native OS APIs; React for UI; Rust backend for mDNS and file operations

backend_stack.value: Rust with Axum embedded sidecar for mDNS, OTA transfer, and gateway REST proxy
backend_stack.rationale: Rust suits long-running background services; Axum for internal IPC between React UI and Rust sidecar

data_platform.value: SQLite via rusqlite for local device inventory, commissioning sessions, and engineer profiles
data_platform.rationale: Embedded DB; no server dependency; works offline at customer sites

hosting_target.value: Native desktop installer for Windows, macOS, and Linux distributed via internal GitLab package registry
hosting_target.rationale: Field engineers use diverse OS environments; GitLab registry provides controlled distribution with version tracking

privacy_retention_policy.value: Commissioning session logs retained locally for 90 days then auto-deleted; no session data uploaded to any cloud service
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Customer site data must not leave premises; 90-day local retention supports post-incident debugging

future_scope.value: Cloud sync of commissioning sessions to a central fleet management system; automated pre-commissioning checklist generation; barcode scanner integration for device identification
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 when internet connectivity at customer sites is more reliable

constraints.value: Must operate fully offline; must run on Windows 10 and 11, macOS 12+, Ubuntu 22.04; installer package under 50 MB; no runtime dependencies on customer laptop
constraints.rationale: Field engineers cannot install runtimes at customer sites; offline operation is mandatory

observability_baseline.value: Sentry crash reporting for unhandled panics and React error boundaries; structured JSON log file per commissioning session in local app data directory
observability_baseline.rationale: Crash reports help diagnose field issues without requiring live telemetry

execution_preference.value: Milestone-driven delivery with internal QA sign-off before each field pilot release
execution_preference.rationale: Field releases require sign-off to avoid breaking live customer deployments

compliance_context.value: SOC 2 controls for access credentials stored locally; internal firmware signing verification policy requiring SHA-256 manifest validation before OTA flash
compliance_context.rationale: SOC 2 CC6.1 for credential protection; internal supply chain integrity policy for firmware

project.goal: Desktop commissioning tool for IoT gateway field engineers

frontend.state: Zustand for device and session state

backend.api: Internal Tauri IPC; gateway REST proxy

security.auth: Custom JWT stored in system keychain via keyring-rs
security.transport: HTTPS to gateway REST API; local IPC over Unix socket
security.firmware_integrity: SHA-256 hash validated against vendor manifest before OTA

reasoner_reviews.product: The scope is tightly defined around the field commissioning workflow. The mDNS discovery to OTA update path is the critical flow and should be the focus of the MVP. Session logging is important for post-incident debugging at customer sites. The parameter configuration form will need dynamic rendering based on the gateway model's parameter schema.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Tauri 2 with Rust commands is the right architecture for a cross-platform desktop tool that needs native OS access for mDNS and local file operations. The Rust sidecar pattern using Axum for internal IPC allows the React UI to communicate with mDNS and OTA subsystems without Tauri API limitations. SQLite with rusqlite and refinery migrations is the correct embedded persistence choice for offline operation.
reasoner_reviews.security: Firmware integrity verification before OTA flash is the most critical security control. The SHA-256 hash must be fetched from the vendor firmware manifest over HTTPS before the engineer arrives on-site, not derived from the firmware file itself. Device API credentials must be stored in the OS keychain via keyring-rs, not in plaintext SQLite columns. Custom JWT session tokens should be signed with a key derived from the engineer profile password at login.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 50 MB installer constraint is achievable with Tauri 2 since the Rust binary is statically linked. Tauri bundles the WebView2 runtime on Windows for first-run installation which adds size; consider the NSIS installer with WebView2 bootstrapper. Cross-compilation in GitHub Actions using cross-rs targets Windows, macOS, and Linux from a single CI pipeline.

frontend.app_structure: Tauri 2 React SPA with page components: DeviceList, DeviceDetail (params + firmware), CommissioningSession, EngineerProfile
frontend.pages_and_flows: DeviceList shows discovered gateways with hostname, firmware version, and status badge (online/unreachable/update-available); DeviceDetail shows parameter form and OTA update panel; CommissioningSession shows real-time log of actions taken in the current session
frontend.state_management: Zustand stores: discoveredDevices (updated via Tauri event listener on mdns_discovery events), activeSession (commissioning steps, timestamps), engineerProfile (JWT and display name). Tauri event API used for OTA progress updates.
frontend.frontend_risks: Parameter form schema must be fetched from the gateway REST API on device selection; schema loading failures need graceful fallback to a generic key-value editor

backend.service_design: Rust Tauri commands: cmd_start_discovery (spawns mDNS resolver), cmd_fetch_device_params (proxies to gateway REST API), cmd_write_device_params (validates against schema then writes), cmd_push_ota (verifies SHA-256, chunks binary, streams to gateway OTA endpoint with progress events)
backend.api_patterns: Tauri IPC commands from React to Rust for all gateway interactions; gateway REST API accessed from Rust using reqwest; Tauri events emitted from Rust back to React for async progress (ota_progress, discovery_update)
backend.failure_handling: OTA transfer failure rolls back: gateway is instructed to abort and retain current firmware; Rust command returns structured error enum (NetworkError, HashMismatch, GatewayRejected) mapped to user-facing messages in React

security.auth_design: Engineer logs in with username and password stored in SQLite (password hashed with Argon2id); JWT signed with HMAC-SHA256 key derived from password hash; token stored in OS keychain via keyring-rs crate; 8-hour expiry
security.secrets_management: Device API credentials (username/password per gateway) stored encrypted in SQLite using AES-256-GCM with key derived from engineer JWT; decrypted in Rust at command invocation time only
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Each Tauri command invocation logged to commissioning_sessions with engineer_id, device_id, action, parameters (redacted), result, and timestamp; log file written to OS app data directory

data.entities: discovered_devices (id, hostname, ip_address, mac_address, firmware_version, model, last_seen_at, reachable BOOL), device_configurations (id, device_id, param_key, param_value_encrypted, written_at, engineer_id), firmware_packages (id, model, version, file_path, sha256_hash, downloaded_at), ota_update_jobs (id, device_id, firmware_id, status ENUM pending/in_progress/success/failed, progress_pct, started_at, completed_at), commissioning_sessions (id, engineer_id, device_id, action, result, log_json, created_at), engineer_profiles (id, username, password_hash, created_at, last_login_at)
data.storage_design: SQLite database in OS app data directory; rusqlite for queries; refinery for schema migrations on startup; WAL mode enabled for concurrent reads during OTA logging
data.retention_and_deletion: Commissioning sessions older than 90 days purged on app startup via DELETE WHERE created_at < now() - 90 days; firmware packages deleted from file system when ota_update_job is success and firmware_package.version < current device firmware

devops.deployment_topology: Tauri 2 bundler produces platform-specific installers: NSIS .exe (Windows), .dmg (macOS), .deb/.AppImage (Linux); distributed via internal GitLab package registry with version tagging
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions matrix: ubuntu-latest (Linux + cross-compile check), windows-latest (.exe bundle), macos-latest (.dmg bundle); cargo test for Rust unit tests; vitest for React components; tauri build with signing certs from GitHub Secrets; upload artifacts to GitLab package registry API
devops.rollback_strategy: Previous installer version pinned in GitLab registry; engineers can download and reinstall previous version; SQLite migrations use refinery which does not auto-rollback, so schema changes are additive only

target_output.thinking_summary: This is a cross-platform desktop commissioning tool with no cloud dependency. The key architectural decisions are Tauri 2 for native OS APIs without Electron's overhead, Rust commands for mDNS and OTA subsystems, and SQLite for embedded offline persistence. Firmware SHA-256 validation before OTA push is the critical security control given the risk of bricking gateways at customer sites. The 50 MB installer constraint guides the choice of Tauri over Electron.
target_output.title: IoT Gateway Field Commissioning Desktop Tool
target_output.executive_summary: Cross-platform Tauri 2 desktop application enabling field engineers to discover, configure, and OTA-update industrial IoT gateways on-site over local network without internet connectivity. Rust Tauri commands handle mDNS device discovery, parameter write via gateway REST API, and chunked firmware OTA with SHA-256 integrity verification. SQLite stores device inventory, commissioning sessions, and engineer profiles locally. Sentry captures crash reports from the field.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single-process Tauri 2 application with a React 18 TypeScript frontend rendered in a system WebView and a Rust backend exposing Tauri commands. The Rust backend uses the mdns-sd crate for multicast DNS service discovery on the local subnet, reqwest for proxying parameter reads and writes to the gateway REST API, and a custom chunked transfer loop for OTA firmware push with Tauri event progress callbacks. SQLite database in the OS app data directory (via rusqlite) stores discovered devices, commissioning sessions, firmware packages, and engineer profiles. The keyring-rs crate stores the engineer JWT and device API credentials in the OS keychain (Windows Credential Manager, macOS Keychain, libsecret on Linux), never in SQLite plaintext. refinery crate applies SQLite schema migrations on startup. Tauri bundler produces native installers under 50 MB via static Rust linking and exclusion of WebView2 from the macOS and Linux bundles.
target_output.technology_stack: Desktop Framework: Tauri 2.0 with WebView2 (Windows), WKWebView (macOS), WebKitGTK (Linux). Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, Zustand. Rust Backend: Axum 0.7 (internal IPC), reqwest 0.12 (gateway REST proxy), mdns-sd 0.10 (device discovery), keyring-rs 2.x (credential storage), rusqlite 0.31 with refinery for migrations, argon2 crate for password hashing. Firmware Integrity: SHA-256 from sha2 crate validated against vendor manifest. CI: GitHub Actions with tauri-action cross-platform build matrix. Crash Reporting: Sentry Rust SDK + Sentry React.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Device Discovery: mDNS scan populates discovered_devices list with hostname, IP, firmware version, model, and update-available badge computed from local firmware package cache. Parameter Configuration: DeviceDetail page fetches JSON parameter schema from gateway REST GET /api/v1/config/schema, renders a typed form (text, integer, enum, boolean fields), validates locally, then writes via PUT /api/v1/config with engineer JWT-derived session token. OTA Firmware Update: engineer selects a firmware package from local cache or downloads from vendor manifest URL; Rust command verifies SHA-256 against manifest hash, chunks binary into 4 KB blocks, streams to gateway POST /api/v1/ota/upload with progress events, polls GET /api/v1/ota/status until gateway reports reboot_complete. Commissioning Session Log: timestamped log of all actions in current session displayed in real time; exported as JSON on session close. Engineer Profile: local username/password login with Argon2id hashing; last 10 sessions displayed per engineer.
target_output.system_components: Tauri 2 Application Shell (WebView renderer + Rust process, single binary). mDNS Discovery Service (mdns-sd crate, browses _iot-gateway._tcp.local service type, emits discovery_update Tauri events on device appear/disappear). Gateway REST Proxy Commands (cmd_fetch_params, cmd_write_params using reqwest to gateway HTTP API). OTA Transfer Engine (Rust async task, SHA-256 pre-validation, 4 KB chunked POST, progress emitted as ota_progress Tauri events). SQLite Local Database (rusqlite, WAL mode, refinery migrations, path: {AppDataDir}/gateway-tool.db). OS Keychain Integration (keyring-rs, stores engineer JWT and per-device API credentials under service name gateway-commissioning-tool). Sentry Crash Reporter (Rust panic handler + React ErrorBoundary, sends to Sentry project with release version tag).
target_output.workflows: Device Discovery and Selection: engineer opens app and clicks Scan; Rust mDNS resolver browses _iot-gateway._tcp.local for 10 seconds and emits discovery_update events; Zustand discoveredDevices store updates; device rows show firmware version fetched from gateway GET /api/v1/info; badges show update-available when firmware_package table has a newer version for the device model. Firmware OTA Update: engineer selects gateway in device list; navigates to OTA tab; selects firmware package from local cache or inputs vendor manifest URL; Rust cmd_push_ota fetches manifest JSON over HTTPS (if online), validates SHA-256 of local binary against manifest hash, rejects if mismatch; on validation success chunks firmware and POSTs to gateway OTA endpoint in 4 KB blocks emitting ota_progress {pct, bytes_sent, total_bytes} events; React progress bar updates; on completion Rust polls gateway status endpoint until reboot_complete; commissioning_sessions row logged with action=ota_update, result=success and firmware versions.
target_output.data_model: discovered_devices (id INTEGER PK, hostname TEXT, ip_address TEXT, mac_address TEXT UNIQUE, firmware_version TEXT, model TEXT, last_seen_at TEXT, reachable INTEGER DEFAULT 1). device_configurations (id INTEGER PK, device_id INTEGER FK, param_key TEXT, param_value_encrypted BLOB, written_at TEXT, engineer_id INTEGER). firmware_packages (id INTEGER PK, model TEXT, version TEXT, file_path TEXT, sha256_hash TEXT, downloaded_at TEXT). ota_update_jobs (id INTEGER PK, device_id INTEGER FK, firmware_id INTEGER FK, status TEXT CHECK(status IN ('pending','in_progress','success','failed')), progress_pct INTEGER DEFAULT 0, started_at TEXT, completed_at TEXT). commissioning_sessions (id INTEGER PK, engineer_id INTEGER FK, device_id INTEGER FK, action TEXT, result TEXT, log_json TEXT, created_at TEXT). engineer_profiles (id INTEGER PK, username TEXT UNIQUE, password_hash TEXT, created_at TEXT, last_login_at TEXT). All TEXT datetime columns store ISO-8601 strings; SQLite in WAL mode with refinery migrations versioned in Rust source.
target_output.api_design: Tauri IPC Commands (invoked from React via invoke()): cmd_start_discovery() -> void, emits discovery_update events. cmd_fetch_device_params(device_id: i64) -> Vec<ParamEntry> with key, value, type, description. cmd_write_device_params(device_id: i64, params: HashMap<String, Value>) -> Result<(), CommissionError>. cmd_push_ota(device_id: i64, firmware_id: i64) -> Result<(), OtaError>, emits ota_progress events {pct: u8, bytes_sent: u64, total: u64}. cmd_login(username: String, password: String) -> Result<EngineerProfile, AuthError>. cmd_list_sessions(engineer_id: i64, limit: i64) -> Vec<SessionSummary>. Gateway REST API (consumed by Rust via reqwest): GET /api/v1/info (firmware version, model), GET /api/v1/config/schema (JSON parameter schema), PUT /api/v1/config (write parameters), POST /api/v1/ota/upload (chunked binary), GET /api/v1/ota/status (reboot progress).
target_output.security_and_compliance: SOC 2 CC6.1: device API credentials stored encrypted in SQLite with AES-256-GCM key derived from engineer JWT material; keyring-rs stores JWT in OS keychain preventing plaintext exposure on disk. Firmware supply chain integrity: SHA-256 hash fetched from vendor firmware manifest over HTTPS and validated against local binary before any OTA transfer begins; HashMismatch error displayed to engineer with abort. Engineer authentication: Argon2id password hashing (m=65536, t=3, p=4) stored in SQLite; 8-hour JWT with HMAC-SHA256 signing; forced re-login after expiry. Audit trail: all Tauri command invocations logged to commissioning_sessions with action, parameters (API credentials redacted), result, and engineer_id. Log files written to {AppDataDir}/logs/ directory; auto-purged after 90 days on startup.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Tauri bundler produces: NSIS .exe installer for Windows (WebView2 bootstrapper included for Windows 10 first install), signed .dmg for macOS (Apple Developer ID notarization), .deb and .AppImage for Ubuntu 22.04. GitHub Actions matrix: ubuntu-latest builds Linux targets; windows-latest builds Windows .exe; macos-latest builds macOS .dmg. Code signing certificates stored in GitHub Secrets (APPLE_CERTIFICATE, WINDOWS_PFX). Artifacts uploaded to internal GitLab package registry with semver tags via GitLab API from the Actions workflow. Engineers download from GitLab registry link distributed via internal wiki. SQLite refinery migrations applied automatically on app startup; migration files embedded in Rust binary via include_str!. Rollback by downloading previous version from GitLab registry and reinstalling; database schema is additive-only to support rollback without data loss.
target_output.observability: Sentry Rust SDK initialized with release version and environment tag; panics in Rust commands captured as Sentry events with device_id and action context. Sentry React SDK with ErrorBoundary on DeviceList and OTA panel components; frontend exceptions captured with component stack. Structured JSON session log written to {AppDataDir}/logs/session_{date}.json with one entry per Tauri command invocation; log level filter via GATEWAY_TOOL_LOG_LEVEL environment variable. Sentry performance tracing for cmd_push_ota transactions to measure OTA transfer duration per firmware size and gateway model.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: No cloud hosting cost; Sentry free tier covers crash reporting for internal field team of up to 50 engineers. GitLab package registry storage ~$0 on existing GitLab instance. Build CI minutes ~40 minutes per release across 3 OS matrix jobs. Scale path: if fleet grows to 500+ engineers, replace GitLab registry distribution with a self-hosted update server using Tauri updater plugin for automatic update notifications.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): mDNS device discovery, device list with firmware version, parameter read/write via gateway REST, local engineer login with Argon2id and JWT, SQLite schema with refinery migrations, GitHub Actions multi-platform CI producing signed installers. Phase 2 (3 weeks): OTA firmware update with SHA-256 validation, progress tracking via Tauri events, commissioning session log with export, device credential encryption in SQLite. Phase 3 (1 week): Sentry crash reporting integration, 90-day session log auto-purge, QA sign-off testing on all 3 OS targets with 5 gateway models.
target_output.development_guidelines: Rust: clippy and rustfmt in CI; unit tests for OTA SHA-256 validation, commissioning session logging, and Argon2id hash/verify functions using Rust test module. React: vitest for Zustand store reducers and form validation logic; no E2E tests until phase 2 (native WebView automation tooling limited). Tauri commands return typed Result enums; never return raw strings as error indicators. SQLite migrations are additive-only; never drop or rename columns in applied migrations. Device API credentials never written to log files; param_value is redacted to [REDACTED] before logging to commissioning_sessions.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: gateway REST API varies by manufacturer model; mitigation: parameter schema endpoint standardized in firmware spec; fallback to generic key-value editor when schema endpoint returns 404. Risk: OTA transfer interrupted by local network instability bricks gateway; mitigation: gateway OTA endpoint implements atomic swap and rollback on incomplete transfer; app detects timeout and instructs gateway to abort. Tradeoff: Tauri over Electron chosen for 50 MB installer constraint; WebView rendering differences across OS WebView versions require CSS cross-browser testing. Tradeoff: custom JWT over OS-level SSO for offline independence at customer sites without AD connectivity.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Tauri 2 selected over Electron due to 50 MB installer constraint and native Rust backend performance. keyring-rs selected over SQLite encryption for JWT storage to leverage OS-managed credential protection. SHA-256 fetched from vendor manifest over HTTPS before site visit rather than at OTA time to enable fully offline OTA on-site. refinery selected over diesel for SQLite migrations due to simpler embedded binary integration with Tauri builds.

metadata.notes: First-pass desktop commissioning tool for IoT gateway field engineers using Tauri 2 and Rust with offline-first SQLite and mDNS discovery.

project_goal.value: Automation tool managing the full CI/CD lifecycle for embedded firmware projects targeting ARM Cortex-M devices: cross-compilation, static analysis, hardware-in-the-loop tests on a physical dev board farm, and OTA release publishing
project_goal.rationale: Replace ad-hoc GitLab CI YAML scripts with a purpose-built pipeline orchestrator that manages physical board allocation and release signing

target_users.value: Embedded firmware engineers and DevOps engineers managing firmware build and release pipelines for ARM Cortex-M projects
target_users.rationale: Primary users who trigger builds and review test results

project_class.rationale: Pipeline orchestration tool running on bare-metal Kubernetes; no browser UI; exposes a webhook API for GitLab CI triggers

capabilities.rationale: Celery task queue for build and test jobs, GitLab CI integration, hardware board pool management, OTA package publishing

complexity_level.rationale: Physical board pool concurrency management, cross-compilation toolchain isolation, and cryptographic release signing are non-trivial

risk_level.rationale: Incorrect firmware signing or release channel publishing could push bad firmware to production devices

data_sensitivity.rationale: Build artifacts and signing keys are internal engineering assets; firmware binaries are proprietary

external_exposure.rationale: Pipeline API and artifact storage are on internal network; no public exposure

access_model.value: Keycloak OIDC for engineer web dashboard and API; GitLab webhook tokens for CI trigger authentication; signing key access restricted to release Celery workers via Vault policy
access_model.rationale: Separation of access: engineers see build status; CI triggers builds; only designated workers can access signing keys

feature_scope.value: Cross-compilation job with CMake and arm-none-eabi-gcc, cppcheck and clang-tidy static analysis, physical board allocation and flash-and-run HIL test execution, cryptographic OTA package signing, release channel publishing with staged rollout flags
feature_scope.rationale: Full firmware supply chain from source to signed release

mvp_scope.value: Cross-compilation and static analysis jobs, board pool allocation for HIL tests on 10 physical boards, OTA package generation from successful build artifacts
mvp_scope.rationale: Core build-test-package pipeline before adding signing and release channel management

security_baseline.value: Firmware signing with Ed25519 key stored in HashiCorp Vault; signing worker authenticated to Vault via Kubernetes service account; build artifacts stored in Minio with per-project access policies; static analysis findings gate release promotion
security_baseline.rationale: IEC 62443 firmware supply chain integrity requirements; internal signing policy

frontend_stack.value: none - automation tool; minimal status web UI is a future consideration only
frontend_stack.rationale: Automation tool exposes REST API and webhook integration; no browser UI in scope

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI for webhook API and status endpoints; Celery 5 with Redis broker for job queue
backend_stack.rationale: FastAPI for async webhook handling; Celery for distributed task execution across build and HIL worker pools

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for pipeline state and board pool inventory; Redis 7 as Celery broker and result backend
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL for durable pipeline records and board leases; Redis for Celery task routing

hosting_target.value: Bare-metal Kubernetes on on-premises cluster; physical dev boards connected to HIL worker nodes via USB/JTAG
hosting_target.rationale: Physical board connectivity requires on-premises hardware nodes; Kubernetes manages build and test workers

privacy_retention_policy.value: Build artifacts retained 90 days; static analysis reports retained 1 year; signed OTA release packages retained indefinitely for fleet rollback capability
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Signed releases must be retained for full fleet lifecycle; analysis reports for compliance audit

future_scope.value: Web dashboard for pipeline visualization; automated regression detection comparing static analysis findings across firmware versions; multi-architecture support for RISC-V targets
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 improvements after ARM Cortex-M pipeline is stable

constraints.value: Physical board pool limited to 10 dev boards initially; cross-compilation must be hermetic (Docker-isolated toolchain); Keycloak already deployed on internal network; HashiCorp Vault already deployed
constraints.rationale: Board pool size determines HIL test throughput; Docker isolation prevents toolchain version conflicts across projects

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for job queue depth, board utilization, and build duration; GitLab CI integration for build status badges; structured logging via structlog
observability_baseline.rationale: Pipeline visibility for engineers; board utilization drives capacity planning

execution_preference.value: Iterative delivery with weekly internal dogfooding on active firmware projects; GitLab CI integration tested on at least 2 real firmware repos before broader rollout
execution_preference.rationale: Validate against real workloads early to catch toolchain edge cases

project.goal: Automated firmware CI/CD pipeline orchestrator for ARM Cortex-M projects with HIL board testing
project.target_users: Embedded firmware engineers and DevOps engineers

backend.api: REST webhook and status API

security.auth: Keycloak OIDC for engineers; GitLab webhook token for CI triggers
security.artifacts: Minio object storage with per-project policies

reasoner_reviews.product: The pipeline scope is well-defined. The board pool allocation is the most operationally critical component -- a leaked board lease would starve concurrent builds. The static analysis gate before release promotion is the right quality control mechanism. Signed OTA packages must carry a version manifest so the fleet update service can validate compatibility before flashing.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Celery with separate worker queues per job type is the correct architecture. Build workers run in Docker-isolated containers to hermetically contain the arm-none-eabi toolchain. HIL workers must run on Kubernetes nodes with USB/JTAG device host paths mounted via nodeSelector. The board pool allocation must use database-level locking (SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED) to prevent double-allocation under concurrent Celery task execution.
reasoner_reviews.security: HashiCorp Vault integration via Kubernetes auth method (pod service account JWT) is the correct approach for signing key access. The signing Celery worker should run in a separate, network-isolated worker pool with a Vault policy granting transit/sign access only to the firmware-signing key. Build workers must not have Vault access. Static analysis findings with severity=error must gate the signing step.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Ten physical boards with 10-minute average HIL test duration yields a throughput of ~6 parallel builds per board per hour. Board queue depth alert at > 20 queued tests is a reasonable SLA signal. Docker build cache for arm-none-eabi toolchain layers must be stored in the on-premises Docker registry to avoid pulling on every build.

backend.service_design: FastAPI app handling webhook POST /pipeline/trigger (validates GitLab token, creates firmware_builds record, enqueues build Celery task). Separate Celery worker pools: build-workers (Docker-in-Docker with arm-none-eabi), hil-workers (nodeSelector: board-node=true, USB hostPath), signing-workers (Vault service account, isolated network policy).
backend.business_modules: Board pool manager using PostgreSQL SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED to atomically lease test_board_pool entries; lease released on HIL task completion or timeout (15-minute TTL). Build artifact manager pushing ELF and binary to Minio. Release channel publisher writing OTA bundle metadata to release_channels table.
backend.background_jobs: build_firmware_task (Docker run with CMake + arm-none-eabi-gcc, publishes ELF to Minio, triggers analysis task). run_static_analysis_task (cppcheck and clang-tidy in Docker, writes static_analysis_reports to DB). flash_and_run_hil_task (allocates board lease, flashes via OpenOCD, runs Unity test suite via serial, collects JUnit XML, releases board). sign_and_package_ota_task (fetches ELF from Minio, calls Vault transit/sign, generates OTA bundle with version manifest, publishes to release_channels).
backend.failure_handling: Build failure: updates firmware_builds.status=failed, posts GitLab commit status via API. HIL task timeout: releases board lease, retries once on different board. Vault unavailable: signing task fails with retry_countdown=300; PagerDuty alert after 3 consecutive failures.

security.auth_design: FastAPI webhook endpoint validates X-Gitlab-Token header against HMAC-SHA256 of payload using per-project webhook secret from Vault. Engineer status API uses Keycloak OIDC Bearer tokens validated via python-jose.
security.secrets_management: Vault Kubernetes auth method: signing worker pod's service account JWT exchanged for Vault token with policy firmware-signing (transit/sign on key firmware-ed25519 only). Minio credentials from Kubernetes Secrets projected into worker pods.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: All Celery task state transitions logged to firmware_builds and hardware_test_runs tables with worker_id, timestamps, and artifact paths. Vault audit log records every transit/sign operation with pod name and firmware_build_id.

data.entities: firmware_builds (id, project_id, commit_sha, branch, status ENUM queued/building/analysis/hil_testing/signing/published/failed, build_artifact_path, elf_sha256, started_at, completed_at), test_board_pool (id, board_model, board_serial, usb_path, worker_node, leased_by_task_id, leased_at, available BOOL), hardware_test_runs (id, firmware_build_id, board_id, junit_xml_path, pass_count, fail_count, duration_seconds, started_at, completed_at), static_analysis_reports (id, firmware_build_id, tool ENUM cppcheck/clang_tidy, finding_count, error_count, warning_count, report_path, created_at), ota_packages (id, firmware_build_id, version, package_path, signature_hex, manifest_json, published_to_channel, published_at), release_channels (id, name TEXT, staged_pct INT, ota_package_id FK, created_at)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 for all pipeline state; Redis 7 for Celery broker and result backend; Minio for ELF binaries, OTA packages, cppcheck XML reports, and JUnit XML results
data.retention_and_deletion: firmware_builds and artifacts purged after 90 days via pg_cron scheduled DELETE; static_analysis_reports retained 1 year; ota_packages retained indefinitely in Minio with versioned bucket

devops.deployment_topology: Bare-metal Kubernetes: API pod (FastAPI, 2 replicas), Celery build-worker pods (Docker-in-Docker, 4 replicas), Celery hil-worker pods (nodeSelector board-node=true, hostPath USB mounts, 2 pods per board node), Celery signing-worker pod (isolated NetworkPolicy, 1 replica). PostgreSQL and Redis as StatefulSets.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI .gitlab-ci.yml triggers POST to pipeline webhook on push; GitHub Actions used for the automation tool's own Python unit tests and Docker image builds; images pushed to internal Harbor registry.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus: celery_task_queue_depth gauge per queue, board_pool_available_count gauge, build_duration_seconds histogram, hil_test_pass_rate gauge. Grafana dashboard with board utilization heatmap and queue depth trends.

target_output.thinking_summary: This automation tool orchestrates a physical hardware testing pipeline with a board pool that must support concurrent job scheduling. Key decisions: Celery with separate worker pools per job type isolates the arm-none-eabi toolchain in Docker from the board-attached HIL workers; PostgreSQL SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED for atomic board lease allocation prevents double-booking; HashiCorp Vault Kubernetes auth for signing worker access to the Ed25519 key without static credential distribution. The OTA package must carry a signed version manifest for fleet-side compatibility validation.
target_output.title: Embedded Firmware CI/CD Pipeline Automation Tool
target_output.executive_summary: Python 3.12 automation tool orchestrating cross-compilation, static analysis, hardware-in-the-loop testing on a physical ARM Cortex-M dev board farm, and cryptographically signed OTA release publishing for embedded firmware projects. FastAPI handles GitLab CI webhook triggers. Celery 5 with Redis broker distributes jobs across three specialized worker pools: Docker-isolated build workers, board-node-pinned HIL workers, and Vault-authenticated signing workers. PostgreSQL manages pipeline state and board pool leases with SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED for concurrency-safe allocation.
target_output.architecture_overview: Event-driven pipeline architecture triggered by GitLab CI webhooks to a FastAPI endpoint. On trigger, a firmware_builds record is created and a build_firmware_task is enqueued to the build-workers Celery queue. Build workers run arm-none-eabi-gcc and CMake in a Docker container, publish the ELF binary to Minio, then chain to run_static_analysis_task. If cppcheck and clang-tidy report zero error-severity findings, flash_and_run_hil_task is enqueued to the hil-workers queue. HIL workers run on Kubernetes nodes with USB/JTAG hostPath mounts, atomically acquire a board lease via PostgreSQL SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED, flash firmware via OpenOCD, execute the Unity test suite over serial, parse JUnit XML results, and release the board lease. On HIL pass, sign_and_package_ota_task runs on the isolated signing-workers pool, fetches the ELF from Minio, requests an Ed25519 signature from HashiCorp Vault transit engine, packages the binary with a version manifest JSON, and publishes to the designated release channel in PostgreSQL and Minio.
target_output.technology_stack: API: Python 3.12, FastAPI 0.115, uvicorn, python-jose for Keycloak JWT validation. Task Queue: Celery 5.4, Redis 7 broker and result backend, kombu for routing. Build Toolchain: arm-none-eabi-gcc 13, CMake 3.28, cppcheck 2.14, clang-tidy 18, running in Docker containers from internal Harbor registry. HIL Testing: OpenOCD 0.12 for flash via JTAG/SWD, Unity test framework on device, pyserial for serial capture. Signing: HashiCorp Vault 1.16 transit engine with Ed25519 key, hvac Python client with Kubernetes auth. Storage: PostgreSQL 16, Minio (S3-compatible) for artifacts. Infrastructure: bare-metal Kubernetes, GitLab CI, Harbor container registry. Observability: Grafana + Prometheus, structlog.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Webhook Trigger: GitLab CI posts to POST /pipeline/trigger with project_id, commit_sha, branch; FastAPI validates HMAC-SHA256 webhook secret, creates firmware_builds record, enqueues build_firmware_task. Cross-Compilation: Docker container runs cmake --build in isolated workspace, captures compiler warnings as structured JSON, publishes ELF and binary to Minio at builds/{project_id}/{commit_sha}. Static Analysis: cppcheck and clang-tidy run against source tree in Docker; findings written to static_analysis_reports; error_count > 0 blocks pipeline promotion. Board Allocation and HIL Test: board pool manager acquires available test_board_pool row with SKIP LOCKED; OpenOCD flashes binary via SWD; Unity tests execute; JUnit XML parsed; pass/fail written to hardware_test_runs; board lease released. OTA Signing and Publishing: Vault transit/sign called with firmware SHA-256 as payload; Ed25519 signature encoded as hex; OTA bundle (binary + version manifest JSON + signature) written to ota_packages and Minio releases bucket. Release Channel Management: ota_packages associated with release_channels rows with staged_pct for gradual rollout; fleet update agents query GET /channels/{channel}/latest.
target_output.system_components: FastAPI Application (2 replicas, handles webhook triggers and status queries, validates GitLab tokens and Keycloak JWTs). Celery Build Workers (4 replicas, Docker-in-Docker, arm-none-eabi toolchain from internal Harbor image, Minio artifact upload). Celery HIL Workers (2 pods per board-node Kubernetes node, nodeSelector board-node=true, USB hostPath mount for OpenOCD, board lease management via PostgreSQL). Celery Signing Workers (1 replica, isolated NetworkPolicy allowing only egress to Vault and Minio, hvac Vault Kubernetes auth). PostgreSQL 16 StatefulSet (pipeline state, board pool inventory, release channels). Redis 7 StatefulSet (Celery broker, result backend). Minio StatefulSet (ELF binaries, OTA packages, analysis reports). HashiCorp Vault (existing deployment, firmware-ed25519 transit key, signing worker Vault policy). Harbor Registry (internal, stores build toolchain Docker images).
target_output.workflows: Build and Analysis Pipeline: GitLab CI webhook triggers FastAPI POST /pipeline/trigger; firmware_builds record created with status=queued; build_firmware_task enqueued to build-workers queue; worker pulls arm-none-eabi Docker image from Harbor, runs cmake --preset arm-cortex-m --config Release, captures ELF output, computes SHA-256, uploads to Minio, updates firmware_builds.status=analysis; run_static_analysis_task enqueued; Docker container runs cppcheck --xml and clang-tidy on source, writes static_analysis_reports row; if error_count > 0 pipeline status=failed and GitLab commit status updated via API; on pass status=hil_testing. Hardware-in-the-Loop Test: flash_and_run_hil_task attempts SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED on test_board_pool WHERE available=true; on lease acquired updates leased_by_task_id; OpenOCD invoked to flash binary via SWD; serial port opened, Unity test output captured until 'TEST COMPLETE' sentinel; JUnit XML parsed, hardware_test_runs row written; board lease released (available=true, leased_by=null); on all-pass status=signing, on failure status=failed with board_id logged. OTA Signing: sign_and_package_ota_task downloads ELF from Minio; builds version manifest JSON with firmware_version, target_arch, min_bootloader_version, sha256; calls hvac Vault transit sign with Ed25519 key; creates OTA bundle tarball; uploads to Minio releases bucket; writes ota_packages row with signature_hex and manifest_json; posts commit status=success to GitLab.
target_output.data_model: firmware_builds (id UUID PK, project_id TEXT, commit_sha TEXT, branch TEXT, status TEXT CHECK IN ('queued','building','analysis','hil_testing','signing','published','failed'), build_artifact_path TEXT, elf_sha256 TEXT, error_message TEXT, started_at TIMESTAMPTZ, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). test_board_pool (id UUID PK, board_model TEXT, board_serial TEXT UNIQUE, usb_path TEXT, worker_node TEXT, leased_by_task_id TEXT, leased_at TIMESTAMPTZ, available BOOL DEFAULT true). hardware_test_runs (id UUID PK, firmware_build_id UUID FK, board_id UUID FK, junit_xml_path TEXT, pass_count INT, fail_count INT, duration_seconds NUMERIC, started_at TIMESTAMPTZ, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). static_analysis_reports (id UUID PK, firmware_build_id UUID FK, tool TEXT CHECK IN ('cppcheck','clang_tidy'), finding_count INT, error_count INT, warning_count INT, report_path TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). ota_packages (id UUID PK, firmware_build_id UUID FK, version TEXT, package_path TEXT, signature_hex TEXT, manifest_json JSONB, published_to_channel TEXT, published_at TIMESTAMPTZ). release_channels (id UUID PK, name TEXT UNIQUE, staged_pct SMALLINT DEFAULT 0, ota_package_id UUID FK, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: POST /pipeline/trigger {project_id, commit_sha, branch, gitlab_token} -- validates HMAC-SHA256 webhook token, creates firmware_builds, returns {build_id, status}. GET /builds/{build_id} -- returns firmware_builds record with status, static analysis findings count, HIL pass/fail. GET /builds/{build_id}/hil-results -- returns hardware_test_runs with pass_count, fail_count, board_id. GET /channels/{channel}/latest -- returns latest published ota_packages record for the channel (queried by fleet update agents). POST /channels/{channel}/promote {ota_package_id, staged_pct} -- updates release channel staged rollout percentage; requires Keycloak manager role. GET /boards -- returns test_board_pool availability status; requires Keycloak engineer role.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Webhook authentication: X-Gitlab-Token HMAC-SHA256 validated per-project using secrets stored in Vault KV at kv/projects/{project_id}/webhook_secret. Keycloak OIDC Bearer token required for all non-webhook endpoints; engineer and manager roles enforced in FastAPI dependencies. Vault Kubernetes auth: signing Celery worker pod service account exchanged for Vault token with policy permitting only vault transit/sign on firmware-ed25519 key; build and HIL workers have no Vault access. Minio access: per-project Minio policies enforced via IAM rules; build workers have write access to builds/ prefix only; signing workers have write access to releases/ prefix. Static analysis gate: firmware_builds cannot transition to signing status if static_analysis_reports.error_count > 0 for any tool; enforced in Celery task state machine.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Bare-metal Kubernetes cluster: API Deployment (2 replicas, resource request 0.5 CPU / 512 MB). Build worker Deployment (4 replicas, Docker-in-Docker privileged containers, arm-none-eabi toolchain image from Harbor, resource request 2 CPU / 2 GB). HIL worker DaemonSet (board-node-labeled nodes only, nodeSelector board-node=true, hostPath /dev/bus/usb for OpenOCD, 1 pod per node, resource request 1 CPU / 1 GB). Signing worker Deployment (1 replica, NetworkPolicy deny-all ingress and egress except Vault ClusterIP and Minio ClusterIP). PostgreSQL StatefulSet with PVC. Redis StatefulSet with PVC. GitLab CI pipeline for the automation tool itself: Python unit tests with pytest, Docker build and push to Harbor, Helm chart deployment to Kubernetes via Helm upgrade. Rollback: Helm rollback to previous release; board pool lease TTL (15 min) auto-releases stale leases on worker crash.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics via prometheus-client in Celery workers: celery_task_queue_depth{queue} gauge (build/hil/signing queues), board_pool_available_count gauge, build_duration_seconds histogram per project, hil_test_pass_rate gauge per board_model. Grafana dashboard with board utilization heatmap (busy vs available per board over 24 hours), queue depth trend, and static analysis error rate per project. structlog structured JSON logs for all Celery task transitions (task_id, firmware_build_id, status, duration). GitLab commit status API updated on pipeline terminal states (success/failure) so engineers see build status in MR views. PagerDuty alert: signing task failure after 3 retries and board_pool_available_count < 1 for > 30 minutes.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: On-premises hardware; primary costs are board farm expansion and Kubernetes node compute. 10 boards at average 10-minute HIL test duration supports ~60 HIL tests/hour. At 4 build workers with 3-minute build time supports ~80 builds/hour, keeping build queue depth low relative to HIL queue. Scale path: add board-node-labeled Kubernetes nodes with additional dev boards; PostgreSQL board pool table scales to hundreds of boards with no schema changes; Celery hil-workers DaemonSet automatically pods onto new board nodes.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): webhook trigger to FastAPI, build_firmware_task with Docker arm-none-eabi toolchain, run_static_analysis_task with cppcheck and clang-tidy, PostgreSQL schema with board pool, GitLab commit status integration. Phase 2 (4 weeks): HIL worker deployment on board-node Kubernetes nodes, flash_and_run_hil_task with OpenOCD and Unity serial capture, board lease allocation with SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED, hardware_test_runs results recording. Phase 3 (2 weeks): Vault Kubernetes auth for signing workers, sign_and_package_ota_task with Ed25519 signing, OTA bundle generation with version manifest, release channel API, Grafana dashboard and PagerDuty alerts.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python: pytest with 80% coverage gate on board pool lease logic, static analysis gate state machine, and Vault signing task; mypy strict mode; ruff for linting and formatting. Celery tasks: all tasks are idempotent; firmware_build_id passed as task argument to enable deduplication. PostgreSQL migrations via Alembic with migration files committed alongside code. Docker toolchain image version pinned in Helm values; never use latest tag for arm-none-eabi toolchain image. Vault transit key rotation handled via Vault policy; automation tool does not manage key lifecycle.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: all 10 boards occupied when a high-priority build needs HIL testing; mitigation: priority queue routing in Celery hil-workers queue; board_pool_available_count < 1 PagerDuty alert prompts ops to evaluate board farm expansion. Risk: OpenOCD version incompatibility with a new board revision bricks the device during flash; mitigation: board model and OpenOCD config version pinned per test_board_pool entry; tested in staging-cluster before production board node update. Tradeoff: Docker-in-Docker for build workers adds layer of container privilege overhead vs. building on host; chosen for toolchain version isolation across concurrent firmware projects. Tradeoff: Celery over a GitLab CI custom runner for HIL tests; chosen to enable database-level board pool concurrency control that GitLab CI cannot express.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED chosen over advisory locks for board pool allocation for atomicity and automatic release on PostgreSQL session disconnect. Ed25519 over RSA for OTA signing chosen for smaller signature size fitting constrained bootloader flash budgets. Vault transit engine over AWS KMS chosen since the deployment is on-premises with no cloud dependency. HIL test serial output parsing via regex on Unity output format rather than custom protocol to reuse existing device test code.

metadata.notes: First-pass firmware CI/CD automation tool with physical board pool HIL testing, Vault-based Ed25519 signing, and Celery multi-pool job routing.

project_goal.value: Mobile application for healthcare facilities enabling nurses and biomedical technicians to monitor connected medical device telemetry (infusion pump flow rates, ventilator settings, patient monitor vitals) from a nursing station tablet or personal phone
project_goal.rationale: Replace bedside physical monitoring rounds with a real-time mobile view of all devices in a care unit, improving response time to device alarms

target_users.value: Nurses, charge nurses, and biomedical technicians in ICU, PCU, and general ward care units at hospital facilities
target_users.rationale: Primary users who need real-time device visibility and alarm acknowledgment capability away from the nursing station

project_class.rationale: Flutter mobile app for iOS and Android tablets and phones; real-time push notifications for alarms

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, realtime, integrations, analytics
capabilities.rationale: Flutter UI, Spring Boot backend, HL7 FHIR device integration, WebSocket telemetry, Keycloak auth, alarm analytics

complexity_level.rationale: HL7 FHIR integration, real-time WebSocket telemetry across hundreds of devices, HIPAA PHI controls, and IEC 80001 network safety requirements

risk_level.rationale: Delayed or missed device alarms could cause patient harm; PHI exposure violates HIPAA

data_sensitivity.rationale: Device telemetry includes patient vitals, medication flow rates, and ventilator settings linked to patient records

external_exposure.rationale: Keycloak authentication required for all app access; accessible only on hospital network or approved VPN

access_model.value: Keycloak OIDC with hospital AD federation; RBAC roles nurse, charge_nurse, biomedical_tech; care unit scoping enforced at API level; MFA for biomedical_tech role
access_model.rationale: HIPAA minimum necessary access; staff see only devices in their assigned care unit

feature_scope.value: Care unit device dashboard with live telemetry, per-device detail view with trend charts, alarm event list with acknowledgment workflow, push notification for critical alarms, biomedical tech device configuration view, alarm response time analytics
feature_scope.rationale: Complete clinical device monitoring workflow from alarm detection through acknowledgment reporting

mvp_scope.value: Real-time telemetry dashboard for one care unit, alarm push notifications with acknowledgment, device detail view with 1-hour trend charts, nurse and charge nurse roles
mvp_scope.rationale: Single care unit pilot to validate alarm workflow before hospital-wide rollout

security_baseline.value: Keycloak MFA for biomedical_tech; TLS 1.3 for all API and WebSocket traffic; PHI encrypted at rest with AES-256 via RDS encryption; HIPAA audit log for every device telemetry access; 15-minute session timeout on mobile app; no PHI in push notification payload
security_baseline.rationale: HIPAA minimum security controls; IEC 80001 network security requirements for medical device integration

frontend_stack.value: Flutter 3.22 with Dart 3
frontend_stack.rationale: Single codebase for iOS and Android tablets and phones; Flutter charts library for real-time trend rendering

backend_stack.value: Java 21 with Spring Boot 3.3 and Spring WebFlux for reactive WebSocket streaming
backend_stack.rationale: Spring Boot for HL7 FHIR integration libraries; WebFlux for non-blocking reactive streams to support hundreds of concurrent device subscriptions

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for device assignments, alarm events, and audit logs; Redis 7 for real-time device telemetry cache and WebSocket pub/sub
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL for durable clinical records; Redis for low-latency telemetry fan-out to connected mobile clients

hosting_target.value: AWS GovCloud EKS with Spring Boot backend on EKS pods; PostgreSQL on RDS; Redis on ElastiCache
hosting_target.rationale: AWS GovCloud for HIPAA-eligible services; EKS for containerized Spring Boot workloads

privacy_retention_policy.value: Device telemetry retained 7 years per HIPAA medical record retention; alarm events and acknowledgment logs retained 7 years; audit logs retained 7 years; data retained in us-gov-east-1 only
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: HIPAA 7-year medical record retention; US federal healthcare data residency requirement

future_scope.value: Predictive alarm fatigue analytics identifying devices with high false-alarm rates; integration with nurse call system for automatic escalation; wearable nurse badge proximity-based alarm routing
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 clinical workflow intelligence after MVP adoption

constraints.value: Must integrate with existing HL7 FHIR R4 device observation API at each hospital; mobile app must function on hospital Wi-Fi with intermittent signal in RF-shielded ICU rooms; iOS and Android tablet support required
constraints.rationale: Existing hospital HIS/EHR system exposes HL7 FHIR; RF shielding causes connectivity gaps

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM for Spring Boot; structured JSON logs in CloudWatch; PagerDuty for alarm delivery SLA breaches (critical alarm push notification not delivered within 30 seconds)
observability_baseline.rationale: Alarm delivery SLA is clinically critical; 30-second breach must page on-call engineer

execution_preference.value: Agile sprints with clinical stakeholder review at end of each sprint; HIPAA security review before each environment promotion
execution_preference.rationale: Clinical workflow changes require nurse stakeholder sign-off; HIPAA controls must be reviewed before production

compliance_context.value: HIPAA Privacy and Security Rules; HITECH for breach notification; FDA 21 CFR Part 11 for electronic records integrity; IEC 80001-1 for medical device network integration risk management
compliance_context.rationale: PHI in device telemetry requires HIPAA; electronic alarm acknowledgment records require 21 CFR Part 11; network integration with medical devices requires IEC 80001

project.goal: Mobile clinical device telemetry monitoring with real-time alarms
project.timeline: MVP single care unit in 14 weeks

frontend.state: Riverpod for reactive state, web_socket_channel for WebSocket

backend.api: REST + WebSocket reactive streams

security.auth: Keycloak OIDC with hospital AD federation

constraints.fhir: HL7 FHIR R4 device observation API
constraints.connectivity: hospital Wi-Fi with intermittent RF shielding

reasoner_reviews.product: The alarm acknowledgment workflow is the most clinically critical path. Push notifications must not contain PHI per HIPAA, so the notification payload should be a generic alert that triggers the app to fetch device details on open. The care unit scoping for nurse access is essential to prevent information overload and align with minimum necessary access. Alarm response time analytics will be important for the hospital's Joint Commission accreditation reporting.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Spring WebFlux with reactive WebSocket streams is the right choice for non-blocking fan-out to hundreds of concurrent mobile clients. The HL7 FHIR integration layer should use HAPI FHIR client for parsing device observation resources; a dedicated FHIR integration service decoupled from the main Spring Boot app allows hospital-specific FHIR endpoint configuration without touching core logic. Redis pub/sub for telemetry fan-out is standard for WebSocket horizontal scaling.
reasoner_reviews.security: PHI must never appear in push notification payloads -- APNs and FCM notifications should carry only a device_id and alarm_event_id so the app fetches PHI after Keycloak authentication. HIPAA audit log must record every device telemetry view with nurse_id, device_id, care_unit, and timestamp. 21 CFR Part 11 requires that alarm acknowledgment records be tamper-evident; an append-only audit table with a hash chain or immutable write pattern is required.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Flutter web_socket_channel must implement exponential backoff reconnection for ICU RF shielding gaps. The app must cache the last 5 minutes of telemetry locally in Hive (Flutter local DB) so nurses see recent data during a connectivity gap, with a visual staleness indicator.

frontend.app_structure: Flutter app with Riverpod providers for auth, care unit device list, and individual device telemetry streams. Pages: CareUnitDashboard, DeviceDetail (with fl_chart trend charts), AlarmEventList, AlarmDetail with acknowledge action, ProfileAndSettings
frontend.pages_and_flows: CareUnitDashboard shows a card grid of medical_devices in the nurse assigned care unit with live telemetry badges (SpO2, heart rate, pump flow rate, ventilator PEEP); cards pulse red on alarm_event. DeviceDetail shows a 1-hour fl_chart sparkline for each telemetry parameter and the last 10 alarm_events. AlarmDetail shows device, patient bed, alarm type, severity, and an Acknowledge button that calls POST /alarms/{id}/acknowledge.
frontend.state_management: Riverpod: authNotifierProvider (Keycloak token refresh), careUnitDevicesProvider (REST fetch on load), deviceTelemetryStreamProvider (WebSocket subscription per device, updates from Redis pub/sub). Hive local storage caches last 5 minutes of telemetry per device for offline display during Wi-Fi gaps.
frontend.frontend_risks: PHI must not be stored in Flutter app cache beyond session; Hive cache cleared on app backgrounding beyond 15 minutes

backend.service_design: Spring Boot 3.3 with three modules: REST API (device assignments, alarm CRUD, acknowledgment, analytics), WebSocket reactive streams (Spring WebFlux, Redis pub/sub subscriber per care unit room), FHIR Integration Service (HAPI FHIR R4 client polling hospital FHIR endpoints, normalizing DeviceObservation resources to internal telemetry schema).
backend.api_patterns: REST for device list, alarm events, acknowledgment, and analytics. WebSocket (STOMP over SockJS) for real-time telemetry push keyed by care_unit_id. HL7 FHIR R4 DeviceObservation polling every 10 seconds from hospital HIS.
backend.failure_handling: FHIR polling failures log to alarm_events with source_unavailable type and trigger push notification to charge nurse. WebSocket disconnect with no reconnect after 30 seconds triggers Redis TTL expiry of device telemetry cache and stale indicator push to app.

security.auth_design: Keycloak OIDC Bearer token validated by Spring Security on every REST endpoint and WebSocket handshake. Hospital Active Directory federated via Keycloak LDAP provider. Keycloak MFA policy enforced for biomedical_tech realm role.
security.authorization_model: Spring Security method-level @PreAuthorize: nurses see only devices in their assigned care_unit_id claim; charge_nurse sees all units; biomedical_tech has read-only device configuration access. Care unit claim injected into Keycloak token from AD group membership.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: HIPAA audit service: Spring AOP aspect intercepts all controller methods returning PHI and writes to device_telemetry_access_audit (nurse_id, device_id, patient_assignment_id, care_unit, access_type, timestamp). 21 CFR Part 11 alarm acknowledgment: alarm_acknowledgments table with row hash (SHA-256 of alarm_id + nurse_id + acknowledged_at) for tamper evidence.

data.entities: medical_devices (id, care_unit_id, device_type ENUM infusion_pump/ventilator/patient_monitor, device_model, serial_number, fhir_device_id, status ENUM active/offline/maintenance), device_telemetry (id, device_id, observation_type, value NUMERIC, unit TEXT, fhir_observation_id, recorded_at TIMESTAMPTZ), patient_assignments (id, patient_mrn_encrypted, device_id, care_unit_id, assigned_at, discharged_at), alarm_events (id, device_id, care_unit_id, alarm_type, severity ENUM critical/high/medium/low, message TEXT, acknowledged BOOL, fired_at TIMESTAMPTZ), alarm_acknowledgments (id, alarm_event_id, nurse_id, acknowledged_at TIMESTAMPTZ, row_hash TEXT), device_telemetry_access_audit (id, nurse_id, device_id, patient_assignment_id, care_unit, access_type, timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ), nursing_staff (id, keycloak_sub, display_name, role, care_unit_id, active BOOL)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ with AES-256 at-rest encryption; RDS IAM authentication for Spring Boot datasource. Redis 7 ElastiCache for WebSocket pub/sub (channel per care_unit_id) and 5-minute device telemetry cache (key device:{id}:latest).
data.retention_and_deletion: device_telemetry retained 7 years via pg_partman monthly partitions with S3 Glacier archival after 2 years. alarm_events and alarm_acknowledgments retained 7 years. patient_mrn_encrypted decryptable only via KMS key with HIPAA policy condition.

devops.deployment_topology: AWS GovCloud us-gov-east-1: EKS cluster running Spring Boot API pods (3 replicas), FHIR Integration Service pod (1 replica per hospital endpoint). RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ. ElastiCache Redis cluster. ECR for container images. ALB with WAF for API ingress.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI: Java unit tests with JUnit 5, integration tests with Testcontainers (PostgreSQL + Redis), Docker build and push to ECR, Helm chart deploy to EKS via kubectl. Flutter CI: flutter analyze, flutter test, flutter build ios/apk for distribution. HIPAA security review gate before staging promotion.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM Java agent on Spring Boot pods; custom metrics: alarm_delivery_latency_seconds histogram, active_websocket_connections_gauge per care_unit, fhir_poll_failures_total. PagerDuty alert when P99 alarm_delivery_latency > 30 seconds.

target_output.thinking_summary: This is a HIPAA-regulated clinical IoT mobile application where alarm delivery latency is a patient safety concern. Key decisions: Spring WebFlux reactive WebSocket with Redis pub/sub for non-blocking fan-out to hundreds of concurrent nurse devices; HAPI FHIR R4 client in a dedicated integration service decoupled from the core API for hospital-specific FHIR endpoint isolation; PHI never in push notification payloads per HIPAA; 21 CFR Part 11 tamper-evident alarm acknowledgments via SHA-256 row hashing; Flutter Hive cache for telemetry display during ICU RF connectivity gaps with PHI cleared on 15-minute app background.
target_output.title: Clinical Medical Device Telemetry Mobile Platform
target_output.executive_summary: Flutter mobile application enabling nurses and biomedical technicians to monitor real-time telemetry from infusion pumps, ventilators, and patient monitors across hospital care units. Spring Boot 3.3 with WebFlux reactive WebSocket streams delivers live device readings and alarm notifications via Redis pub/sub fan-out. HL7 FHIR R4 device observations are ingested from hospital HIS endpoints by a dedicated FHIR integration service using HAPI FHIR R4 client. HIPAA and 21 CFR Part 11 controls govern PHI handling, audit logging, and tamper-evident alarm acknowledgment records. Hosted on AWS GovCloud EKS.
target_output.architecture_overview: Layered architecture on AWS GovCloud: Flutter mobile clients connect via TLS 1.3 to an Application Load Balancer with WAF fronting Spring Boot pods on EKS. The REST API handles device assignments, alarm queries, and acknowledgment writes. A Spring WebFlux WebSocket endpoint accepts authenticated connections scoped to the nurse care_unit_id claim from Keycloak; a Redis pub/sub subscriber per care unit room receives telemetry published by the FHIR Integration Service and pushes STOMP messages to connected Flutter clients. The FHIR Integration Service runs as a separate EKS pod, polls the hospital HL7 FHIR R4 endpoint for DeviceObservation resources every 10 seconds using HAPI FHIR client, normalizes observations to internal telemetry schema, upserts device_telemetry records in PostgreSQL, and publishes telemetry updates to Redis. Firebase Cloud Messaging and Apple Push Notification Service are invoked on alarm_events creation with PHI-free payloads (device_id, alarm_event_id only) so the app fetches PHI after re-authentication. HIPAA audit AOP aspect intercepts all PHI-returning controller methods.
target_output.technology_stack: Mobile: Flutter 3.22, Dart 3, Riverpod 2.5, fl_chart 0.68, web_socket_channel 2.4, firebase_messaging 15, Hive 2.2 for local telemetry cache. Backend: Java 21, Spring Boot 3.3, Spring WebFlux, Spring Security 6 with Keycloak OIDC adapter, Spring Data JPA, HAPI FHIR R4 client 7.0. Database: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ with pg_partman for time-partitioned device_telemetry. Cache: Redis 7 ElastiCache. Infrastructure: AWS GovCloud EKS, ALB with WAF, ECR, KMS for RDS encryption, CloudWatch. CI: GitLab CI. Observability: Datadog APM, PagerDuty.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Care Unit Dashboard: Riverpod careUnitDevicesProvider fetches device list on auth; deviceTelemetryStreamProvider opens WebSocket subscription per care unit; telemetry cards update in real time; alarm state shown as pulsing red badge on device card. Device Detail View: fl_chart sparkline charts for last 1 hour of SpO2, heart rate, pump flow rate mL/hr, or ventilator PEEP depending on device_type; last 10 alarm_events with severity color coding. Alarm Acknowledgment: nurse opens alarm notification, app calls GET /alarms/{id} to fetch PHI after auth, taps Acknowledge, POST /alarms/{id}/acknowledge writes alarm_acknowledgments row with SHA-256 tamper hash and timestamps start and end of acknowledgment to measure response time. Push Notification: Spring alarm publisher sends FCM/APNs notification with {device_id, alarm_event_id, severity} only; nurse opens app which fetches device details after Keycloak token validation. Biomedical Tech Configuration View: read-only device metadata including firmware version, last calibration date, and device model from medical_devices table.
target_output.system_components: Spring Boot REST API (3 replicas, EKS, handles device, alarm, acknowledgment, and analytics endpoints). Spring WebFlux WebSocket Server (co-deployed with REST API, STOMP over SockJS, Redis subscriber per care_unit_id room). FHIR Integration Service (1 replica per hospital FHIR endpoint, HAPI FHIR R4 client, scheduled poll every 10 seconds, publishes to Redis, upserts device_telemetry). HIPAA Audit AOP Aspect (Spring AOP intercepts PHI controller methods, writes to device_telemetry_access_audit). Alarm Publisher Service (Spring event listener on alarm_events creation, invokes Firebase Admin SDK for FCM and APNs HTTP/2 for push notifications with PHI-free payload). PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ (AES-256 KMS encryption, IAM auth, pg_partman monthly partitions on device_telemetry). Redis 7 ElastiCache (WebSocket pub/sub channels care_unit:{id}:telemetry, device telemetry 5-minute cache). Keycloak (existing hospital deployment, AD federation, OIDC). ALB + WAF (EKS ingress, OWASP CRS rule set). ECR (container registry). Datadog APM + PagerDuty.
target_output.workflows: Real-Time Telemetry Delivery: FHIR Integration Service polls hospital FHIR endpoint GET /DeviceObservation?device={fhir_device_id}&_sort=-date&_count=1 every 10 seconds; HAPI FHIR client parses ObservationResource; normalizes to internal schema; upserts device_telemetry row in PostgreSQL; publishes {device_id, observation_type, value, unit, recorded_at} to Redis PUBLISH care_unit:{unit_id}:telemetry; Spring WebFlux WebSocket server receives via Redis subscriber and emits STOMP message to all clients subscribed to /topic/care-unit/{unit_id}; Flutter Riverpod deviceTelemetryStreamProvider receives WebSocket frame, parses telemetry, updates Hive local cache, updates Riverpod state, re-renders telemetry card in CareUnitDashboard. Critical Alarm Acknowledgment: alarm_event created in PostgreSQL when FHIR observation value crosses threshold; Alarm Publisher sends FCM notification {device_id, alarm_event_id, severity:critical, message:'Device alarm requires attention'} (no patient name or PHI); nurse receives notification, taps to open app; Flutter calls GET /alarms/{alarm_event_id} with Keycloak Bearer token; HIPAA audit AOP logs access; nurse taps Acknowledge; POST /alarms/{id}/acknowledge writes alarm_acknowledgments row with row_hash = SHA-256(alarm_event_id + nurse_id + acknowledged_at ISO-8601); acknowledgment_latency_seconds computed and stored; Datadog metric alarm_acknowledgment_latency updated.
