target_output.data_model: medical_devices (id UUID PK, care_unit_id UUID, device_type TEXT CHECK IN ('infusion_pump','ventilator','patient_monitor'), device_model TEXT, serial_number TEXT UNIQUE, fhir_device_id TEXT, status TEXT CHECK IN ('active','offline','maintenance'), last_seen_at TIMESTAMPTZ). device_telemetry (id UUID PK, device_id UUID FK, observation_type TEXT, value NUMERIC, unit TEXT, fhir_observation_id TEXT, recorded_at TIMESTAMPTZ) PARTITION BY RANGE (recorded_at). patient_assignments (id UUID PK, patient_mrn_encrypted BYTEA, device_id UUID FK, care_unit_id UUID, assigned_at TIMESTAMPTZ, discharged_at TIMESTAMPTZ). alarm_events (id UUID PK, device_id UUID FK, care_unit_id UUID, alarm_type TEXT, severity TEXT CHECK IN ('critical','high','medium','low'), message TEXT, acknowledged BOOL DEFAULT false, fired_at TIMESTAMPTZ). alarm_acknowledgments (id UUID PK, alarm_event_id UUID FK, nurse_id UUID, acknowledged_at TIMESTAMPTZ, row_hash TEXT NOT NULL). device_telemetry_access_audit (id UUID PK, nurse_id UUID, device_id UUID, patient_assignment_id UUID, care_unit UUID, access_type TEXT, timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ). nursing_staff (id UUID PK, keycloak_sub TEXT UNIQUE, display_name TEXT, role TEXT, care_unit_id UUID, active BOOL). All PHI fields use RDS KMS encryption; patient_mrn_encrypted uses application-level AES-256-GCM in addition.
target_output.api_design: REST base /api/v1. GET /care-units/{id}/devices returns medical_devices with current alarm state for dashboard render. GET /devices/{id}/telemetry?start&end returns device_telemetry for trend charts (filtered to nurse care_unit claim). GET /alarms?care_unit_id&acknowledged=false returns pending alarm_events. POST /alarms/{id}/acknowledge {acknowledged_at} writes alarm_acknowledgments with row_hash, returns acknowledgment_id. GET /analytics/alarm-response?care_unit_id&start&end returns mean and P95 alarm acknowledgment latency by alarm_type. GET /devices/{id}/config returns device metadata for biomedical_tech role only. WebSocket: STOMP /topic/care-unit/{id} for telemetry push; STOMP /topic/alarms/{care_unit_id} for alarm notifications. All endpoints protected by Keycloak Bearer token; care_unit_id claim enforced in Spring Security @PreAuthorize.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HIPAA Security Rule: PHI encrypted at rest via RDS KMS AES-256; patient_mrn_encrypted uses additional application-level AES-256-GCM; TLS 1.3 for all API and WebSocket traffic; HIPAA audit AOP aspect records every PHI data access to device_telemetry_access_audit; logs shipped to CloudWatch with 7-year retention. HITECH breach notification: CloudWatch anomaly detector on audit log volume spike triggers PagerDuty for security team review. 21 CFR Part 11: alarm_acknowledgments.row_hash computed as SHA-256(alarm_event_id || nurse_id || acknowledged_at) provides tamper evidence for electronic records integrity; hash chain validated on compliance export. IEC 80001-1: FHIR Integration Service implements network failure isolation; FHIR poll failures do not propagate to clinical data; device status set to offline on consecutive failures with alarm_event generated. Push notifications: FCM and APNs payloads contain only alarm_event_id and severity, no patient name, bed number, or diagnosis; PHI fetched only after Keycloak authentication in the app. Keycloak MFA enforced for biomedical_tech role; 15-minute session timeout on mobile enforced via Keycloak token TTL and Flutter token refresh check.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: AWS GovCloud us-gov-east-1: EKS cluster (3 m5.xlarge nodes, 3 Spring Boot API replicas, 1 FHIR Integration Service pod per hospital endpoint). RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ (db.r6g.xlarge). ElastiCache Redis 7 cluster (cache.r6g.large, 2 nodes). ALB with WAF (OWASP CRS managed rule group). KMS for RDS encryption with HIPAA usage policy condition. GitLab CI: JUnit 5 unit tests, Testcontainers integration tests (PostgreSQL + Redis), Docker build and push to ECR, Helm chart deploy to EKS via kubectl with Helm diff review in MR. Flutter CI: flutter analyze, flutter test, flutter build ios and apk, TestFlight distribution for iOS. HIPAA security review checklist gate before staging and production promotion. Rollback: Helm rollback to previous chart revision; RDS automated snapshots for data recovery. Environments: dev (single EKS node, mock FHIR endpoint), staging (multi-AZ replica), production (full EKS cluster).
target_output.observability: Datadog APM Java agent on Spring Boot pods: distributed traces for FHIR poll to WebSocket delivery path; custom metrics: alarm_delivery_latency_seconds histogram (FHIR observation receipt to FCM send), active_websocket_connections gauge per care_unit_id, fhir_poll_failures_total counter per hospital_endpoint, alarm_acknowledgment_latency_seconds histogram. PagerDuty alert when P99 alarm_delivery_latency > 30 seconds (patient safety SLA). CloudWatch Logs receives structured JSON from Spring Boot via logback-appender; 7-year CloudWatch log archive with S3 Glacier lifecycle for HIPAA retention. Flutter Firebase Crashlytics for mobile crash reporting; crashes linked to Datadog trace IDs via custom attribute.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: EKS 3 m5.xlarge nodes ~$450/month; RDS db.r6g.xlarge Multi-AZ ~$400/month; ElastiCache cache.r6g.large 2-node ~$200/month; ALB + WAF ~$50/month; total ~$1,100/month for single care unit MVP. Scale path at hospital-wide rollout (10 care units, 50 devices per unit): EKS horizontal pod autoscaler on WebSocket connection count; Redis ElastiCache cluster mode for pub/sub throughput; pg_partman monthly partitions for device_telemetry archive to S3 Glacier after 2 years to manage storage cost.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): FHIR Integration Service with HAPI FHIR R4 client for 1 hospital endpoint, Spring Boot REST API for device list and alarm queries, WebSocket telemetry push for 1 care unit, Keycloak auth with nurse and charge_nurse roles, Flutter CareUnitDashboard and DeviceDetail pages, push notification with PHI-free payload, PostgreSQL schema with device_telemetry partitioning. Phase 2 (4 weeks): alarm acknowledgment workflow with 21 CFR Part 11 row hash, HIPAA audit AOP aspect, alarm response time analytics endpoint, Flutter AlarmEventList and AlarmDetail pages, Hive local telemetry cache for connectivity gap resilience, Datadog APM and PagerDuty alarm delivery SLA alert. Phase 3 (2 weeks): biomedical_tech role with device configuration view, MFA enforcement for biomedical_tech in Keycloak, HIPAA security review documentation, load test simulating 200 concurrent WebSocket connections across 4 care units.
target_output.development_guidelines: Java: JUnit 5 and Testcontainers for integration tests covering FHIR poll to Redis publish pipeline, alarm acknowledgment hash computation, and HIPAA audit aspect invocation; JaCoCo 80% line coverage gate. Kotlin-style null safety via Optional and records in Java 21. Flutter: widget tests for CareUnitDashboard alarm state rendering; integration test for alarm push notification receipt and acknowledge flow. No PHI in log statements; Spring AOP prevents raw telemetry logging in API controllers. alarm_acknowledgments.row_hash must be validated in unit tests against known input/output pairs. Migrations via Flyway; partitioned device_telemetry table configured in first migration.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: HL7 FHIR endpoint at hospital becomes unavailable during clinical use; mitigation: FHIR Integration Service retries with exponential backoff; device set to offline status after 3 consecutive poll failures; offline alarm generated to notify charge nurse. Risk: Redis ElastiCache failure disrupts WebSocket fan-out; mitigation: Spring WebFlux WebSocket falls back to long-polling on Redis unavailability; Flutter displays stale telemetry indicator; Hive cache provides last 5 minutes of readings. Tradeoff: Spring WebFlux reactive over Spring MVC for WebSocket tier chosen for non-blocking concurrency without thread-per-connection overhead at 200+ concurrent mobile clients. Tradeoff: STOMP over raw WebSocket for structured topic-based routing that aligns with care unit scoping without custom routing logic. Risk: PHI exposure via push notification payload; mitigation: strict policy and unit test verifying FCM payload contains no patient name, MRN, diagnosis, or care unit name.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: HAPI FHIR R4 client selected over custom REST parsing for standards-compliant parsing of DeviceObservation resources with built-in FHIR validation. Spring WebFlux selected over Netty + custom reactive server for idiomatic Spring integration with Keycloak Spring Security adapter. SHA-256 row hash selected for 21 CFR Part 11 tamper evidence over a blockchain approach due to simplicity and auditability. AWS GovCloud us-gov-east-1 confirmed over AWS commercial for HIPAA Business Associate Agreement coverage on all used services including EKS and ElastiCache.

metadata.notes: First-pass HIPAA-compliant clinical device telemetry mobile platform with Flutter, Spring WebFlux, HAPI FHIR integration, and 21 CFR Part 11 alarm acknowledgment.

project.goal: Benchmark Gorilla, Chimp, and Deflate on constrained ARM devices

backend.api: CLI with --algorithm, --dataset, --target flags

data.export: Parquet via polars, CSV for paper tables

constraints.binary_size: Under 256 KB for STM32 embedded target

reasoner_reviews.product: Benchmarking scope is well-bounded: three algorithms, two hardware targets, and a standardized sensor corpus. This directly supports the paper methodology and avoids scope creep into real deployment tooling.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Rust eliminates runtime overhead from measurements. The key architectural challenge is maintaining no_std compatibility for the STM32 path while sharing algorithm implementations with the hosted Raspberry Pi harness via Cargo feature flags. Criterion is too heavy for bare metal; DWT cycle counters are the correct substitution.
reasoner_reviews.security: Local-only operation with no network stack removes most attack surface. The primary risk is accidentally committing real customer telemetry into the sensor corpus directory; a .gitignore rule and README warning are sufficient controls.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 256 KB binary constraint on STM32 is tight when including Criterion. The HTML reporting infrastructure must be conditionally compiled out for the embedded target, retaining only the raw cycle counter measurement loop.

backend.service_design: Single Cargo workspace with three crates: algorithms (no_std, Gorilla/Chimp/Deflate impls), harness (std, Criterion integration, CLI runner), analysis (std, polars Parquet exporter and comparison report generator)
backend.api_patterns: CLI subcommands via clap 4: 'harness run --algorithm [gorilla|chimp|deflate|all] --dataset PATH --runs N'; 'analysis report --format [csv|parquet] --output PATH'; 'analysis compare --algorithms A,B --metric compression_ratio'
backend.business_modules: AlgorithmRegistry for static dispatch to compression impls; DatasetLoader for CBOR sensor corpus ingestion; ResultStore for SQLite persistence with WAL mode; ReportExporter for Parquet and CSV output via polars
backend.failure_handling: Panics on missing or malformed CBOR dataset files with descriptive message; non-zero exit code on SQLite write failure; transactions wrap each run insertion to prevent partial result rows

security.auth_design: No authentication; tool runs under researcher Unix account with physical lab access control
security.secrets_management: No secrets stored anywhere; no external API credentials required
security.security_risks: Risk of committing real sensor telemetry to corpus; mitigated by .gitignore on data/ directory and README warning. cargo-audit in CI checks miniz_oxide and polars dependency trees.

data.entities: benchmark_runs (id, algorithm, dataset_name, device_target, run_timestamp, git_sha), compression_results (id, run_id FK, chunk_index, original_bytes, compressed_bytes, compression_ratio, encode_ns, decode_ns, peak_memory_kb), sensor_datasets (id, dataset_name, signal_type, num_samples, sample_rate_hz, registered_at)
data.storage_design: SQLite at ~/.local/share/iot-compress-bench/results.db in WAL mode; file created with permissions 0600 via rusqlite OpenFlags
data.data_risks: Result database corruption if harness is interrupted mid-write; mitigated by SQLite transactions wrapping each full run insertion

devops.deployment_topology: Local execution only; cargo cross compiles aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu for RPi5 and thumbv7em-none-eabi for STM32 via pinned Docker cross image
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions matrix on ubuntu-latest: clippy for both targets, cargo test for std targets, binary size assertion confirming embedded binary under 256 KB, cargo-audit for supply chain CVE scan
devops.observability_stack: Criterion generates target/criterion/report/index.html with violin plots and regression charts; analysis crate produces comparison_report.csv; GitHub Actions uploads Criterion HTML as workflow artifact
devops.devops_risks: Cross-compilation Docker images can drift from actual board toolchain versions; pin cross image digest in workflow YAML

target_output.thinking_summary: Rust no_std benchmark prototype for IoT compression algorithms on ARM hardware. Key decisions: Cargo workspace splits no_std algorithm crate from hosted harness enabling code reuse across RPi5 and STM32 targets; Criterion for statistical rigor on Linux; DWT cycle counters replace Criterion on bare metal where std is unavailable; SQLite provides relational query capability for multi-run analysis without heavyweight dependencies.
target_output.title: IoT Edge Compression Algorithm Benchmark Harness
target_output.executive_summary: A Rust benchmark tool measuring Gorilla, Chimp, and Deflate compression performance on IoT sensor data across Raspberry Pi 5 aarch64 and STM32 Discovery thumbv7em targets. Per-algorithm metrics including compression ratio, encode/decode nanoseconds, and peak memory KB are stored in SQLite and exported to Parquet and CSV for academic paper statistical analysis.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single Cargo workspace with three member crates separated by std/no_std boundary. The algorithms crate is no_std and contains Gorilla XOR-based floating-point compression, Chimp128 with FCMOV optimization, and Deflate wrapping miniz_oxide. The harness crate is std-only, integrates Criterion for statistical benchmarking on Linux, and exposes a clap CLI. For STM32, a separate embedded-runner crate links the algorithms crate and substitutes Criterion with DWT cycle counter reads via the cortex-m crate. Results flow to SQLite on hosted targets; on embedded targets they are serialized as newline-delimited JSON over UART and captured by a host-side Python collection script into the shared SQLite schema.
target_output.technology_stack: Rust stable 1.78+. algorithms crate: no_std with miniz_oxide 0.7 for Deflate, custom Gorilla and Chimp128 impls. harness crate: Criterion 0.5, clap 4, rusqlite 0.31, polars 0.39. embedded-runner crate: cortex-m 0.7, cortex-m-rt, defmt over RTT. Build: cargo cross with pinned Docker images for aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu and thumbv7em-none-eabi. CI: GitHub Actions with cargo-audit.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Algorithm Benchmark Runner: Criterion suites for Gorilla, Chimp, Deflate against each corpus chunk with configurable iteration count and warm-up. Dataset Loader: reads CBOR-encoded sensor corpus files for temperature, humidity, CO2, and vibration signals. Result Store: writes benchmark_runs and compression_results rows to SQLite in a single transaction per run. STM32 Cycle Counter Runner: measures encode/decode DWT cycles on bare metal, serializes over UART for host capture. Report Exporter: produces comparison_report.csv and results.parquet with per-algorithm statistical aggregates via polars.
target_output.system_components: Cargo workspace root with three member crates. algorithms/ (no_std): Gorilla encoder/decoder, Chimp128 encoder/decoder, Deflate wrapper over miniz_oxide. harness/ (std, hosted): Criterion benchmark groups per algorithm, clap CLI, rusqlite result store, polars Parquet exporter. embedded-runner/ (no_std, thumbv7em): DWT cycle counter harness, defmt over RTT, UART JSON serializer. Sensor corpus in data/corpus/*.cbor (gitignored). SQLite results at ~/.local/share/iot-compress-bench/results.db.
target_output.workflows: Hosted Benchmark Run: researcher invokes 'cargo run --bin harness -- run --algorithm all --dataset data/corpus/temperature_1h.cbor --runs 100'; harness loads CBOR dataset into Vec<f32> chunks, runs Criterion groups for each algorithm measuring encode and decode throughput, computes compression ratio per chunk, inserts benchmark_runs and compression_results rows in a single SQLite transaction, prints summary table with p50 latency and mean compression ratio per algorithm. Embedded STM32 Run: researcher cross-compiles embedded-runner, flashes via 'probe-rs run', opens serial terminal at 115200 baud; firmware iterates over a ROM-resident sensor array, reads DWT cycle counter before/after each encode/decode call, serializes result JSON over UART; host-side Python capture script appends rows to SQLite as device_target='stm32'. Post-Run Analysis: 'cargo run --bin analysis -- report --format parquet --output results.parquet' queries SQLite, aggregates mean and stddev per algorithm-device pair, writes Parquet and comparison_report.csv.
target_output.data_model: benchmark_runs (id INTEGER PK, algorithm TEXT NOT NULL CHECK(algorithm IN ('gorilla','chimp','deflate')), dataset_name TEXT NOT NULL, device_target TEXT NOT NULL CHECK(device_target IN ('rpi5','stm32')), run_timestamp INTEGER NOT NULL, git_sha TEXT). compression_results (id INTEGER PK, run_id INTEGER NOT NULL REFERENCES benchmark_runs(id), chunk_index INTEGER NOT NULL, original_bytes INTEGER NOT NULL, compressed_bytes INTEGER NOT NULL, compression_ratio REAL NOT NULL, encode_ns INTEGER NOT NULL, decode_ns INTEGER NOT NULL, peak_memory_kb INTEGER). sensor_datasets (id INTEGER PK, dataset_name TEXT UNIQUE NOT NULL, signal_type TEXT CHECK(signal_type IN ('temperature','humidity','co2','vibration')), num_samples INTEGER, sample_rate_hz REAL, registered_at INTEGER NOT NULL).
target_output.api_design: CLI via clap 4 subcommands. 'harness run --algorithm [gorilla|chimp|deflate|all] --dataset PATH --runs N --output-db PATH': runs benchmark and stores results. 'harness list-runs': prints run summary table from SQLite. 'analysis report --run-id ID|--all --format [csv|parquet] --output PATH': exports aggregated comparison. 'analysis compare --algorithms gorilla,chimp --metric compression_ratio': side-by-side statistical table. No HTTP API; all interaction via CLI.
target_output.security_and_compliance: No network stack eliminates remote attack surface. Sensor corpus data/ directory is gitignored to prevent commit of real telemetry. SQLite database created with permissions 0600 via rusqlite OpenFlags. cargo-audit runs in CI on every push checking miniz_oxide and polars dependency trees for known CVEs. No credential storage or external service calls anywhere in the codebase.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Development: 'cargo build' for hosted debug build. RPi5 deployment: 'cargo cross build --target aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu --release' then scp binary to board. STM32 deployment: 'cargo cross build --target thumbv7em-none-eabi --release' then 'probe-rs run' to flash. GitHub Actions CI matrix on ubuntu-latest: clippy for both targets; cargo test for hosted; binary size check asserting embedded binary under 256 KB; cargo-audit dependency scan on every push. No container orchestration; single-shot CLI invocations on owned lab hardware.
target_output.observability: Criterion generates target/criterion/report/index.html with violin plots, regression detection charts, and throughput comparisons per benchmark group after each hosted run. CLI prints per-algorithm summary table to stdout with compression ratio, mean encode latency, mean decode latency, and peak memory KB. GitHub Actions uploads Criterion HTML as workflow artifact. Ad-hoc SQLite queries for cross-run trend analysis: 'SELECT algorithm, device_target, AVG(compression_ratio), COUNT(*) FROM compression_results JOIN benchmark_runs ON run_id=id GROUP BY algorithm, device_target'.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero cloud cost; all computation on owned lab hardware. RPi5 and STM32 Discovery boards are existing lab inventory. GitHub Actions free tier covers the two-target CI matrix. SQLite database remains under 50 MB across the 18-month project lifetime at one benchmark run per day. Criterion suites complete in under 8 minutes per algorithm set on RPi5.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (3 weeks): Gorilla and Chimp algorithm crates, hosted Criterion harness, SQLite result storage, CSV export; validate methodology on RPi5 with temperature corpus. Phase 2 (2 weeks): Deflate via miniz_oxide; add humidity, CO2, and vibration corpus; extend comparison report to all signal types. Phase 3 (2 weeks): STM32 embedded-runner with DWT cycle counter, cross-compilation CI matrix, UART capture script, merge embedded results into shared SQLite schema. Phase 4 (2 weeks): polars Parquet exporter, analysis CLI with statistical comparison, Criterion regression CI gate, final report generation for paper submission.
target_output.development_guidelines: Rust stable only; no nightly features except cortex-m-rt. All algorithm crates compile with no_std and no implicit global allocator. Criterion groups must specify sample_size(50) minimum for statistically valid results. Each run records git SHA in benchmark_runs for reproducibility. cargo-audit runs in CI on every push. Corpus files documented in data/corpus/README.md with signal type, sample rate, and provenance.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: thermal throttling on RPi5 inflates Criterion wall-clock measurements; mitigated by 10-minute CPU warm-up and discarding the first 20% of samples. Risk: miniz_oxide Deflate compression level must match the variant used in target firmware; document the compression_level constant in the algorithm crate API. Tradeoff: DWT cycle counters on STM32 give reproducible isolated measurements but cross-device latency comparisons require CPU frequency normalization in the analysis step. Tradeoff: polars adds 15 MB to the analysis binary but eliminates custom aggregation code.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected SQLite over InfluxDB because result data is tabular and relational not time-series. Chose miniz_oxide over flate2 for Deflate to maintain no_std compatibility on STM32. Corpus format chosen as CBOR over CSV to preserve binary fidelity of floating-point sensor samples without precision loss from decimal serialization.

project.goal: RL-based adaptive traffic signal control via SUMO simulation

backend.api: CLI experiment runner with argparse

data.db: InfluxDB 2 for time-series, SQLite for experiment catalog

reasoner_reviews.product: The scope is appropriately bounded for a research prototype: DQN and PPO on a simulated urban grid with SUMO, LoRa sensor ingestion stub for future hardware integration, and InfluxDB episode storage. The convergence detector is a key differentiator from naive training loops.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: asyncio is the right concurrency model here because SUMO TraCI calls are I/O-bound and need to interleave with the RL policy inference loop without blocking. The InfluxDB write path must be buffered to avoid becoming the training throughput bottleneck at high episode rates.
reasoner_reviews.security: Local-only system with no sensitive data. InfluxDB must be bound to 127.0.0.1 only to prevent other lab machines from writing spurious data into experiment buckets.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 50ms inference latency budget per timestep is the critical constraint. DQN inference on GPU should comfortably meet this; PPO policy gradient updates must be batched and run asynchronously to avoid blocking the simulation loop.

backend.service_design: Python package with four modules: simulation (SUMO TraCI bridge and episode lifecycle manager), rl_agents (DQN and PPO wrappers over stable-baselines3 with checkpoint management), sensor_bridge (asyncio LoRa sensor ingestion stub returning vehicle counts per intersection approach), storage (InfluxDB writer with in-memory buffer, SQLite experiment registry)
backend.api_patterns: CLI entry point via argparse: 'train --agent [dqn|ppo] --intersections N --episodes E --checkpoint-interval 1000 --config CONFIG_YAML'; 'evaluate --checkpoint PATH --episodes E'; 'compare --runs RUN_ID1,RUN_ID2 --metric avg_wait_time'
backend.business_modules: EpisodeOrchestrator driving the asyncio event loop; SignalPhaseSampler translating RL action indices to SUMO traffic light phase strings; RewardCalculator computing negative aggregate wait time from SUMO vehicle subscription data; ConvergenceDetector comparing rolling 100-episode average against target threshold
backend.failure_handling: SUMO crash detection via TraCI connection health check; automatic episode restart with same random seed on SUMO fault; InfluxDB write failures logged and buffered for retry; policy checkpoint saved on KeyboardInterrupt to preserve training progress

security.auth_design: No authentication; tool runs under researcher Unix account
security.secrets_management: InfluxDB token stored in .env file excluded from git; no external service credentials
security.security_risks: InfluxDB must bind to 127.0.0.1 to prevent other workstation cluster nodes from injecting data into experiment buckets

data.entities: intersections (id, grid_x, grid_y, num_approaches, phase_count), vehicle_counts (measurement, intersection_id, approach, count, simulation_time), signal_phases (measurement, intersection_id, phase_index, simulation_time), rl_policies (id, agent_type, config_hash, checkpoint_path, created_at), simulation_episodes (id, policy_id FK, episode_number, total_reward, avg_wait_time_s, steps, converged, recorded_at), reward_records (measurement, policy_id, episode, timestep, reward, simulation_time)
data.storage_design: InfluxDB 2 bucket 'traffic_experiments' for per-timestep vehicle_counts, signal_phases, reward_records with 90-day retention; SQLite for rl_policies and simulation_episodes relational metadata
data.analytics_events: Episode completion event written to InfluxDB with total_reward and avg_wait_time tags for Grafana convergence dashboard; convergence detection event logged to SQLite simulation_episodes.converged flag

devops.deployment_topology: Single on-premises workstation cluster; InfluxDB 2 and Grafana run as Docker Compose services; SUMO installed system-wide; Python environment managed via pyenv + pip requirements.txt
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: pytest unit tests for RewardCalculator, ConvergenceDetector, and sensor_bridge; mypy type checking; flake8 lint; SUMO simulation tests using a minimal 2-intersection scenario
devops.observability_stack: Grafana dashboards on InfluxDB: reward_trajectory panel (line chart per policy), avg_wait_time_distribution (histogram per episode bucket), convergence_curve (rolling 100-episode mean vs threshold line), signal_phase_heatmap (phase utilization per intersection approach)
devops.devops_risks: SUMO version pinning is critical; TraCI API changes between SUMO minor versions can silently break the simulation bridge; pin sumo==1.19 in requirements.txt

ISS-001.title: InfluxDB write path blocks asyncio simulation loop
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan used synchronous influxdb-client writes inside the episode timestep loop, causing simulation to pause 80-200ms per write and failing the 50ms inference latency budget

ISS-002.title: Convergence detector uses incorrect rolling window calculation
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 convergence check computed simple moving average over last 100 episodes but compared against instantaneous reward rather than rolling average, producing false convergence signals after only 20 episodes in low-variance scenarios

ISS-003.title: PPO policy gradient updates not decoupled from simulation loop
ISS-003.detail: PPO gradient updates run synchronously in the main asyncio loop; for large intersection grids this causes simulation step latency spikes up to 400ms during update steps, violating the 50ms budget on multi-intersection scenarios

focus_issues.title: Convergence detector uses incorrect rolling window calculation
focus_issues.detail: Rolling average must compare 100-episode window mean against target threshold, not instantaneous reward

focus_issues.title: PPO policy gradient updates not decoupled from simulation loop
focus_issues.detail: PPO updates must run in a separate thread or process pool executor to avoid blocking the asyncio simulation loop

latest_recommendations: Fix convergence detector to compare rolling 100-episode mean against threshold, not instantaneous reward per episode
Offload PPO gradient update steps to asyncio.run_in_executor with a ThreadPoolExecutor to decouple from simulation timestep loop

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 revision addressing ISS-002 (convergence detector rolling window fix) and ISS-003 (PPO gradient update decoupling from asyncio loop). ISS-002 fix: replace instantaneous reward comparison with collections.deque(maxlen=100) rolling mean comparison. ISS-003 fix: wrap PPO learn() in run_in_executor with ThreadPoolExecutor to prevent blocking the simulation timestep loop.

fix_report.action_taken: Rewrote ConvergenceDetector to maintain a collections.deque(maxlen=100) of per-episode total rewards. Convergence is signaled when the window mean falls within 2% of the target avg_wait_time threshold for 50 consecutive episodes, replacing the incorrect instantaneous reward comparison
fix_report.expected_outcome: False convergence signals eliminated; detector correctly identifies sustained policy improvement rather than single-episode fluctuations

fix_report.action_taken: PPO gradient update (learn() call) moved to asyncio.run_in_executor using a concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=2). The simulation loop enqueues rollout batches to an asyncio.Queue; a background executor consumer drains the queue and runs PPO updates without blocking TraCI sim steps
fix_report.expected_outcome: PPO training on 16-intersection grid no longer causes simulation step latency spikes; inference latency budget of 50ms maintained across all agent types

target_output.title: Adaptive Traffic Signal RL Control Prototype
target_output.executive_summary: A Python asyncio research prototype training DQN and PPO reinforcement learning agents to adaptively control traffic signal phases across a simulated urban intersection grid using SUMO TraCI. Vehicle count observations from LoRa sensor stubs drive per-timestep action selection; episode rewards and convergence metrics are stored in InfluxDB and visualized in Grafana. Round 3 resolves convergence detector logic and PPO gradient update decoupling.
target_output.architecture_overview: asyncio event loop orchestrates SUMO TraCI simulation ticks, RL policy inference, and sensor data ingestion within a 50ms per-timestep budget. The simulation bridge calls TraCI.simulationStep(), reads vehicle subscription data per intersection approach, and passes vehicle_counts to the active RL agent for phase selection. DQN inference runs directly in the event loop under 5ms on GPU. PPO gradient updates are decoupled: rollout batches are enqueued to an asyncio.Queue and consumed by a background ThreadPoolExecutor worker running learn() without blocking simulation ticks. InfluxDB writes are buffered via a separate asyncio.Queue and drained by a dedicated coroutine outside the critical timestep path. ConvergenceDetector maintains a deque(maxlen=100) of episode total_reward values and signals convergence when the rolling mean is within 2% of the target avg_wait_time threshold for 50 consecutive episodes.
target_output.technology_stack: Python 3.12 with asyncio. SUMO 1.19 with TraCI Python bindings. stable-baselines3 2.3 for DQN and PPO agents. PyTorch 2.3 for GPU-accelerated policy inference. InfluxDB 2.7 for time-series episode data. influxdb-client-python async API. SQLite via aiosqlite for experiment metadata. Grafana 10 on InfluxDB for dashboards. Docker Compose for InfluxDB and Grafana services. GitHub Actions for CI.
target_output.functional_feature_map: RL Agent Training: DQN and PPO agents via stable-baselines3 controlled by CLI experiment runner with configurable intersection count, episode budget, and policy config YAML. SUMO TraCI Bridge: asyncio simulation step loop with vehicle count subscriptions per intersection approach and programmatic traffic light phase control. LoRa Sensor Stub: asyncio coroutine returning per-approach vehicle counts from either TraCI subscription data or a replay file for future hardware integration. ConvergenceDetector: deque(maxlen=100) rolling mean comparator with 50-episode stability window and configurable threshold. InfluxDB Async Writer: asyncio.Queue-backed buffer draining writes outside simulation timestep critical path. Grafana Dashboards: reward trajectory, wait time distribution, convergence curve, signal phase heatmap.
target_output.system_components: simulation/ module: SUMOBridge (TraCI lifecycle, vehicle subscriptions, phase commands), EpisodeOrchestrator (asyncio loop, timestep budget enforcement, checkpoint trigger). rl_agents/ module: DQNAgent and PPOAgent wrapping stable-baselines3 with observation normalization and checkpoint save/load; PPORolloutQueue (asyncio.Queue feeding ThreadPoolExecutor PPO learner). sensor_bridge/ module: LoRaSensorStub returning vehicle_counts dict per intersection approach from TraCI or replay file. storage/ module: InfluxDBWriter (asyncio.Queue buffer, async influxdb-client batch writes), ExperimentRegistry (aiosqlite wrapper for rl_policies and simulation_episodes tables), ConvergenceDetector (deque rolling mean logic). Grafana/InfluxDB Docker Compose stack on localhost.
target_output.workflows: Training Episode Loop: EpisodeOrchestrator calls TraCI.simulationStep() advancing SUMO by one step (1 second simulated time); reads vehicle counts from subscription data per approach for all active intersections; passes observation vector to active agent.predict() returning phase action indices; applies phase commands via TraCI.trafficlight.setPhase(); computes reward as negative sum of vehicle waiting times across all intersections; enqueues (observation, action, reward, next_obs) to PPORolloutQueue if PPO agent; enqueues InfluxDB write for vehicle_counts, signal_phases, reward_records measurements; repeats until episode step budget or terminal condition. PPO Update (decoupled): ThreadPoolExecutor worker monitors PPORolloutQueue; when rollout buffer reaches batch_size, calls agent.learn(batch) synchronously in worker thread; posts updated policy weights back to main loop via asyncio.Queue without blocking simulation ticks. Convergence Check: after each episode, ConvergenceDetector.record(total_reward) appends to deque; if len(deque)==100 and abs(deque.mean() - threshold)/threshold < 0.02 for 50 consecutive episodes, fires convergence event to EpisodeOrchestrator which saves final checkpoint and terminates training loop.
target_output.data_model: InfluxDB 2 bucket 'traffic_experiments' (90-day retention): vehicle_counts (measurement, tags: intersection_id, approach, fields: count, simulation_time_s), signal_phases (measurement, tags: intersection_id, fields: phase_index, phase_duration_s, simulation_time_s), reward_records (measurement, tags: policy_id, episode, fields: reward, cumulative_reward, simulation_time_s). SQLite (aiosqlite): rl_policies (id, agent_type TEXT CHECK IN ('dqn','ppo'), config_json TEXT, checkpoint_path TEXT, created_at INTEGER), simulation_episodes (id, policy_id INTEGER REFERENCES rl_policies(id), episode_number INTEGER, total_reward REAL, avg_wait_time_s REAL, steps INTEGER, converged INTEGER, recorded_at INTEGER).
target_output.api_design: CLI via argparse. 'train --agent [dqn|ppo] --intersections N --episodes E --checkpoint-interval 1000 --config config.yaml': starts training loop with specified agent and scenario. 'evaluate --checkpoint PATH --episodes E --output-json PATH': runs evaluation episodes and writes metrics JSON. 'compare --policy-ids ID1,ID2 --metric [avg_wait_time|total_reward] --window 100': queries InfluxDB and prints rolling average comparison table. 'export --policy-id ID --format parquet --output PATH': exports episode data from InfluxDB to Parquet for paper analysis. No HTTP API.
target_output.security_and_compliance: InfluxDB bound to 127.0.0.1:8086 to prevent other cluster nodes from writing to experiment buckets. InfluxDB admin token stored in .env file excluded from git via .gitignore. No external API calls; no network exposure beyond localhost Docker bridge. SUMO executable path and version pinned in config.yaml to prevent silent TraCI API drift. No PII or sensitive data anywhere in the system.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Docker Compose on each workstation node brings up InfluxDB 2.7 and Grafana 10 as services. Python environment via pyenv 3.12 and pip with pinned requirements.txt including sumo==1.19 and stable-baselines3==2.3. SUMO installed system-wide via apt on Ubuntu 22.04. Training launched via CLI: 'python -m traffic_rl.train --agent ppo --intersections 16 --episodes 5000'. Policy checkpoints saved to checkpoints/ directory every 1000 episodes. GitHub Actions CI runs pytest, mypy, flake8, and a 2-intersection SUMO smoke test on every push to main.
target_output.observability: Grafana dashboards on InfluxDB: reward_trajectory panel showing rolling 100-episode mean per policy_id, avg_wait_time_distribution histogram per 500-episode bucket, convergence_curve panel with rolling mean and threshold reference line, signal_phase_heatmap showing phase utilization per intersection approach over training. Structured JSON log per episode to logs/experiment_{run_id}.jsonl including episode number, total_reward, avg_wait_time, convergence status, and PPO update latency. Prometheus metrics endpoint in planned future scope; not in current prototype.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero cloud cost; all training on owned workstation cluster with RTX 3090 GPUs. SUMO simulation at 10x real-time on CPU; PPO training accelerated on GPU via PyTorch CUDA. InfluxDB storage estimated at 2 GB per 10,000 episodes with 16 intersections at 1-second resolution; 90-day retention sufficient for active research cycle. Adding intersections beyond 64 may require distributed SUMO instances via libsumo parallel simulation API.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (complete): DQN on single 4-way intersection in SUMO; InfluxDB episode logging; basic convergence detection; Grafana reward dashboard. Phase 2 (complete): PPO agent with rollout batching; async InfluxDB write buffer resolving ISS-001; 4x4 intersection grid scenario. Phase 3 (this round): Fix convergence detector rolling window comparison (ISS-002); decouple PPO gradient updates to ThreadPoolExecutor (ISS-003); 16-intersection grid validation. Phase 4 (planned): LoRa hardware sensor integration replacing stub; multi-agent cooperative signal coordination; paper results export pipeline.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with strict mypy type checking and flake8 lint enforced in CI. asyncio coroutines must not perform blocking I/O; all database and external calls use async equivalents. SUMO version pinned in requirements.txt and config.yaml; never upgrade without re-running full convergence validation suite. Policy checkpoint format versioned with agent type and config hash to prevent loading incompatible checkpoints. Experiment configs stored as YAML under configs/ and committed to git for reproducibility. Random seeds logged per episode for replay capability.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: SUMO TraCI API changes between minor versions can silently break phase command mapping; mitigated by pinning sumo==1.19 and running integration smoke test in CI. Risk: ThreadPoolExecutor PPO updates introduce GIL contention at high episode rates; mitigated by using ProcessPoolExecutor if GIL becomes the bottleneck on large grids. Tradeoff: deque(maxlen=100) convergence window is a fixed hyperparameter; too small causes false convergence, too large delays experiment completion; expose as --convergence-window CLI flag. Tradeoff: InfluxDB 90-day retention purges long-term experiment history; researchers must export Parquet before retention cutoff.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected InfluxDB over TimescaleDB for per-timestep reward records due to simpler Flux query API for Grafana dashboards. Chose stable-baselines3 over RLlib to reduce dependency complexity for a single-node prototype. SUMO TraCI chosen over libsumo bindings to allow out-of-process simulation restart on crash without Python interpreter restart.

metadata.notes: Round 3 revision fixing RL convergence detector rolling window logic and decoupling PPO updates from asyncio simulation loop.

project_goal.value: Real-time AI system monitoring live smart gas meter feeds across a distribution network to detect leakage patterns, pressure anomalies, and tamper events, automatically isolating suspect segments and dispatching field crews with GPS coordinates
project_goal.rationale: Core safety mission replacing manual meter inspection with AI-driven anomaly detection

target_users.value: Gas utility network operations center staff, field crew dispatchers, safety engineers, and on-call incident commanders
target_users.rationale: Primary operators identified in safety system requirements

project_class.rationale: Core functionality is ML-based anomaly scoring on live sensor streams with automated actuation

capabilities.value: backend, data, auth, ai_llm, realtime, analytics, admin_panel
capabilities.rationale: Inference pipeline, time-series storage, Cognito auth, LLM incident summarization, Kafka streaming, Grafana analytics, ops admin console

complexity_level.rationale: Tens of thousands of gas meters transmitting per-minute readings; sub-2-minute end-to-end detection SLA

risk_level.rationale: Critical infrastructure; incorrect isolation commands could interrupt gas supply; false negatives risk life-safety incidents

data_sensitivity.rationale: Distribution network topology and meter locations are security-sensitive operational data

external_exposure.rationale: Operations center and field crew access only; never exposed to public internet

access_model.value: RBAC with roles: ops_center_analyst (read/acknowledge alerts), dispatcher (assign crew), safety_engineer (configure thresholds), incident_commander (authorize segment isolation), system_admin (full access)
access_model.rationale: Principle of least privilege for safety-critical actuation commands

feature_scope.rationale: Full operational scope for gas distribution safety system

mvp_scope.value: Leakage and pressure anomaly detection on 5,000-meter pilot network segment with manual isolation approval flow and PagerDuty crew paging
mvp_scope.rationale: Pilot validates detection accuracy before automated isolation authorization

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3 in transit, AES-256 at rest on TimescaleDB and S3, Cognito MFA for all roles, WAF on ALB, VPC-isolated EKS cluster, network topology data classified confidential
security_baseline.rationale: Critical infrastructure protection requirements and internal security policy

frontend_stack.value: None; operations console is a Grafana-based admin panel with custom plugins for incident management
frontend_stack.rationale: Grafana provides real-time streaming dashboards without custom frontend development; incident actions via FastAPI REST

backend_stack.rationale: FastAPI async performance suits high-throughput telemetry; Kafka provides durable replay for anomaly reprocessing

data_platform.rationale: TimescaleDB hypertables optimize per-meter time-range queries; S3 Glacier for long-term regulatory retention

privacy_retention_policy.value: Meter pressure/flow data retained 7 years for regulatory compliance; incident records retained 10 years; raw Kafka topic retention 72 hours for replay; S3 Glacier archive for long-term
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: National gas safety authority reporting requirements mandate long-term incident data retention

future_scope.rationale: Year 2 safety platform roadmap

execution_preference.rationale: Safety-critical system requires controlled rollout and model update governance
