project.goal: AI-driven gas leakage and tamper detection with automated isolation and crew dispatch
project.target_users: Gas utility ops center staff, field dispatchers, safety engineers
project.timeline: Pilot in 6 months, full rollout 12 months

backend.framework: FastAPI with Kafka Streams consumer groups
backend.api: RESTful with OpenAPI 3.0 and WebSocket for live alert feed

security.encryption: AES-256 at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit
security.compliance: NERC CIP analogues, SOC 2 Type II

reasoner_reviews.product: Scope is well-defined for the pilot: leakage pattern detection across adjacent meters, pressure anomaly scoring, tamper event classification, automated isolation with human approval for MVP, and LLM-generated regulatory reports. The 2-minute SLA is the governing constraint that shapes every architectural decision.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Kafka is the right backbone here because meter telemetry needs durable 72-hour replay for model retraining and incident post-mortems. TimescaleDB hypertables partitioned by network_segment_id will make per-segment time-range queries efficient at scale. The ML inference service must be separate from the ingestion consumer to allow independent scaling when anomaly load spikes during weather events.
reasoner_reviews.security: Critical infrastructure classification requires VPC isolation, no public endpoints, MFA for all roles including field dispatchers, and WAF on the internal ALB. Isolation commands to segment valve controllers must require incident_commander role and log to an append-only audit table with PagerDuty notification.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 2-minute end-to-end SLA means Kafka consumer lag must be monitored and alerting must fire before lag exceeds 30 seconds. The 0.1% false isolation rate requires multi-meter confirmation logic: a single anomalous meter should not trigger isolation; at least two adjacent meters within the same network segment must show correlated anomaly scores above threshold.

backend.service_design: Four EKS deployments: ingestion-consumer (Kafka consumer group reading meter_telemetry topic, writing to TimescaleDB), inference-service (FastAPI serving anomaly scoring endpoint, loads PyTorch model from S3), incident-api (FastAPI REST for incident lifecycle, isolation commands, crew dispatch), llm-reporter (Bedrock GPT-4o integration for regulatory report generation)
backend.api_patterns: REST API at /api/v1: GET /incidents (paginated active incidents), POST /incidents/{id}/isolate (incident_commander role), POST /incidents/{id}/dispatch-crew (dispatcher role), GET /incidents/{id}/report (LLM summary), WebSocket /ws/alerts for live alert stream to Grafana plugin
backend.business_modules: AnomalyScorer: multivariate isolation forest + LSTM for pressure time-series; LeakagePatternConfirmer: checks if 2+ adjacent meters in segment exceed threshold within 5-minute window; SegmentIsolationOrchestrator: validates role, logs to audit table, sends command to valve controller API; CrewDispatcher: PagerDuty API integration with meter GPS coordinates
backend.failure_handling: Kafka consumer group rebalancing on pod failure with automatic offset commit; circuit breaker on valve controller API calls with manual fallback workflow; Dead Letter Queue for unprocessable telemetry messages; inference-service graceful degradation to threshold-only scoring if model load fails

security.auth_design: Cognito user pools with MFA for all five roles; RBAC enforced in FastAPI via JWT claim inspection; incident_commander role requires hardware MFA token
security.authorization_model: RBAC: ops_center_analyst (read incidents, acknowledge alerts), dispatcher (assign and page crews), safety_engineer (configure thresholds and model parameters), incident_commander (execute isolation commands), system_admin (full access including model deployment)
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Append-only incident_audit_log table in TimescaleDB for all isolation commands with user_id, role, timestamp, and justification text; CloudTrail for all AWS API calls; Datadog log aggregation with 7-year retention on S3
security.security_risks: False isolation commands could disrupt gas supply to residential areas; mitigated by multi-meter confirmation requirement and incident_commander approval gate in MVP; valve controller API uses mTLS client certificate authentication

data.entities: gas_meters (id, meter_serial, network_segment_id FK, gps_lat, gps_lon, installation_date, status), pressure_readings (time, meter_id, pressure_bar, flow_m3h, battery_pct), flow_anomalies (id, meter_id, detected_at, anomaly_type, anomaly_score, contributing_meters JSONB), network_segments (id, segment_code, valve_controller_endpoint, district_zone, isolation_status), tamper_events (id, meter_id, event_type, detected_at, acknowledged_by), crew_dispatches (id, incident_id FK, crew_id, dispatched_at, ack_at, resolved_at), isolation_commands (id, segment_id FK, commanded_by, commanded_at, authorization_role, rollback_at)
data.storage_design: TimescaleDB hypertable on pressure_readings partitioned by week and network_segment_id; continuous aggregate for hourly pressure statistics per segment; S3 raw telemetry archive via Kafka S3 sink connector; Redis for ML model parameter cache and per-meter anomaly score deduplication
data.retention_and_deletion: pressure_readings 7 years in TimescaleDB with tiered storage to S3 after 90 days; incident records 10 years; raw Kafka topic retention 72 hours; Redis TTL 1 hour on anomaly score cache

devops.deployment_topology: EKS multi-AZ (us-east-1a/1b/1c); MSK 3-broker cluster with replication factor 3; RDS TimescaleDB Multi-AZ; ElastiCache Redis cluster mode; NLB for WebSocket connections; ALB with WAF for REST API; VPC with private subnets only; no public IPs on any workload pod
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: unit tests for AnomalyScorer and LeakagePatternConfirmer, integration test with embedded Kafka (testcontainers), Docker build and ECR push, Helm chart deploy to staging with canary analysis via Argo Rollouts before production promotion
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM on all pods with distributed tracing across ingestion-consumer, inference-service, and incident-api; custom metrics: kafka_consumer_lag_seconds, anomaly_score_p99, isolation_command_count, false_isolation_rate_7d; PagerDuty escalation on consumer_lag > 60s or error_rate > 0.5%
devops.devops_risks: MSK consumer group rebalancing during EKS node replacement can cause processing gaps; mitigated by graceful shutdown hook committing offsets before pod termination and using Kafka Streams exactly-once semantics

target_output.thinking_summary: Safety-critical gas distribution AI system with 2-minute detection SLA. Key decisions: separate ingestion-consumer from inference-service for independent scaling; multi-meter confirmation logic prevents single-sensor false isolation; TimescaleDB hypertables by network_segment_id optimize per-segment queries; incident_commander RBAC gate on isolation commands with MFA; LLM via Bedrock for regulatory report drafts with mandatory human review.
target_output.title: Gas Distribution Network AI Safety Monitoring System
target_output.executive_summary: A high-scale real-time AI system ingesting pressure and flow telemetry from tens of thousands of smart gas meters via Kafka on AWS EKS, scoring each reading with a multivariate anomaly model, confirming leakage patterns across adjacent meter clusters, and automatically dispatching isolation commands and field crews within the 2-minute regulatory SLA. GPT-4o via Bedrock generates structured regulatory incident report narratives for human review.
target_output.architecture_overview: Event-driven pipeline on AWS EKS with four specialized services. Smart meters transmit pressure_bar, flow_m3h, and tamper_flag readings every 60 seconds via cellular NB-IoT to an MQTT broker that bridges to MSK Kafka topic meter_telemetry. The ingestion-consumer service (Kafka consumer group, 12 partitions) normalizes and writes readings to TimescaleDB hypertables partitioned by network_segment_id. The inference-service polls the inference_queue Kafka topic, runs isolation forest + LSTM anomaly scoring per reading, and publishes high-confidence events to the anomaly_events topic. The incident-api service consumes anomaly_events, applies multi-meter leakage pattern confirmation (2+ adjacent meters in same segment within 5-minute window), creates incident records, and when confirmed, sends isolation commands to segment valve controllers via mTLS REST and pages field crews via PagerDuty with GPS coordinates. The llm-reporter service consumes confirmed incidents and calls GPT-4o via AWS Bedrock to generate structured regulatory report drafts pending human review. All services run in a VPC private subnet with no public IP assignment; ALB with WAF serves the internal operations REST API and Grafana dashboards.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI (incident-api and inference-service), confluent-kafka-python consumer (ingestion-consumer). ML: PyTorch 2.3 for LSTM pressure time-series model, scikit-learn IsolationForest for multivariate scoring. LLM: AWS Bedrock GPT-4o for regulatory report generation. Data: TimescaleDB 2.14 on RDS with continuous aggregates, ElastiCache Redis 7 cluster mode, MSK Kafka 3.6 with 3-broker cluster. Infrastructure: EKS 1.30 multi-AZ, ALB with AWS WAF, NLB for WebSocket, Cognito user pools with MFA, S3 for model artifacts and raw telemetry archive. Observability: Datadog APM, PagerDuty. CI/CD: GitHub Actions, Helm, Argo Rollouts.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Meter Telemetry Ingestion: MQTT-to-Kafka bridge normalizing NB-IoT payloads; ingestion-consumer writing to TimescaleDB with per-partition offset tracking. Anomaly Scoring Engine: LSTM model scoring pressure trend per meter; IsolationForest for multivariate (pressure, flow, battery) outlier detection; scores published to anomaly_events Kafka topic. Leakage Pattern Confirmation: sliding 5-minute window checking for 2+ adjacent meters in same network_segment above anomaly_threshold before incident creation. Segment Isolation: incident_commander-role-gated REST endpoint sending mTLS valve controller command; append-only isolation_commands audit log. Field Crew Dispatch: PagerDuty API call with meter GPS coordinates, affected segment code, and anomaly type; crew acknowledgment tracked in crew_dispatches table. LLM Incident Reporting: Bedrock GPT-4o drafts regulatory narrative from incident timeline JSON; queued for human reviewer approval before submission. Operations Admin Console: Grafana dashboards with custom incident management plugin for alert acknowledgment and threshold configuration.
target_output.system_components: MSK Kafka 3-broker cluster: topics meter_telemetry (72h retention, 12 partitions), inference_queue (12 partitions), anomaly_events (24 partitions), incident_commands. ingestion-consumer EKS deployment (3 replicas): Kafka consumer group, TimescaleDB writer with batch inserts. inference-service EKS deployment (6 replicas, GPU node pool): FastAPI + PyTorch LSTM + IsolationForest, Redis model cache. incident-api EKS deployment (3 replicas): FastAPI, LeakagePatternConfirmer, SegmentIsolationOrchestrator, CrewDispatcher, aiosqlite audit writer. llm-reporter EKS deployment (2 replicas): Bedrock API client, report template renderer. TimescaleDB RDS Multi-AZ with read replica for analytics queries. ElastiCache Redis 7 cluster mode for anomaly score deduplication and model cache. S3 buckets: model-artifacts (versioned), telemetry-archive (Glacier lifecycle after 90 days).
target_output.workflows: Meter Reading to Incident (happy path): meter transmits NB-IoT payload containing meter_serial, pressure_bar=0.02 (leak-level), flow_m3h=0.0, tamper_flag=false; MQTT bridge publishes to meter_telemetry Kafka topic; ingestion-consumer writes pressure_readings row to TimescaleDB and enqueues reading to inference_queue; inference-service scores reading with LSTM (pressure trend deviation) and IsolationForest (multivariate), produces anomaly_score=0.94 above threshold 0.85, publishes to anomaly_events; incident-api LeakagePatternConfirmer checks anomaly_events for adjacent meters in same network_segment within 5-minute window, finds meter GAS-4471 and GAS-4472 both scoring >0.85, creates flow_anomalies record and incident record; SegmentIsolationOrchestrator blocks on incident_commander approval (MVP manual gate), ops analyst acknowledges in Grafana plugin, incident_commander executes POST /incidents/INC-0042/isolate, isolation command sent to segment valve controller via mTLS, isolation_commands row appended; CrewDispatcher calls PagerDuty API with GPS coordinates of GAS-4471 and GAS-4472 and segment code SEG-17. LLM Report Generation: after incident_status=resolved, llm-reporter consumes incident event, calls Bedrock GPT-4o with incident timeline JSON including anomaly timestamps, pressure readings, isolation actions, and crew dispatch times; GPT-4o returns structured narrative; llm-reporter creates regulatory_report record with status=pending_review; safety_engineer reviews and approves in admin console.
target_output.data_model: gas_meters (id UUID PK, meter_serial TEXT UNIQUE, network_segment_id UUID FK, gps_lat NUMERIC, gps_lon NUMERIC, installation_date DATE, status TEXT CHECK(status IN ('active','decommissioned','suspended'))). pressure_readings (time TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL, meter_id UUID NOT NULL, pressure_bar NUMERIC NOT NULL, flow_m3h NUMERIC, battery_pct SMALLINT) [TimescaleDB hypertable, partition by week and network_segment_id]. flow_anomalies (id UUID PK, meter_id UUID FK, detected_at TIMESTAMPTZ, anomaly_type TEXT CHECK(anomaly_type IN ('leakage','pressure_drop','tamper','flow_reversal')), anomaly_score NUMERIC, contributing_meters UUID[]). network_segments (id UUID PK, segment_code TEXT UNIQUE, valve_controller_endpoint TEXT, district_zone TEXT, isolation_status TEXT CHECK(isolation_status IN ('normal','isolated','degraded'))). tamper_events (id UUID PK, meter_id UUID FK, event_type TEXT, detected_at TIMESTAMPTZ, acknowledged_by UUID). crew_dispatches (id UUID PK, incident_id UUID FK, crew_id TEXT, dispatched_at TIMESTAMPTZ, ack_at TIMESTAMPTZ, resolved_at TIMESTAMPTZ). isolation_commands (id UUID PK, segment_id UUID FK, commanded_by UUID, commanded_at TIMESTAMPTZ, authorization_role TEXT, justification TEXT, rollback_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: REST API at /api/v1 with OpenAPI 3.0. GET /incidents?status=active&segment_id=X: paginated active incident list for ops_center_analyst. GET /incidents/{id}: full incident detail including anomaly timeline and contributing meters. POST /incidents/{id}/isolate (incident_commander): sends mTLS isolation command to valve controller, appends isolation_commands row. POST /incidents/{id}/dispatch-crew (dispatcher): triggers PagerDuty with crew assignment and GPS coordinates. GET /incidents/{id}/report: returns LLM-generated regulatory report draft with status. PATCH /incidents/{id}/report (safety_engineer): approves or rejects LLM report draft. GET /meters/{id}/readings?start=T&end=T: time-range pressure readings from TimescaleDB. WebSocket /ws/alerts: live anomaly event stream for Grafana real-time panel. Auth: Cognito JWT validated at ALB and re-validated in FastAPI role guard.
target_output.security_and_compliance: VPC private subnets with no public IP on any EKS pod; ALB with AWS WAF (SQLi, XSS, rate limiting 500 req/min per IP) as sole ingress. Cognito MFA mandatory for all five roles; incident_commander role requires hardware MFA token enrollment. TLS 1.3 for all internal service-to-service calls; mTLS client certificates for valve controller API communication. TimescaleDB AES-256 encryption at rest via RDS KMS. Isolation commands require incident_commander JWT claim and are logged to append-only isolation_commands table with justification field. CloudTrail captures all AWS API calls. Datadog log aggregation with 7-year S3 archive meets national gas safety authority retention requirements. SOC 2 Type II controls implemented: access reviews quarterly, penetration test annually, change management gate for model deployments.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: EKS multi-AZ cluster (us-east-1a/1b/1c) with separate node groups: general (ingestion-consumer, incident-api, llm-reporter) and gpu (inference-service on g4dn.xlarge nodes). MSK 3-broker cluster with replication factor 3 and min.insync.replicas=2. RDS TimescaleDB Multi-AZ with automated failover and daily snapshots to S3. Helm charts for each service with values files per environment (dev/staging/production). GitHub Actions CI: pytest unit tests, testcontainers Kafka integration tests, Docker build and ECR push. Argo Rollouts canary deployment for inference-service: 10% traffic shift, automated rollback if anomaly_score_p99 increases >15% or error rate >1% during analysis window. Rollback: Argo Rollouts abort command reverts to previous image in under 90 seconds. Secrets: AWS Secrets Manager with 30-day auto-rotation for DB credentials.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM tracing across all four EKS services with correlation IDs propagated via Kafka message headers. Custom metrics pushed to Datadog: kafka_consumer_lag_seconds (alert if >60s), anomaly_score_p99 (alert if >0.95 sustained 5 min indicating potential mass event), isolation_command_count_hourly, false_isolation_rate_7d (alert if >0.1%), crew_dispatch_ack_time_s (SLA 5 minutes). PagerDuty escalation policy: consumer lag >60s pages on-call DevOps; confirmed incident with no crew dispatch ack within 5 minutes pages incident_commander. Grafana dashboards: network-wide anomaly heatmap by segment, real-time pressure/flow time-series per meter, incident timeline, crew dispatch status board.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $8,500/month for 50,000 meters at steady state. MSK 3-broker kafka.m5.xlarge: $1,200/month. EKS node groups (6 inference + 8 general nodes): $3,500/month. RDS TimescaleDB db.r6g.2xlarge Multi-AZ: $2,200/month. ElastiCache Redis r6g.large cluster: $600/month. Bedrock GPT-4o: ~$200/month at 500 incidents/day. Scaling: EKS HPA on kafka_consumer_lag for ingestion-consumer (2-20 replicas); inference-service scales on GPU utilization (3-12 replicas). At 200,000 meters, add 2 MSK brokers and scale RDS to r6g.4xlarge.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Pilot (8 weeks): ingestion-consumer and TimescaleDB schema for 5,000-meter pilot segment; threshold-based leakage detection (no ML); PagerDuty crew paging; manual isolation workflow via admin console; Datadog dashboards. Phase 2 ML Integration (6 weeks): LSTM and IsolationForest anomaly models trained on Phase 1 data; inference-service deployment; LeakagePatternConfirmer multi-meter confirmation logic; canary rollout via Argo Rollouts. Phase 3 Automation (4 weeks): automated isolation command flow with incident_commander approval gate; LLM regulatory report generation via Bedrock; SOC 2 controls documentation; full 50,000-meter network rollout. Phase 4 Advanced (ongoing): predictive corrosion risk scoring; GIS topology integration; mobile field crew app.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with mypy strict mode and ruff linting in CI. All Kafka consumer groups use exactly-once semantics for TimescaleDB writes. ML model updates require canary deployment with Argo Rollouts analysis template validating false_isolation_rate before full promotion. LLM-generated regulatory reports must be stored with model version and prompt hash for audit trail. Isolation commands require idempotency key to prevent duplicate valve actuations on API retry. Integration tests use testcontainers with real Kafka and TimescaleDB instances, not mocks. PR requires two approvals for any change to AnomalyScorer thresholds or SegmentIsolationOrchestrator logic.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: LSTM model drift over seasonal pressure pattern changes could increase false negative rate in winter; mitigated by monthly retraining trigger on rolling 30-day dataset with accuracy regression gate before deployment. Risk: valve controller API downtime during isolation command prevents automated response; mitigated by circuit breaker with manual fallback workflow and PagerDuty alert for failed isolation. Risk: Bedrock GPT-4o regulatory report hallucination; mitigated by mandatory human review gate before submission and structured prompt constraining output to incident timeline facts only. Tradeoff: multi-meter confirmation requirement reduces false isolations below 0.1% but may add 2-3 minutes to detection time for isolated single-meter leaks; acceptable per safety engineering review. Tradeoff: TimescaleDB over InfluxDB chosen for ACID compliance needed by isolation_commands audit trail.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected TimescaleDB over InfluxDB for combined time-series and relational audit trail requirements. Chose MSK over self-managed Kafka for automated broker replacement during AZ failures without operational intervention. mTLS chosen over API key authentication for valve controller communication to prevent credential compromise from enabling unauthorized network isolation. GPT-4o via Bedrock selected over self-hosted LLM to avoid GPU resource contention with inference-service.

metadata.notes: Safety-critical gas distribution AI with Kafka, TimescaleDB, LSTM anomaly scoring, multi-meter leakage confirmation, and LLM regulatory reporting on AWS EKS.

profile.domain: IoT Edge MQTT Broker Performance Profiling

project_goal.value: Continuously profile MQTT broker performance on IoT edge gateways, identify slow subscribers, topic hot spots, and connection storms, then automatically adjust broker configuration parameters to maintain message throughput targets
project_goal.rationale: Core automation objective to reduce manual broker tuning on edge gateway fleet

target_users.value: IoT platform engineers and DevOps operators managing MQTT broker fleets on edge gateways across production sites
target_users.rationale: Internal engineering team identified as primary operators

project_class.rationale: Polling, analysis, and automated configuration adjustment workflow not a user-facing application

capabilities.rationale: Profiling backend, broker management API integrations, scheduled batch analysis jobs

complexity_level.rationale: Go REST API with broker management integrations and InfluxDB time-series storage

risk_level.rationale: Automated configuration changes to production MQTT brokers carry operational risk

data_sensitivity.rationale: Broker topology and throughput metrics are internal operational data

external_exposure.rationale: Runs on edge servers in internal production network, no external exposure

access_model.value: Custom JWT authentication for tool management API; broker management credentials stored in local secrets file
access_model.rationale: Internal tool with single operator role per edge server

feature_scope.value: Per-broker metric collection via EMQX management API, InfluxDB time-series storage, hot spot detection, automated configuration adjustment with stabilization wait, Grafana dashboards, alert rules for throughput degradation
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for autonomous broker management

mvp_scope.value: Metric collection from single EMQX broker, InfluxDB storage, manual threshold-based config adjustment trigger, Grafana dashboard
mvp_scope.rationale: Validates integration with EMQX management API before multi-broker fleet management

security_baseline.value: JWT auth on management API, broker credentials in encrypted secrets file, TLS for InfluxDB connections, no external network exposure
security_baseline.rationale: Internal tool security controls appropriate for medium-risk internal automation

frontend_stack.value: None; Grafana dashboards for monitoring and alerting
frontend_stack.rationale: Automation tool with no interactive UI; Grafana provides sufficient operator visibility

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with Gin HTTP framework
backend_stack.rationale: Go performance and small binary size appropriate for edge server deployment

data_platform.value: InfluxDB 2 for time-series broker metrics; no relational database required
data_platform.rationale: Time-series database optimally suited for per-topic throughput trend analysis

hosting_target.value: Docker Compose on edge servers running Ubuntu 22.04 alongside EMQX broker
hosting_target.rationale: Colocated deployment minimizes network latency for management API polling

privacy_retention_policy.value: Broker metrics retained 90 days in InfluxDB; configuration adjustment logs retained 1 year for operational audit
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Sufficient for trend analysis and incident investigation

future_scope.value: Multi-broker fleet management with central aggregation; support for VerneMQ and Mosquitto broker types beyond EMQX
future_scope.rationale: Fleet-wide management planned after single-broker MVP validation

constraints.value: Profiling poll interval 60 seconds; configuration adjustment requires 5-minute stabilization wait before next change; must not overload EMQX management API (max 10 req/min)
constraints.rationale: EMQX management API rate limits and operational safety for production brokers

observability_baseline.value: Grafana dashboards on InfluxDB; Prometheus metrics endpoint on profiler service; alert rules for throughput degradation > 20% from baseline
observability_baseline.rationale: Standard internal tooling observability requirements

execution_preference.value: Continuous daemon with 60-second poll loop; Docker Compose managed service restart on failure
execution_preference.rationale: Always-on automation tool not a scheduled batch job

project.goal: Automated MQTT broker performance profiling and configuration tuning

backend.api: REST management API for manual trigger and status

security.transport: TLS to InfluxDB and EMQX management API

reasoner_reviews.product: The automation scope is well-defined: poll EMQX management API, store metrics, detect hot spots, apply configuration changes with stabilization guard. The 5-minute stabilization wait is an important safety mechanism for production brokers.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: A Go daemon is appropriate here but the design needs careful attention to the configuration adjustment state machine: the stabilization timer must prevent cascading config changes when the broker is under stress. InfluxDB continuous queries can efficiently detect the 90th percentile baseline exceedance without custom aggregation code.
reasoner_reviews.security: Broker management credentials must never be stored in plain text. The automated configuration adjustment path should require a dry-run mode for initial deployment and a maximum change count per hour to limit blast radius from a runaway tuning loop.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 10 req/min EMQX management API limit with a 60-second poll interval means the profiler can poll at most 10 brokers simultaneously. Multi-broker fleet management in future scope will require a staggered polling scheduler.

backend.service_design: Single Go binary with three goroutines: MetricsPoller (60s ticker calling EMQX HTTP management API), HotSpotAnalyzer (processes new InfluxDB data to detect topic throughput anomalies), ConfigAdjuster (state machine with stabilization timer applying broker config changes via EMQX management API)
backend.api_patterns: REST API via Gin: GET /status (current profiler state and last poll timestamp), GET /brokers/{id}/metrics (recent metric summary), POST /brokers/{id}/adjust (manual config adjustment trigger, dry-run param), GET /adjustments (adjustment history)
backend.business_modules: EMQXClient wrapping EMQX HTTP management API calls for topic stats, subscriber counts, and config updates; MetricsWriter batching InfluxDB line protocol writes; AdjustmentRuleEngine evaluating ranked rule set for config changes; StabilizationGuard enforcing 5-minute cool-down between adjustments
backend.failure_handling: EMQX management API timeouts retried with exponential backoff up to 3 attempts; InfluxDB write failures buffered in memory ring buffer up to 1000 points; adjustment rule evaluation errors logged and skipped without applying any change

security.auth_design: Custom JWT for profiler management REST API using HMAC-SHA256 with shared secret; EMQX API key stored in sops-encrypted secrets.yaml
security.secrets_management: EMQX API credentials loaded from sops-encrypted YAML at startup; never logged or exposed in API responses
security.security_risks: Runaway config adjustment loop risk if HotSpotAnalyzer produces false positives; mitigated by StabilizationGuard and max_adjustments_per_hour limit configurable in config.yaml

data.entities: broker_instances (id, hostname, emqx_version, management_api_url, registered_at), topic_metrics (measurement, tags: broker_id, topic, fields: msg_rate_in, msg_rate_out, subscriber_count, retained_count, timestamp), subscriber_sessions (measurement, tags: broker_id, client_id, fields: recv_msg_rate, send_msg_rate, queue_depth, connected_at), config_adjustments (measurement, tags: broker_id, param_name, fields: old_value, new_value, trigger_rule, applied_at), performance_baselines (id, broker_id, topic, p90_msg_rate, computed_at)
data.storage_design: InfluxDB 2 bucket 'broker_metrics' with 90-day retention; separate bucket 'adjustment_log' with 1-year retention for config_adjustments; continuous aggregate for hourly topic throughput statistics
data.data_risks: InfluxDB local instance on edge server has no replication; data loss on server failure. Acceptable for operational metrics; critical adjustment_log entries also written to local SQLite as backup.

devops.deployment_topology: Docker Compose on each edge server: profiler service (Go binary), InfluxDB 2, Grafana. Profiler container has read access to EMQX management API on host network. No Kubernetes; edge servers run minimal Docker daemon.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: go vet, golangci-lint, go test with mock EMQX server (httptest), Docker build and push to internal registry, integration test with testcontainers InfluxDB
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus metrics endpoint at /metrics on profiler: profiler_poll_latency_ms, profiler_adjustment_count_total, profiler_emqx_api_errors_total; Grafana dashboards: topic throughput heatmap, subscriber session count, adjustment history timeline
devops.devops_risks: Edge server disk space exhaustion from InfluxDB growth; mitigated by 90-day retention policy and disk space alert in Grafana

target_output.thinking_summary: INTENTIONAL FLAWS: (1) plan technology_stack and architecture_overview describe a Python Flask implementation instead of the contracted Go 1.22 with Gin, creating a tech stack mismatch; (2) data_model section uses generic Users and Orders entities instead of domain-specific broker_instances, topic_metrics, and config_adjustments; (3) phased_implementation is boilerplate not specific to the MQTT broker profiling domain.
target_output.title: MQTT Broker Performance Profiler and Auto-Tuner
target_output.executive_summary: An automation tool that profiles EMQX MQTT broker performance on IoT edge gateways by polling per-topic message rates, subscriber session depths, and connection counts every 60 seconds, detecting hot spots and automatically applying configuration adjustments with a 5-minute stabilization guard to maintain throughput targets. Metrics are stored in InfluxDB and visualized in Grafana dashboards.
target_output.architecture_overview: A Python Flask application runs as a Docker Compose service on each edge server alongside EMQX and InfluxDB. Three background threads handle metric polling, hot spot analysis, and config adjustment. The Flask REST API exposes status and manual trigger endpoints. This design provides a simple polling loop that connects to the EMQX management API and writes metrics to InfluxDB.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.11 with Flask 3.0 and APScheduler for background polling tasks. EMQX integration: requests library for HTTP management API calls. Data: InfluxDB 2 with influxdb-client Python library. Configuration: PyYAML for config.yaml parsing. Deployment: Docker Compose with Python 3.11-slim image. Monitoring: Prometheus client for Flask metrics endpoint. CI: GitHub Actions with pytest.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Metric Collection: polls EMQX management API every 60 seconds for per-topic message rates, subscriber counts, and connection statistics. Hot Spot Detection: identifies topics where current msg_rate exceeds 90th percentile baseline by 50%. Config Adjustment Engine: evaluates ranked adjustment rules (connection backlog, QoS retry limits, message queue size) and applies highest-priority eligible change. Stabilization Guard: enforces 5-minute cool-down between adjustments and max 12 adjustments per hour. REST API: status, manual trigger, and adjustment history endpoints.
target_output.system_components: Flask application container with APScheduler (MetricsPoller job, HotSpotAnalyzer job, ConfigAdjuster state machine). EMQXClient module wrapping HTTP management API. InfluxDB 2 container for metrics storage. Grafana container for dashboards. Local SQLite for adjustment_log backup. Docker Compose network bridge connecting profiler to EMQX management API on host.
target_output.workflows: Metric Collection Cycle: APScheduler triggers MetricsPoller every 60 seconds; poller calls EMQX GET /api/v5/topics, GET /api/v5/clients, and GET /api/v5/stats; normalizes responses and batch-writes to InfluxDB broker_metrics bucket via line protocol. Hot Spot Detection: HotSpotAnalyzer queries InfluxDB continuous aggregate for p90 per topic, compares against latest 60-second sample, marks topics exceeding 150% of p90 as hot spots and writes to hot_spot_events measurement. Configuration Adjustment: ConfigAdjuster checks StabilizationGuard (5-minute elapsed since last change and max_adjustments_per_hour not exceeded), evaluates AdjustmentRuleEngine against current hot_spot_events, applies highest-priority rule via EMQX PUT /api/v5/configs, records change in InfluxDB adjustment_log bucket and SQLite backup.
target_output.data_model: The primary relational data entities include Users, Orders, and Products. Users have a standard one-to-many relationship with Orders. Products are associated with Orders via a junction table. The system also maintains a sessions table for authentication state and a settings table for configuration values.
target_output.api_design: REST API via Flask. GET /status: returns profiler daemon state, last poll timestamp, active broker count, and recent hot spot count. GET /brokers/{broker_id}/metrics?minutes=60: returns recent topic throughput summary from InfluxDB query. POST /brokers/{broker_id}/adjust: manually triggers adjustment evaluation for specified broker; accepts dry_run=true param to preview without applying. GET /adjustments?broker_id=X&limit=50: returns adjustment history from InfluxDB adjustment_log. Auth: Bearer JWT in Authorization header validated against shared HMAC-SHA256 secret.
target_output.security_and_compliance: EMQX management API key stored in sops-encrypted secrets.yaml mounted as Docker Compose secret. JWT shared secret for management REST API in same secrets file. InfluxDB bound to Docker Compose internal network, not exposed to host. TLS configured on InfluxDB connection. No external network exposure from edge server. Dry-run mode available on all adjustment endpoints. Max 12 config adjustments per hour enforced by StabilizationGuard to limit blast radius.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Docker Compose file defines three services: profiler (Python Flask image), influxdb (influxdb:2.7), grafana (grafana/grafana:10). docker compose up -d on each edge server. Environment variables in .env file for broker endpoint, InfluxDB credentials, and JWT secret. GitHub Actions CI: pytest unit tests with mock EMQX server via responses library, Docker build, push to internal registry. No Kubernetes; edge servers run minimal Docker daemon on Ubuntu 22.04. Rollback: docker compose pull previous_tag && docker compose up -d.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics endpoint at /metrics: profiler_poll_duration_seconds histogram, profiler_adjustment_count_total counter, profiler_emqx_api_error_total counter, profiler_hot_spot_topic_count gauge. Grafana dashboards: topic_throughput_heatmap showing msg_rate_in per topic over time, subscriber_session_depth bar chart per client_id, adjustment_history_timeline showing rule name and parameter changed, hot_spot_alert_panel with configurable threshold line.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero cloud cost; runs on owned edge server hardware. Python Flask image ~150 MB; InfluxDB container ~300 MB disk at 90-day retention for single broker. APScheduler background threads handle single-broker polling within 2-second wall-clock budget per 60-second cycle. For multi-broker fleet management, the polling architecture will need to move to a goroutine-per-broker model with staggered 6-second intervals to stay within EMQX 10 req/min limit per broker.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase one deploys the MQTT broker profiler as a Docker Compose service on each edge gateway, enabling live metric collection from EMQX, automated hot-spot detection for slow subscribers and overloaded topics, and immediate configuration adjustment to maintain message throughput targets. Phase two adds additional important features to improve the product. Phase three focuses on optimization and scaling to support more users and higher load. Each phase delivers incremental business value and builds on the previous phase.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.11 with pylint and black formatting in CI. Unit tests for AdjustmentRuleEngine and HotSpotAnalyzer using pytest with 80% coverage gate. EMQX management API interactions tested with responses library mock. config.yaml schema validated with pydantic on startup. Never log EMQX API credentials even at DEBUG level. Dry-run mode must be tested in CI integration test before any adjustment code path change.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: EMQX management API rate limit violation if poll interval reduced below 60 seconds during debugging; mitigated by configurable rate_limit_per_min guard in EMQXClient. Risk: InfluxDB disk exhaustion on edge server with limited storage; mitigated by 90-day retention and Grafana disk space alert. Tradeoff: Python Flask chosen for rapid development but Go binary would be more appropriate for edge server resource constraints per contract specification; this creates a technology mismatch with the contracted backend_stack. Tradeoff: APScheduler background threads in Flask are not the most robust concurrency model; goroutines would provide better error isolation.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected InfluxDB over PostgreSQL because broker metric workload is pure time-series with no relational query requirements. Docker Compose chosen over standalone binary to colocate InfluxDB and Grafana dependencies without requiring Kubernetes on edge servers. sops chosen over Vault for secrets management to avoid additional infrastructure dependency on edge servers.

metadata.notes: Intentionally flawed: plan uses Python Flask instead of contracted Go 1.22 Gin; data_model uses generic Users/Orders/Products; phased_implementation is boilerplate.

project.goal: Public documentation site for IoT microservices reference architecture

frontend.styling: Docusaurus default theme with custom CSS for code blocks
frontend.state: No client-side state; static rendering only

reasoner_reviews.product: Documentation scope is clear and well-bounded for the initial release: 8 core service boundary pages, MQTT topic schema reference, REST and gRPC contract documentation, deployment topology diagrams, and ADR index. Versioned docs support is important given the reference architecture will have multiple releases.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Docusaurus 3 is the right choice: built-in versioning, Algolia DocSearch integration, MDX for interactive Mermaid diagrams in service topology pages. The ADR index with status badges should be generated from MDX front-matter metadata at build time rather than maintained manually to prevent staleness. All architecture diagrams must be Mermaid for git-diffability; binary image files should not be committed.
reasoner_reviews.security: Static site with no user accounts has minimal attack surface. Netlify CSP headers should block inline scripts and restrict frame ancestors to prevent clickjacking. No cookies beyond Netlify Analytics aggregate tracking which is cookie-free.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 2-minute build time constraint requires Docusaurus build optimization: enable swcMinify, use Docusaurus caching plugin, and avoid heavy MDX components that trigger full React SSR renders. With 8 service pages plus schema reference, build time should comfortably stay under 90 seconds.

frontend.app_structure: Docusaurus 3 project at repository root with docs/ directory organized by domain: docs/services/ (one folder per microservice), docs/mqtt-schemas/ (topic reference), docs/api-contracts/ (REST and gRPC), docs/deployment/ (topology diagrams and runbooks), docs/adrs/ (architecture decision records)
frontend.pages_and_flows: Landing page (docusaurus index with architecture overview diagram), Services section (sidebar with 8 service boundary pages), MQTT Reference (topic schema tables with payload examples), API Contracts (OpenAPI rendered via swagger-ui plugin, gRPC proto viewer), Deployment Topology (Mermaid diagrams for single-node, Docker Compose, Kubernetes), ADR Index (auto-generated from MDX front-matter), Runbooks (step-by-step operational procedures)
frontend.ui_modules: Custom Mermaid diagram component with dark/light mode toggle; ServiceBoundaryCard MDX component showing service name, responsibilities, dependencies, and owned MQTT topics; TopicSchemaTable MDX component rendering MQTT topic path, QoS, payload schema, and example JSON; ADRStatusBadge component deriving color from front-matter status field (proposed=yellow, accepted=green, deprecated=red)
frontend.performance_notes: swcMinify enabled in docusaurus.config.js; image assets converted to WebP with explicit width/height to prevent layout shift; Mermaid diagrams rendered client-side on first paint via lazy loading to avoid blocking initial document render; Algolia DocSearch for full-text search without self-hosted search backend

security.auth_design: No authentication; public site
security.secrets_management: Netlify Analytics site ID and Algolia DocSearch API key stored as Netlify environment variables; not committed to repository
security.security_risks: Risk of outdated dependency with CVE in Docusaurus or MDX plugin tree; mitigated by Dependabot PRs on npm packages and pinned lockfile

devops.deployment_topology: Netlify CDN global edge network; GitHub repository connected to Netlify for automatic deploy on push to main; PR preview deploys at unique Netlify URLs for contributor review
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions workflow on push and PR: npm ci, docusaurus build, link checker (lychee) for broken internal links, spell checker (cspell) for technical terminology, Netlify CLI deploy on main branch merge
devops.observability_stack: Netlify Analytics dashboard for page views, top pages, unique visitors, and bandwidth; GitHub Actions build time tracking; no error monitoring needed for static site
devops.devops_risks: Netlify free tier has 100 GB/month bandwidth limit; unlikely to be exceeded for documentation site but monitor monthly bandwidth in Netlify dashboard

target_output.thinking_summary: Static Docusaurus 3 documentation site for an IoT microservices reference architecture on Netlify. No backend, no database, no server-side runtime consistent with static_website constraints. Key decisions: MDX with custom ServiceBoundaryCard and TopicSchemaTable components for structured service docs; Mermaid for all architecture diagrams to enable git-diffable architecture reviews; ADR status badges auto-generated from front-matter to prevent manual maintenance drift; Algolia DocSearch for full-text search without infrastructure.
target_output.title: IoT Microservices Reference Architecture Documentation Site
target_output.executive_summary: A Docusaurus 3 static documentation website hosted on Netlify CDN documenting an open-source IoT microservices reference architecture. Covers service boundaries, MQTT topic schemas, REST and gRPC API contracts, Kubernetes deployment topology diagrams in Mermaid, operational runbooks, and an auto-generated ADR index. Continuous deployment from GitHub main branch with pull request preview URLs for community contributor review.
target_output.architecture_overview: Fully static Docusaurus 3 site built from Markdown and MDX source files in the git repository, with no server-side runtime or database. The build pipeline runs on GitHub Actions: npm ci installs dependencies, docusaurus build generates the static HTML/CSS/JS bundle with swcMinify and asset optimization, lychee checks for broken internal links, and cspell validates IoT terminology spelling. On merge to main the Netlify CLI deploys the bundle to Netlify's global CDN edge network. Pull request branches receive preview deploy URLs from Netlify's build hook integration. All architecture diagrams are authored in Mermaid syntax within MDX files, rendering client-side to maintain git-diffability. The ADR index page is generated at build time from front-matter status metadata in docs/adrs/*.mdx files, eliminating manual index maintenance.
target_output.technology_stack: Framework: Docusaurus 3.5 with MDX 3. React: 18 (SSR at build time, client hydration for interactive components). Plugins: docusaurus-plugin-mermaid for diagram rendering, docusaurus-plugin-openapi-docs for REST contract pages, @docusaurus/plugin-ideal-image for WebP optimization. Search: Algolia DocSearch (free for open-source). Hosting: Netlify CDN with edge caching. CI: GitHub Actions with Netlify CLI. Link checker: lychee. Spell checker: cspell with custom IoT terminology wordlist. Dependency management: npm with committed package-lock.json; Dependabot for automated security updates.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Service Boundary Documentation: one MDX page per microservice using ServiceBoundaryCard component showing service name, owned MQTT topics, REST endpoints, dependencies, and scaling notes. MQTT Topic Schema Reference: TopicSchemaTable components per topic namespace showing full topic path, QoS level, retain flag, payload JSON schema, and example payload. REST API Contract Pages: OpenAPI 3.0 YAML files rendered via docusaurus-plugin-openapi-docs with try-it-out disabled for static hosting. gRPC Proto Viewer: syntax-highlighted .proto definitions with field documentation. Deployment Topology: Mermaid architecture diagrams for single-node Docker Compose, multi-node bare-metal Kubernetes, and cloud-managed K8s configurations. ADR Index: build-time generated page listing all ADRs with status badge, title, date, and linked full text. Runbooks: step-by-step operational procedures for service restart, database migration, and certificate rotation.
target_output.system_components: Docusaurus 3 project root with docusaurus.config.js, sidebars.js, and custom theme components in src/. docs/ content directories: docs/services/ (8 service MDX pages), docs/mqtt-schemas/ (topic reference MDX), docs/api-contracts/ (OpenAPI YAML and gRPC proto files), docs/deployment/ (Mermaid topology diagrams and runbook MDX), docs/adrs/ (ADR MDX files with front-matter status). src/components/: ServiceBoundaryCard.tsx, TopicSchemaTable.tsx, ADRStatusBadge.tsx. static/ for downloadable proto files and OpenAPI YAML. netlify.toml for build settings, CSP headers, and redirect rules.
target_output.workflows: Documentation Update Workflow: contributor forks repository, edits MDX files in docs/ or adds new service page using ServiceBoundaryCard template, submits pull request; GitHub Actions runs 'npm ci && docusaurus build' to validate build succeeds, lychee checks no broken links, cspell validates IoT terminology; Netlify generates a preview deploy URL posted as PR comment; maintainer reviews content and rendered preview, approves and merges to main; GitHub Actions triggers Netlify production deploy completing in under 2 minutes. ADR Authoring Workflow: contributor creates docs/adrs/ADR-042-mqtt-qos-selection.mdx with front-matter fields (status: proposed|accepted|deprecated, date, deciders, tags); at build time the ADR index generation script reads all front-matter metadata and renders the ADR index page with ADRStatusBadge components color-coded by status; no manual index update required.
target_output.data_model: No database. Content is structured as files in the git repository. service_definitions: one MDX file per microservice at docs/services/{service-slug}/index.mdx with front-matter fields (service_name, service_type, owned_topics[], rest_base_path, grpc_package, dependencies[], complexity). mqtt_topic_schemas: MDX pages at docs/mqtt-schemas/{namespace}/index.mdx with TopicSchemaTable entries per topic path. api_contracts: OpenAPI 3.0 YAML at docs/api-contracts/{service}/openapi.yaml. architecture_decisions: MDX at docs/adrs/ADR-{nnn}-{slug}.mdx with front-matter (status, date, deciders[], tags[]). runbooks: MDX at docs/deployment/runbooks/{procedure-slug}/index.mdx.
target_output.api_design: No server-side API. Static assets only. Netlify redirects configured in netlify.toml: /api-docs/* redirects to /docs/api-contracts/* for legacy URL compatibility. Algolia DocSearch crawler indexes the public site; search queries resolved client-side via Algolia hosted index. All downloadable assets (OpenAPI YAML, .proto files) served as static files from static/ with correct Content-Type headers set via netlify.toml headers rules.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HTTPS enforced by Netlify CDN; HTTP redirects to HTTPS automatically. CSP headers in netlify.toml: Content-Security-Policy restricts script-src to self and Algolia DocSearch CDN, frame-ancestors to none preventing clickjacking, object-src to none. X-Frame-Options DENY header. Netlify Analytics is cookie-free aggregate tracking; no GDPR consent banner required. No user-generated content, no authentication, no server-side attack surface. Dependabot monitors npm dependency tree for CVEs with automated PRs targeting patch and minor updates.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: netlify.toml defines build command 'npm run build' (docusaurus build), publish directory 'build', and Node 20 runtime. GitHub Actions workflow 'ci.yml': on push and pull_request triggers, runs 'npm ci', 'npm run build', lychee link check, cspell spell check. On push to main, additional 'deploy.yml' workflow runs 'netlify deploy --prod' using NETLIFY_AUTH_TOKEN secret. PR branches get preview deploys from Netlify webhook integration without needing GitHub Actions. Build cache: Docusaurus build cache stored in GitHub Actions cache keyed on package-lock.json hash, reducing incremental build time from 90 seconds to under 30 seconds for content-only changes.
target_output.observability: Netlify Analytics dashboard shows daily unique visitors, page view counts per URL, top referral sources, and bandwidth consumed. GitHub Actions build log captures docusaurus build output including page count and bundle size per run. Netlify deploy log records deploy duration and CDN invalidation confirmation. Top-pages metric in Netlify Analytics surfaces which service boundary or schema pages get most traffic, informing documentation investment priorities. No error monitoring required; static file serving failures are handled by Netlify CDN edge with automatic retry.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero hosting cost on Netlify free tier (100 GB/month bandwidth, 300 build minutes/month). GitHub Actions free tier covers CI (2,000 minutes/month). Algolia DocSearch is free for open-source projects. Build time under 90 seconds leaves ample GitHub Actions budget for active contribution periods. If bandwidth exceeds 100 GB/month (indicating high adoption), upgrade to Netlify Pro at $19/month. No scaling decisions required for static file serving.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Initial Release (2 weeks): Docusaurus 3 project scaffold, 8 service boundary pages using ServiceBoundaryCard, MQTT topic schema reference for all core topics, single-node and Kubernetes deployment topology Mermaid diagrams, GitHub Actions CI with build and link check, Netlify deploy. Phase 2 API Contracts (1 week): OpenAPI 3.0 YAML integration via docusaurus-plugin-openapi-docs for all 8 services, gRPC proto viewer pages, Algolia DocSearch integration. Phase 3 ADR and Runbooks (1 week): ADR front-matter schema, build-time ADR index generation script, ADRStatusBadge component, operational runbook pages for core maintenance procedures. Phase 4 Versioning (ongoing): docusaurus versioning for reference architecture v1.0 release tag; version selector in navigation header.
target_output.development_guidelines: All architecture diagrams authored in Mermaid inside MDX code fences; no binary image files committed to repository. ServiceBoundaryCard and TopicSchemaTable MDX components must pass TypeScript strict type check (tsc --noEmit in CI). ADR front-matter schema enforced via custom Docusaurus plugin that fails build on missing required fields. Contributor guide documents MDX component usage with copy-paste templates for new service pages. Spell checker cspell wordlist in cspell.json maintained with IoT-specific terms (MQTT, EMQX, LoRaWAN, gRPC, mTLS, NB-IoT). PR description must link to rendered Netlify preview URL.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Algolia DocSearch crawler index lag means new pages are unsearchable for up to 24 hours after deploy; acceptable for documentation site; document in contributing guide. Risk: Mermaid diagram rendering adds 150 KB of client-side JavaScript; mitigated by lazy loading mermaid.js only on pages with diagrams via Docusaurus plugin configuration. Tradeoff: Docusaurus over plain HTML for built-in versioning and sidebar navigation; adds Node.js build toolchain dependency but saves significant custom development. Tradeoff: Netlify over GitHub Pages for PR preview deploy capability which is essential for community contributor review workflow.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Netlify over GitHub Pages for pull request preview deployment URLs enabling community contributor review without merging. Chose Mermaid over draw.io XML for architecture diagrams to enable git-diffable topology reviews in pull requests. ADR index auto-generated from front-matter rather than manually maintained to prevent documentation drift as architecture evolves. Algolia DocSearch chosen over Docusaurus local search plugin for better relevance ranking across large documentation sets.

metadata.notes: Docusaurus 3 static documentation site for IoT microservices reference architecture on Netlify CDN with Mermaid diagrams and auto-generated ADR index.

project_goal.value: Modernize legacy proprietary BACnet building automation systems by discovering devices, extracting point configurations from aging controllers, and auto-generating equivalent configurations for a modern open-source BACnet/IP stack with migration validation reports
project_goal.rationale: Building portfolio owner needs to migrate off unsupported proprietary BACnet controllers before vendor EOL

target_users.value: Building automation engineers and facilities management teams executing BACnet controller migrations
target_users.rationale: Domain experts who understand BACnet object models and controller configuration formats

project_class.rationale: Batch discovery and configuration migration workflow with no user-facing UI

capabilities.rationale: BACnet network integration, batch snapshot processing, PostgreSQL persistence for migration state

complexity_level.rationale: BACnet protocol implementation, multi-device async discovery, parallel config validation against live systems

risk_level.rationale: Tool runs on building OT network with access to live controller configurations; misconfigured migration could affect HVAC and lighting control

data_sensitivity.rationale: Controller configurations and point mappings are internal operational data

external_exposure.rationale: Tool runs on building management workstation on isolated OT network segment

access_model.value: Custom JWT with migration_engineer and read_only_auditor roles
access_model.rationale: Multiple engineers may run migrations; auditors need read-only report access without write permissions

feature_scope.value: BACnet WhoIs device discovery, object list extraction via ReadProperty, legacy snapshot persistence, modern BACnet/IP config generation, 24-hour side-by-side validation, point-by-point match reports with configurable pass/fail threshold
feature_scope.rationale: Full migration lifecycle from discovery through formal sign-off

mvp_scope.value: Device discovery, snapshot capture, modern config generation, and validation report with configurable match threshold for Siemens PXC controller family
mvp_scope.rationale: Target the most common controller brand in the portfolio first

security_baseline.value: JWT-based CLI auth with session binding, PostgreSQL credentials in Docker secrets, TLS for REST API endpoints, Fernet-encrypted keystore for BACnet device credentials
security_baseline.rationale: OT network perimeter handles isolation; application layer handles identity and credential storage

frontend_stack.value: None - CLI and REST API only, no web frontend
frontend_stack.rationale: automation_tool project class; engineers interact via command-line

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with asyncio; BAC0 library for BACnet/IP communication; FastAPI for report REST endpoints
backend_stack.rationale: BAC0 is the mature Python BACnet client; asyncio enables parallel multi-device reads

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 with JSONB for device object properties
data_platform.rationale: Relational model fits device-point hierarchy; JSONB handles BACnet property variability without column explosion

hosting_target.value: Docker Compose on air-gapped building management workstation; GitLab CI for automated regression tests
hosting_target.rationale: OT network isolation requires on-premises deployment; Docker provides reproducible runtime

privacy_retention_policy.value: Migration snapshots retained 24 months for change management audit trail; validation reports retained indefinitely
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Change management policy requires post-migration audit trail for facility certifications

future_scope.value: Support for Johnson Controls N2 and Honeywell Excel controller families; scheduled incremental snapshot diffing for post-migration drift detection
future_scope.rationale: Expand controller coverage after Siemens PXC MVP validates the migration pipeline

constraints.value: Must run on air-gapped OT network; discovery must not disrupt live building operations; BACnet reads throttled per device model to avoid overloading aging controller CPUs
constraints.rationale: OT network safety requirements; legacy PXC100 CPUs saturate at 10 concurrent APDUs

observability_baseline.value: Prometheus metrics scraped by local Grafana stack; structured JSON logs to file for offline audit review
observability_baseline.rationale: Air-gapped environment precludes cloud monitoring; Grafana pre-deployed on workstation

execution_preference.value: Sprint-based with bi-weekly milestones tied to controller migration sign-off sessions with facilities team
execution_preference.rationale: Migration progress gated by facilities team availability for live validation windows

project.goal: Migrate BACnet controllers from proprietary to open-source stack with formal validation

backend.api: FastAPI REST for report retrieval

devops.ci: GitLab CI with BAC0 simulator fixtures

constraints.disruption: zero live system interruption during discovery

reasoner_reviews.product: The phased approach starting with Siemens PXC controllers is sound given they represent the majority of the portfolio. The 24-hour parallel validation window is appropriate for HVAC-critical points. The new configurable match threshold closes the gap identified in round 1 and enables the tool to produce formal sign-off documentation.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: asyncio with BAC0 and per-device semaphores sized from device_model_profiles is the right fix for the concurrency issue. The JSONB schema for object properties correctly handles BACnet type variability. The MigrationStateMachine class enforcing explicit state transitions is a sound correctness pattern for an OT tool where mistakes affect live building systems.
reasoner_reviews.security: JWT tokens bound to migration session UUID prevent stale token replay across buildings. Fernet-encrypted keystore is appropriate for air-gapped environments where HashiCorp Vault is unavailable. The migration_audit table with artifact checksums satisfies ASHRAE 135 change management traceability requirements.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Per-device-model semaphore with device_model_profiles seed data for PXC100 (10), PXC200 (25), and N2 (8) directly addresses the OT controller saturation risk. Default profile of 5 concurrent APDUs for unrecognized models is conservative and safe.

backend.service_design: Three Python modules: BACnetDiscovery (asyncio WhoIs broadcast, per-device ReadProperty batches using per-model semaphore from device_model_profiles), MigrationPlanner (object-type handlers for AI/AO/AV/BI/BO/BV/MSI/MSO translating to modern BACnet/IP YAML format), ValidationRunner (parallel live reads from legacy and modern controller endpoints for 24-hour soak, match scoring against configurable threshold)
backend.api_patterns: FastAPI REST API at /api/v1: GET /devices, GET /devices/{id}/snapshot, GET /migrations, GET /migrations/{id}/report, POST /migrations/{id}/approve. CLI is primary interface; REST is secondary for CI pipeline integration.
backend.failure_handling: BACnet read timeout retries with exponential back-off capped at 3 attempts per object. Partial discovery checkpoints to PostgreSQL so interrupted runs resume without re-scanning completed devices. Validation failures log full APDU payload to failed_points_jsonb for manual review.
backend.background_jobs: Nightly PostgreSQL job archives completed migration records older than 90 days to compressed JSONB exports. GitLab CI scheduled job re-runs validation spot checks on completed migrations to detect post-migration configuration drift.

security.auth_design: JWT tokens issued at CLI login with 8-hour expiry bound to migration session UUID; token payload includes engineer_id and session_id; validation rejects tokens presented for a different session_id
security.secrets_management: PostgreSQL password in Docker secret mounted at /run/secrets/pg_password. BACnet device credentials stored in Fernet-encrypted keystore file; key derived from engineer passphrase via PBKDF2.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: All migration state transitions (discovered, snapshotted, planned, validated, approved, applied) written to migration_audit table with engineer_id, timestamp, and SHA-256 checksum of config artifact. ASHRAE 135 change management traceability.
security.security_risks: Primary risk is misconfigured modern BACnet/IP config applied to live HVAC controllers. Mitigation: mandatory validation pass/fail gate before apply step is unlocked; apply step requires explicit --apply-to-live flag and dual-engineer confirmation for safety-critical zones.

data.entities: device_model_profiles (id, vendor_id INT, model_prefix VARCHAR, max_concurrent_apdus INT). bacnet_devices (id UUID, device_instance INT UNIQUE, device_name, vendor_id, model_name, firmware_rev, ip_address INET, model_profile_id FK, discovered_at). data_points (id UUID, device_id FK, object_type ENUM AI/AO/AV/BI/BO/BV/MSI/MSO, object_instance INT, object_name, properties_jsonb JSONB, snapshotted_at; UNIQUE device_id+object_type+object_instance; GIN index on properties_jsonb). controller_configurations (id UUID, device_id FK, config_format ENUM legacy/modern, config_payload_jsonb JSONB, checksum CHAR64). migration_plans (id UUID, source_config_id FK, target_config_id FK, status ENUM draft/validated/approved/applied, match_threshold_pct FLOAT DEFAULT 95.0, created_by). validation_reports (id UUID, migration_plan_id FK, total_points INT, matched_points INT, match_pct FLOAT, failed_points_jsonb JSONB, outcome ENUM pass/fail/review, signed_off_by, signed_off_at). migration_audit (id UUID, migration_plan_id FK, event_type, engineer_id, artifact_checksum, occurred_at).
data.schema_notes: JSONB for object properties avoids 35-column explosion for BACnet object attributes. GIN index on properties_jsonb supports point name search. match_threshold_pct column added in round 2 with DEFAULT 95.0; ValidationRunner computes outcome as pass when match_pct >= threshold.
data.retention_and_deletion: Migration snapshots retained 24 months. Validation reports never deleted. Device discovery records without migration plans purged after 90 days.

devops.deployment_topology: Docker Compose on Ubuntu 22.04 OT workstation: postgres:16, migration-tool (Python 3.12 from internal GitLab registry), caddy (TLS reverse proxy), grafana:10, prometheus. All images pre-seeded to internal registry; no internet access required.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI: ruff lint, mypy type check, pytest unit tests with BAC0 simulator fixtures, integration tests against Docker-hosted synthetic BACnet device, Docker image build tagged by git SHA pushed to internal GitLab Container Registry.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus metrics: bacnet_devices_discovered_total, data_points_snapshotted_total, validation_match_pct gauge per plan, migration_job_duration_seconds histogram, bacnet_read_errors_total. Grafana dashboard per building segment showing discovery progress and validation outcomes.
devops.rollback_strategy: Migration plans never auto-applied. Apply step requires --apply-to-live flag. Rollback is manual restoration of legacy controller config from snapshot in PostgreSQL; MigrationPlanner can regenerate legacy config artifact from stored JSONB snapshot.

ISS-001.title: Validation report lacks configurable pass/fail threshold
ISS-001.detail: The point-by-point match report outputs raw percentages with no pass/fail gate. Facilities managers cannot use it as a formal migration sign-off document.

history.note: Identified by critic reviewer in round 1

ISS-002.title: Per-device BACnet concurrency limit is global, not model-aware
ISS-002.detail: Single asyncio semaphore for all devices. Siemens PXC100 saturates at 10 concurrent APDUs while PXC200 handles 25. Global limit either starves newer devices or overloads older ones.

focus_issues.title: Validation report lacks configurable pass/fail threshold
focus_issues.detail: No threshold field in migration_plans schema; ValidationRunner always writes outcome as review rather than pass/fail.

focus_issues.title: Per-device BACnet concurrency limit is global, not model-aware
focus_issues.detail: Single semaphore(10) hardcoded in BACnetDiscovery.__init__; will saturate PXC100 units or under-utilize PXC200 units in mixed fleets.

latest_recommendations: Add match_threshold_pct column to migration_plans; ValidationRunner computes outcome as pass/fail against threshold
Create device_model_profiles table with max_concurrent_apdus per model; replace global semaphore with per-device semaphore
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: BACnet Controller Migration Tool Architecture v1

PostgreSQL JSONB for object properties handles BACnet type variability without column explosion
GitLab CI with BAC0 simulator fixtures is a practical test strategy for air-gapped environments

Global asyncio semaphore does not account for per-model APDU capacity differences

ISS-002: Global concurrency limit will saturate legacy PXC100 controllers in mixed fleets

Create device_model_profiles table; BACnetDiscovery looks up max_concurrent_apdus on first device contact and instantiates per-device semaphore

issue_updates.title: Validation report lacks configurable pass/fail threshold
issue_updates.detail: No threshold field exists; ValidationRunner hardcodes outcome as 'review' for all reports

issue_updates.title: Per-device BACnet concurrency limit is global, not model-aware
