previous_plan.title: BACnet Controller Migration Tool Architecture v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Python asyncio tool using BAC0 BACnet client to discover and snapshot legacy building automation controllers, generate modern BACnet/IP config artifacts, and produce validation reports from 24-hour parallel live reads. PostgreSQL 16 with JSONB stores device object hierarchies.
previous_plan.data_model: bacnet_devices, data_points with properties_jsonb, controller_configurations (legacy/modern), migration_plans, validation_reports with match_pct. Global semaphore(10) in BACnetDiscovery. No match_threshold_pct in migration_plans.
previous_plan.workflows: Discovery via WhoIs broadcast with global concurrency semaphore. Validation: 24-hour parallel reads, match_pct computed, outcome always set to review.
previous_plan.system_components: BACnetDiscovery module with global semaphore, MigrationPlanner with object type handlers, ValidationRunner, FastAPI REST, PostgreSQL 16, Docker Compose on OT workstation.

target_output.thinking_summary: Revising the BACnet migration tool to close two blocking issues from round 1. ISS-001 requires adding match_threshold_pct to migration_plans and updating ValidationRunner to compute pass/fail outcomes. ISS-002 requires replacing the global asyncio semaphore with per-device semaphores sized from a new device_model_profiles table. Both are targeted schema and logic changes that make the tool production-ready for mixed Siemens PXC controller fleets without changing the overall asyncio + BAC0 + PostgreSQL architecture.

fix_report.action_taken: Added match_threshold_pct FLOAT column to migration_plans (DEFAULT 95.0). CLI accepts --threshold flag at plan creation. ValidationRunner now computes outcome as pass when match_pct >= threshold, fail when below, review only when validation could not complete due to communication errors. Validation report section documents threshold semantics and sign-off workflow.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Facilities managers receive a formal pass/fail outcome on the validation report, enabling use as change management sign-off document without manual interpretation of raw percentages.

fix_report.action_taken: Added device_model_profiles table with vendor_id, model_prefix, max_concurrent_apdus. BACnetDiscovery queries this table on first contact and stores profile reference in bacnet_devices. Per-device asyncio.Semaphore instantiated from profile value. Default profile (max_concurrent_apdus=5) for unrecognized models. Seeded: Siemens PXC100=10, PXC200=25, Johnson Controls N2=8.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Discovery throughput improves for capable controllers while legacy PXC100 units are protected from APDU saturation; mixed-fleet migrations complete without communication errors on aging hardware.

target_output.title: BACnet Controller Migration Tool Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A Python asyncio tool using the BAC0 BACnet/IP library to discover all devices on a building OT network, extract full object-property snapshots from legacy proprietary controllers via ReadProperty batches, auto-generate equivalent modern BACnet/IP configuration artifacts, and validate migration correctness via 24-hour parallel live reads with a configurable point-match pass/fail threshold. Per-device asyncio semaphores sized from a device_model_profiles lookup protect aging controller CPUs from APDU saturation. PostgreSQL 16 with JSONB stores device hierarchies, migration plans with formal sign-off thresholds, and validation outcomes. Deployed as Docker Compose on an air-gapped OT workstation with Grafana for migration campaign monitoring.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single-process Python 3.12 application with an asyncio event loop orchestrating three pipeline stages: BACnetDiscovery (async WhoIs broadcast followed by per-device ReadProperty batches using per-model semaphores from device_model_profiles), MigrationPlanner (object-type-aware config translator for AI/AO/AV/BI/BO/BV/MSI/MSO to modern BACnet/IP YAML format), and ValidationRunner (concurrent reads from legacy and migrated controller for 24-hour soak, match scoring against configurable match_threshold_pct, formal pass/fail outcome). PostgreSQL 16 with JSONB persists device object hierarchies and migration artifacts. FastAPI provides report REST endpoints for CI integration. Grafana and Prometheus run locally for migration progress dashboards on the air-gapped workstation.
target_output.technology_stack: Runtime: Python 3.12 with asyncio. BACnet client: BAC0 22.x. REST API: FastAPI with asyncpg driver. ORM: SQLAlchemy 2 async. Database: PostgreSQL 16 with JSONB and GIN indexing. Encryption: Fernet for BACnet credential keystore. Reverse proxy: Caddy for TLS on REST API. Containerization: Docker Compose with Docker secrets. CI: GitLab CI with BAC0 simulator Docker fixture. Observability: prometheus-client, Grafana 10, structured JSON logging with logrotate.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Device Discovery: BACnet WhoIs broadcast on configurable subnet, device instance enumeration, model identification via Device Object reads, device_model_profiles lookup for per-device concurrency limits. Snapshot Capture: full object list extraction via ReadProperty, all object properties stored as JSONB, incremental re-snapshot for drift detection post-migration. Config Generation: MigrationPlanner translates each BACnet object type to modern BACnet/IP stack YAML, produces config artifact with SHA-256 checksum. Validation: 24-hour parallel read campaigns, point-by-point present value comparison, configurable match_threshold_pct, formal pass/fail outcome for facilities sign-off. Report API: FastAPI endpoints for report retrieval and approval sign-off.
target_output.system_components: BACnetDiscovery module (asyncio, BAC0, per-device semaphore from device_model_profiles, checkpoint persistence). MigrationPlanner module (object type handlers for 8 BACnet types, config serializer). ValidationRunner module (parallel reader, match scorer, threshold evaluator). FastAPI REST layer. PostgreSQL 16 (devices, snapshots, plans, validation results, audit log). Prometheus exporter. Grafana dashboard. Caddy TLS proxy. Docker Compose orchestration on OT workstation. GitLab CI with BAC0 simulator.
target_output.workflows: Device Discovery: Engineer runs 'bacnet-migrate discover --subnet 192.168.10.0/24'. Tool broadcasts BACnet WhoIs, collects I-Am responses, queries Device Object for vendor_id and model_name, looks up device_model_profiles for max_concurrent_apdus, stores bacnet_devices record, initiates ReadProperty batch with per-device semaphore, persists data_points with properties_jsonb. Checkpoint table saves progress so interrupted runs resume without re-scanning completed devices. Validation: Engineer runs 'bacnet-migrate validate --plan <id> --threshold 97.5'. ValidationRunner initiates parallel reads from legacy controller (BAC0 to legacy IP) and modern controller (BAC0 to new IP) every 5 minutes for 24 hours. Computes match_pct per point and aggregate. Evaluates against match_threshold_pct stored in migration_plans. Writes validation_reports with outcome ENUM pass/fail/review and prints sign-off summary to stdout for facilities engineer review.
target_output.data_model: device_model_profiles (id SERIAL PK, vendor_id INT, model_prefix VARCHAR, max_concurrent_apdus INT, notes TEXT). bacnet_devices (id UUID PK, device_instance INT UNIQUE, device_name VARCHAR, vendor_id INT, model_name VARCHAR, firmware_rev VARCHAR, ip_address INET, model_profile_id FK device_model_profiles, discovered_at TIMESTAMPTZ). data_points (id UUID PK, device_id FK bacnet_devices, object_type ENUM AI/AO/AV/BI/BO/BV/MSI/MSO, object_instance INT, object_name VARCHAR, description VARCHAR, units VARCHAR, properties_jsonb JSONB, snapshotted_at TIMESTAMPTZ; UNIQUE(device_id, object_type, object_instance); GIN index on properties_jsonb). controller_configurations (id UUID PK, device_id FK, config_format ENUM legacy/modern, config_payload_jsonb JSONB, generated_at TIMESTAMPTZ, checksum CHAR(64)). migration_plans (id UUID PK, source_config_id FK, target_config_id FK, status ENUM draft/validated/approved/applied, match_threshold_pct FLOAT DEFAULT 95.0, confidence_score FLOAT, created_by VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). validation_reports (id UUID PK, migration_plan_id FK, validation_start TIMESTAMPTZ, validation_end TIMESTAMPTZ, total_points INT, matched_points INT, match_pct FLOAT, failed_points_jsonb JSONB, outcome ENUM pass/fail/review, signed_off_by VARCHAR, signed_off_at TIMESTAMPTZ). migration_audit (id UUID PK, migration_plan_id FK, event_type VARCHAR, engineer_id VARCHAR, artifact_checksum CHAR(64), occurred_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: FastAPI REST at /api/v1. GET /api/v1/devices - list bacnet_devices with model_profile. GET /api/v1/devices/{id}/snapshot - return data_points for device. GET /api/v1/migrations - list migration plans with status and match_threshold_pct. GET /api/v1/migrations/{id}/report - full validation_report JSON including outcome and failed_points_jsonb. POST /api/v1/migrations/{id}/approve - set status=approved, record signed_off_by and signed_off_at. GET /api/v1/audit - migration_audit log filtered by plan_id or engineer_id. Auth: JWT Bearer token validated against session UUID. Large failed_points_jsonb streamed as chunked response.
target_output.security_and_compliance: JWT tokens issued at CLI login with 8-hour expiry bound to migration session UUID preventing cross-session replay. PostgreSQL password in Docker secret at /run/secrets/pg_password. BACnet device credentials in Fernet-encrypted keystore; key derived via PBKDF2 from engineer passphrase. TLS 1.3 on FastAPI via Caddy reverse proxy in Docker Compose. migration_audit table records all state transitions with engineer_id and artifact SHA-256 checksum for ASHRAE 135 change management traceability. Apply step requires --apply-to-live flag plus dual-engineer confirmation token for safety-critical HVAC zones. RBAC: migration_engineer role has full CLI access; read_only_auditor limited to GET report endpoints.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Docker Compose on Ubuntu 22.04 OT workstation (16GB RAM, air-gapped VLAN): postgres:16 with named volume, migration-tool (Python 3.12 image from internal GitLab registry), caddy (TLS reverse proxy with self-signed cert for OT LAN), grafana:10, prometheus. All base images pre-seeded to internal GitLab Container Registry; no outbound internet required. GitLab CI pipeline: ruff lint, mypy strict check, pytest unit tests with BAC0 simulator fixture container, integration tests against Docker-hosted synthetic BACnet devices, Docker image build tagged by git SHA and pushed to internal registry. Deployment to workstation via SSH trigger: docker compose pull then docker compose up -d migration-tool. PostgreSQL schema migrations via Alembic run-on-startup in migration-tool entrypoint.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics: bacnet_devices_discovered_total (counter), data_points_snapshotted_total (counter, label: device_id), validation_match_pct (gauge, label: migration_plan_id), migration_job_duration_seconds (histogram, labels: stage=discover/snapshot/validate), bacnet_read_errors_total (counter, labels: device_id, error_type). Grafana dashboard shows per-building discovery progress, validation match rate trends, and per-device error rates. Structured JSON logs to /var/log/migration-tool/ with logrotate (30-day retention, gzip). Log fields include: correlation_id, device_instance, object_type, apdu_round_trip_ms. Critical errors write to local alert file polled by facilities SCADA watchdog.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: No cloud cost; all compute on existing OT workstation. PostgreSQL storage estimated 50GB for 10,000 devices with full JSONB snapshots. Validation campaigns for a 1,000-point controller generate approximately 2.9 million read records per 24-hour run; archived to compressed JSONB export after sign-off. Discovery throughput: 500 devices per hour at PXC100 concurrency limits (10 APDUs); 1,200 devices per hour for PXC200 class. Horizontal scaling not applicable given OT network air-gap constraint.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): Siemens PXC100/PXC200 discovery and snapshot with device_model_profiles seeded for PXC family, PostgreSQL schema including match_threshold_pct, BACnetDiscovery with per-model semaphores, MigrationPlanner for AI/AO/AV/BI/BO/BV object types, ValidationRunner with configurable threshold and pass/fail outcome, FastAPI report API, Docker Compose deployment, GitLab CI with BAC0 simulator. Phase 2 (4 weeks): MSI/MSO multi-state object type handlers, Johnson Controls N2 and Honeywell Excel model profiles, incremental re-snapshot for drift detection, migration_audit sign-off workflow, Grafana dashboard for facilities review. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Automated nightly drift detection comparing post-migration live reads to validation baseline, GitLab scheduled regression job, PDF report generation for change management submission.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 strict mypy. ruff for linting. BAC0 client calls wrapped in asyncio.wait_for with 10-second timeout to prevent hung coroutines on unresponsive controllers. Per-device semaphore initialized from device_model_profiles; default profile (max_concurrent_apdus=5) applied for unrecognized models. JSONB ingest validates against per-object-type property allowlist before insert to prevent schema drift from unexpected BAC0 responses. All migration state transitions through MigrationStateMachine class with explicit allowed transitions. Integration tests use BAC0 simulator with synthetic Siemens PXC object definitions. No --apply-to-live command available in test environment.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Aging PXC100 controllers crash on APDU bursts even within profile limits during peak HVAC load cycles. Mitigation: per-device read_interval_ms in device_model_profiles (default 200ms between batches) with field-adjustable override. Risk: BACnet properties missing from JSONB snapshot due to controller firmware variations. Mitigation: properties_jsonb stores raw BAC0 response; MigrationPlanner logs unmapped properties to failed_points list in migration plan for manual review. Tradeoff: JSONB over normalized columns avoids 35-column BACnet property explosion but requires JSONB operators for point queries. Tradeoff: air-gapped Docker deployment trades cloud monitoring for OT network compliance; local Grafana is sufficient for migration campaign visibility.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: match_threshold_pct default set to 95.0; facilities approved 97.5% as formal threshold for safety-critical HVAC points and 90.0% for lighting circuits, configured per migration plan. BAC0 chosen over bacpypes3 for broader real-world controller compatibility. Docker secrets chosen over HashiCorp Vault which is unavailable on the air-gapped workstation.

metadata.notes: BACnet OT migration automation tool; round 2 fixes per-device concurrency control and adds formal validation pass/fail gate.

project_goal.value: CLI tool for auditing security configurations of IoT device fleets managed through AWS IoT Core, checking for overly permissive policies, unrotated device certificates, disabled audit logging, and shadow document exposure, producing a prioritized remediation report
project_goal.rationale: Security team needs automated compliance checks across fleets of thousands of IoT devices to meet NIST SP 800-213 baseline

target_users.value: Security engineers and DevSecOps teams managing AWS IoT Core device fleets
target_users.rationale: Primary operators who run security audits and apply remediations in CI/CD pipelines or on-demand

project_class.rationale: Command-line tool invoked by engineers and CI pipelines; no browser UI required

capabilities.rationale: AWS IoT Core API integration, finding storage and reporting, no frontend or auth system needed beyond IAM

complexity_level.rationale: Multiple AWS API integrations with rule evaluation engine; manageable scope for a CLI tool

risk_level.rationale: Tool reads confidential device certificate and policy data; remediation commands modify live IoT fleet configurations

data_sensitivity.rationale: IoT device policies, certificate ARNs, and shadow documents contain confidential fleet configuration data

external_exposure.rationale: CLI runs in engineer workstations and internal CI runners; never exposed publicly

access_model.value: AWS IAM roles with least-privilege policy for iot:ListThings, iot:GetPolicy, iot:ListCertificates, iot:DescribeThing, iot:GetThingShadow read scopes; separate remediation role with iot:UpdateCertificate, iot:CreatePolicy write scopes
access_model.rationale: Read-only audit and write remediation roles must be separate to enforce principle of least privilege

feature_scope.value: Policy wildcard check, certificate age and rotation audit, audit logging enablement check, shadow document exposure scan, prioritized finding report in JSON and Markdown, automated safe remediations with diff output and confirmation prompt
feature_scope.rationale: Covers the five most common IoT fleet security gaps identified in internal red team exercise

mvp_scope.value: Policy wildcard audit, certificate age check, JSON finding report, and --dry-run remediation mode for certificate rotation
mvp_scope.rationale: Highest-severity findings first; gets immediate value into security team hands

security_baseline.value: AWS IAM roles assumed via environment credentials; no long-lived keys stored on disk; finding reports sanitized before logging to CloudWatch to avoid leaking policy ARNs in log aggregation systems
security_baseline.rationale: SOC 2 Type II requires no credential persistence and sanitized log output

frontend_stack.value: None - CLI only, stdout/stderr output with Rich terminal formatting
frontend_stack.rationale: cli_tool project class; no browser interface

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Click for CLI framework; boto3 for AWS IoT Core API calls
backend_stack.rationale: Click is the standard Python CLI framework; boto3 is the official AWS SDK

data_platform.value: No persistent database; findings written to local JSON/Markdown files and optionally shipped to AWS CloudWatch Logs
data_platform.rationale: CLI tool with no long-running state; findings are ephemeral per audit run

hosting_target.value: Local CLI on engineer workstation and GitHub Actions / CI pipeline runner
hosting_target.rationale: Distributed execution across engineer machines and CI; installed via pip or Docker image

privacy_retention_policy.value: Audit finding reports retained locally per engineer policy; CloudWatch log shipping retention set to 90 days per SOC 2 log retention requirement
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: SOC 2 Type II audit evidence retention for certificate lifecycle management findings

future_scope.value: Support for Azure IoT Hub and GCP IoT Core fleet auditing; plugin architecture for custom hardening rules; SARIF output format for GitHub Advanced Security integration
future_scope.rationale: Multi-cloud IoT fleet audit coverage for organisations with heterogeneous cloud footprints

constraints.value: Must run without persistent infrastructure; AWS API rate limits require paginated calls with back-off; large fleets (10,000+ things) must complete audit in under 10 minutes using concurrent API calls
constraints.rationale: Engineer experience requirement; CI pipeline timeout budgets restrict slow sequential scans

observability_baseline.value: AWS CloudWatch Logs for audit run summaries; local structured JSON output for machine consumption in CI pipelines
observability_baseline.rationale: CloudWatch provides audit run history for SOC 2 evidence; JSON output enables CI pass/fail gates

execution_preference.value: Iterative releases via PyPI and Docker Hub; semantic versioning with CHANGELOG
execution_preference.rationale: Security tooling needs predictable release cadence for fleet-wide version pinning

compliance_context.value: NIST SP 800-213 IoT cybersecurity baseline for audit rule definitions; SOC 2 Type II security controls for certificate lifecycle management findings
compliance_context.rationale: risk_level=high and data_sensitivity=confidential require explicit compliance framing for audit findings

project.goal: Audit AWS IoT Core device fleet security posture and produce prioritized remediation reports
project.timeline: Q2 delivery for SOC 2 audit cycle

backend.api: boto3 AWS IoT Core SDK

security.auth: AWS IAM roles via environment credentials
security.transport: HTTPS to AWS APIs via boto3
security.log_sanitization: policy ARNs stripped before CloudWatch shipping

constraints.rate_limits: paginated boto3 calls with exponential back-off
constraints.concurrency: asyncio concurrent API calls for large fleets

reasoner_reviews.product: The five audit checks cover the highest-severity IoT fleet security gaps from the red team exercise. The separation of read-only audit IAM role from write remediation role is critical and must be enforced in the CLI command structure, not just documented.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: asyncio concurrent boto3 calls with a semaphore for AWS API rate limiting is the right pattern for large fleet scans. The plugin architecture for custom hardening rules should be designed now as an abstract base class even if only the five built-in rules ship in MVP, to avoid an incompatible refactor when the feature is prioritised.
reasoner_reviews.security: Log sanitization of policy ARNs before CloudWatch shipping is non-negotiable for SOC 2 compliance. The remediation mode must emit a machine-readable diff before any changes are applied and require explicit --confirm flag rather than a Y/N prompt to work in CI pipelines.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: AWS IoT Core ListThings paginates at 250 items per page; for a 10,000-thing fleet that is 40 API calls minimum per audit check. Concurrent check execution across all five rule types with per-rule semaphores sized to AWS API rate limits is needed to meet the 10-minute budget.

backend.service_design: Click command group 'iotaudit' with subcommands: audit (runs all checks), audit policy (policy wildcard check only), audit certs (certificate age check), audit shadow (shadow exposure check), audit logging (CloudWatch logging check), remediate (apply safe automated fixes). Each audit check implemented as a subclass of AuditRule abstract base class with check() -> list[Finding] method.
backend.api_patterns: boto3 IoT client with paginator for list_things, list_certificates, list_policies, list_targets_for_policy. Async execution via asyncio.gather across concurrent rule evaluations. Each rule uses dedicated boto3 session to avoid state sharing.
backend.failure_handling: boto3 ClientError caught per API call; throttling errors trigger exponential back-off with jitter; AccessDenied errors produce a Permission warning finding rather than crashing; partial results written to output file before exit on unrecoverable errors.

security.auth_design: AWS credentials resolved via boto3 default chain (environment variables, instance profile, SSO). No credential storage by the tool. IAM audit role assumed via sts:AssumeRole if --role-arn flag provided.
security.secrets_management: No secrets stored by the tool. AWS credential chain only. Finding reports written to local filesystem with 0600 permissions.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Audit run summary (run_id, timestamp, fleet_size, findings_count per severity, rules_executed) shipped to CloudWatch Logs group /iotaudit/runs. Policy document content and certificate ARNs stripped before CloudWatch shipping via sanitize_for_logging() function.
security.security_risks: Risk: remediation mode applied to production thing groups without review. Mitigation: --confirm flag required for production; --dry-run default shows diff without applying. Risk: overly broad IAM role grants more than needed. Mitigation: published least-privilege IAM policy JSON in repo for security team review.

data.entities: Finding (dataclass: id, rule_id, thing_name, severity ENUM critical/high/medium/low, title, detail, remediation_hint, detected_at). AuditReport (dataclass: run_id, fleet_size, findings list[Finding], rules_executed list[str], duration_seconds, generated_at). RemediationDiff (dataclass: action, resource_arn, before_state dict, after_state dict, applied bool).
data.schema_notes: All data structures are in-memory dataclasses; no database. JSON serialization uses dataclasses.asdict(). Markdown report generated from Finding list sorted by severity weight.
data.retention_and_deletion: Local report files managed by caller. CloudWatch log retention set to 90 days via stack parameter. No data stored by the tool beyond the current process.

devops.deployment_topology: Python package published to PyPI (iotaudit) and Docker image pushed to GHCR. GitHub Actions workflow: test matrix (Python 3.11, 3.12), moto-based AWS mock tests, Docker build and push on tag.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ruff lint, mypy check, pytest with moto mocks for all boto3 calls, coverage gate 85%, Docker build. Release triggered by git tag; PyPI publish via trusted publisher OIDC.
devops.observability_stack: CloudWatch Logs /iotaudit/runs for audit run summaries. Exit codes: 0 no findings, 1 findings found, 2 tool error. JSON output to stdout for CI machine consumption; Rich formatted table to stderr for human reading.
devops.devops_risks: AWS API rate limits vary by account tier; enterprise accounts have higher limits. Provide --concurrency flag to tune asyncio semaphore size per environment.

ISS-001.title: Remediation mode lacks machine-readable diff for CI pipeline integration
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 remediation mode printed human-readable text diff to stdout mixed with finding output, making it impossible to parse in CI pipelines. Fixed in round 2 by separating machine JSON diff to --output-diff flag.

history.note: --output-diff JSON flag added; stdout/stderr separation implemented

ISS-002.title: AuditRule plugin interface not defined, blocking custom rule extensibility
ISS-002.detail: Round 2 introduced an abstract base class sketch but the plugin loading mechanism and rule registration API were not implemented. Third-party rules cannot be loaded without modifying the tool source.

history.note: ABC added but plugin loader not implemented

ISS-003.title: Shadow document exposure check missing from audit suite
ISS-003.detail: Round 1 and 2 plans only implemented policy wildcard and certificate age checks. The shadow document exposure check (key feature in PRD feature_scope) has not been designed or implemented.

focus_issues.title: AuditRule plugin interface not defined, blocking custom rule extensibility
focus_issues.detail: Abstract base class exists but plugin loading from external packages not implemented.

focus_issues.title: Shadow document exposure check missing from audit suite
focus_issues.detail: iot:GetThingShadow scan for publicly readable shadow documents not designed in prior rounds.

latest_recommendations: Implement entry_points-based plugin loader so third-party packages can register AuditRule subclasses under iotaudit.rules group
Design and implement ShadowExposureRule: enumerate things, call GetThingShadow, flag shadows with named=false and no policy restriction
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: IoT Fleet Security Audit CLI Architecture v2

previous_audits.summary: Solid CLI architecture with clean Click command structure and good IAM separation. Round 2 fixed the CI diff output issue but the plugin loader and shadow exposure check remain unimplemented.

IAM role separation between audit and remediation roles is correctly enforced at CLI flag level

previous_plan.title: IoT Fleet Security Audit CLI Architecture v2
previous_plan.executive_summary: Python Click CLI using boto3 to audit AWS IoT Core device fleets for policy wildcards, certificate age, disabled audit logging, and shadow exposure. IAM role separation between read audit and write remediation. JSON and Markdown output with CloudWatch log shipping.
previous_plan.system_components: Click command group, AuditRule ABC with five built-in subclasses (PolicyWildcardRule, CertAgeRule, AuditLoggingRule), asyncio concurrent execution, boto3 paginators, Finding and AuditReport dataclasses, Rich terminal output, CloudWatch Logs shipping.
previous_plan.data_model: Finding dataclass (id, rule_id, thing_name, severity, title, detail, remediation_hint). AuditReport dataclass (run_id, fleet_size, findings, rules_executed, duration_seconds). No persistent storage.
previous_plan.workflows: Audit run: Click invokes audit command, rules execute concurrently via asyncio.gather, findings aggregated, report written to JSON file and stdout table. Remediation: --dry-run by default, --confirm required for apply, JSON diff written to --output-diff path.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 addresses the two remaining medium issues: implementing the entry_points-based plugin loader so third-party packages can register custom AuditRule subclasses, and designing the ShadowExposureRule that was missing from prior rounds. The shadow check requires paginated GetThingShadow calls with concurrency control. The plugin system uses importlib.metadata.entry_points under the iotaudit.rules group. Both changes integrate cleanly with the existing Click/asyncio/boto3 architecture.

fix_report.action_taken: Implemented entry_points-based plugin loader using importlib.metadata.entry_points(group='iotaudit.rules'). RuleRegistry class discovers and instantiates all registered AuditRule subclasses at startup. Built-in rules registered via pyproject.toml entry_points. Plugin packages declare their rules in the same group. CLI --list-rules command prints all discovered rules with source package. CONTRIBUTING.md documents the plugin package structure.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Security teams can publish internal custom audit rules as separate pip packages that are auto-discovered by iotaudit at runtime without forking the tool.

fix_report.action_taken: Designed and added ShadowExposureRule implementing AuditRule ABC. Rule paginates all things, calls iot:GetThingShadow for each, identifies shadows with non-empty state.desired or state.reported, then checks the thing's attached policies for any statement granting iot:GetThingShadow to principal '*'. Things with open shadow read are reported as high-severity findings. Batch processing uses asyncio semaphore(20) for GetThingShadow calls to respect API rate limits.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Shadow document exposure findings appear in audit reports; fleet operators can identify things with unrestricted shadow read access before they are exploited to exfiltrate device state telemetry.

target_output.title: IoT Fleet Security Audit CLI
target_output.executive_summary: A Python 3.12 Click CLI that audits AWS IoT Core device fleet security configurations by running five hardening checks (policy wildcard detection, certificate rotation age, audit logging enablement, shadow document exposure, and thing group membership policy) concurrently against fleet inventories of up to 10,000 things. Findings are prioritised by NIST SP 800-213 severity, written to structured JSON for CI pipeline consumption, and summarised in Markdown for human review. An entry_points plugin system allows teams to register custom AuditRule subclasses from internal packages. A remediation subcommand applies safe automated fixes with machine-readable JSON diffs and explicit --confirm gating.
target_output.architecture_overview: Stateless Python CLI with a Click command group. Audit rules are subclasses of AuditRule abstract base class discovered at startup via importlib.metadata.entry_points under the iotaudit.rules group. Concurrent rule execution uses asyncio.gather with per-rule boto3 sessions and per-API semaphores sized to AWS rate limits. boto3 paginators handle ListThings (250 per page), ListCertificates, and ListPolicies. GetThingShadow calls use a semaphore(20) to avoid throttling. Findings are aggregated into an AuditReport dataclass, serialised to JSON, and optionally shipped to CloudWatch Logs with ARN sanitisation. Rich library renders severity-coloured finding tables to stderr. Exit codes (0=clean, 1=findings, 2=error) enable CI pass/fail gates.
target_output.technology_stack: Runtime: Python 3.12. CLI framework: Click 8.x with command group structure. AWS SDK: boto3 1.34 with botocore paginators. Async: asyncio with concurrent rule execution. Terminal output: Rich 13 for coloured severity tables to stderr. Serialisation: dataclasses.asdict() to JSON. Plugin system: importlib.metadata entry_points. Testing: pytest, moto 5.x for AWS mock, coverage.py. Packaging: pyproject.toml with Hatch build backend, PyPI publish via OIDC trusted publisher, Docker image on GHCR.
target_output.functional_feature_map: PolicyWildcardRule: lists all IoT policies, parses policy document JSON, flags statements with Action '*' or Resource '*' as critical findings. CertAgeRule: lists all certificates, checks creation date against configurable --max-cert-age-days (default 365), flags unrotated certs as high. AuditLoggingRule: checks iot:GetV2LoggingOptions, flags accounts where logging level is DISABLED as high. ShadowExposureRule: paginates things, GetThingShadow, identifies non-empty shadows with policies granting GetThingShadow to principal '*', flags as high. AuditLoggingRule: verifies AWS IoT Device Defender audit is enabled per account. RuleRegistry: entry_points discovery of built-in and third-party rules. Remediation: CertRotationRemediation applies iot:UpdateCertificate status=INACTIVE on near-expiry certs with --dry-run default and JSON diff output.
target_output.system_components: Click command group (iotaudit audit, iotaudit audit policy/certs/shadow/logging, iotaudit remediate, iotaudit --list-rules). AuditRule ABC (abstract check() -> list[Finding] method, rule_id, severity_label, boto3_permissions list). RuleRegistry (entry_points loader, rule instantiation, --list-rules output). AsyncAuditRunner (asyncio.gather across registered rules, per-rule semaphore). boto3 IoT client with paginators. ShadowExposureRule (GetThingShadow batch with semaphore(20)). Finding and AuditReport dataclasses. ReportWriter (JSON to file, Markdown to file). CloudWatchShipper (sanitize_for_logging strips ARNs, PutLogEvents). Rich ConsoleRenderer (stderr table). RemediationEngine (diff generation, --confirm gate).
target_output.workflows: Fleet Audit Workflow: Engineer runs 'iotaudit audit --output report.json --region us-east-1'. RuleRegistry discovers all rules via entry_points. AsyncAuditRunner invokes rule.check() concurrently via asyncio.gather. Each rule paginates its target AWS resource list, evaluates findings, returns list[Finding]. Runner aggregates all findings, constructs AuditReport, writes JSON to report.json and Markdown to report.md, ships sanitised summary to CloudWatch Logs, renders Rich table to stderr, exits 0 (clean) or 1 (findings found). Shadow Exposure Audit: ShadowExposureRule.check() calls ListThings paginator, then GetThingShadow for each thing with semaphore(20) to respect AWS rate limits. For things with non-empty shadow state, fetches attached policies, parses policy documents, checks for iot:GetThingShadow with principal '*'. Flags exposure as a high-severity Finding with thing_name, shadow_state_keys, and offending_policy_arn in detail field.
target_output.data_model: Finding (dataclass: id UUID, rule_id str, thing_name str, severity ENUM critical/high/medium/low, title str, detail str, remediation_hint str, detected_at ISO8601). AuditReport (dataclass: run_id UUID, account_id str, region str, fleet_size int, findings list[Finding], rules_executed list[str], duration_seconds float, generated_at ISO8601). RemediationDiff (dataclass: action str, resource_arn str, before_state dict, after_state dict, applied bool). AuditRunSummary (dataclass for CloudWatch: run_id, fleet_size, critical_count, high_count, medium_count, low_count, rules_executed, duration_seconds; ARN fields excluded). All structures serialised via dataclasses.asdict(); no persistent database.
target_output.api_design: CLI commands as the primary API surface. 'iotaudit audit [--rules RULE_ID,...] [--output PATH] [--region REGION] [--role-arn ARN] [--concurrency N]' - runs audit, exit code 0/1/2. 'iotaudit audit policy|certs|shadow|logging' - runs single rule category. 'iotaudit remediate [--plan report.json] [--dry-run] [--confirm] [--output-diff diff.json]' - applies remediations from a prior report. 'iotaudit --list-rules' - prints all discovered rules with source package and required IAM permissions. JSON output to --output path; Rich table to stderr. CloudWatch shipping enabled by --cloudwatch-log-group flag.
target_output.security_and_compliance: AWS credentials via boto3 default chain only; no credential files written by the tool. IAM audit role (least-privilege: iot:ListThings, iot:GetPolicy, iot:ListCertificates, iot:GetThingShadow, iot:GetV2LoggingOptions) separate from remediation role (iot:UpdateCertificate, iot:CreatePolicy). --role-arn flag triggers sts:AssumeRole for cross-account audits. sanitize_for_logging() strips all ARNs and policy document content before CloudWatch PutLogEvents to prevent confidential data in log aggregation systems (SOC 2 Type II control). Local report files written with 0600 permissions. Plugin packages vetted via internal code review before installation in production CI runners. NIST SP 800-213 control IDs referenced in each Finding.detail for traceability.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Published as iotaudit Python package on PyPI and Docker image on GHCR (iotaudit:latest, iotaudit:1.x.x). GitHub Actions CI: ruff lint, mypy strict, pytest with moto 5 mocks for all boto3 calls, 85% coverage gate, Docker build. Release on git tag: PyPI publish via OIDC trusted publisher, Docker push to GHCR. Installation: pip install iotaudit or docker run ghcr.io/org/iotaudit. CI integration: iotaudit audit --output findings.json exits 1 if any findings; downstream step parses JSON for severity gate (fail on critical or high). Plugin installation: pip install internal-iotaudit-rules in CI environment; RuleRegistry auto-discovers on next run.
target_output.observability: Exit codes for CI integration: 0 clean, 1 findings present, 2 tool error. CloudWatch Logs group /iotaudit/runs: one log event per run with AuditRunSummary JSON (ARN-sanitised). Rich stderr table shows findings sorted by severity with colour coding (red=critical, yellow=high, cyan=medium). --verbose flag enables per-thing progress bar via Rich Live. Audit duration logged to AuditRunSummary.duration_seconds for performance trending. Finding count per rule per run enables tracking rule-specific drift over time in CloudWatch Insights queries.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: No infrastructure cost; runs on existing CI runners and engineer workstations. AWS API cost: ListThings, GetThingShadow, ListCertificates, ListPolicies are standard IoT Core API calls billed at AWS standard rates (typically sub-dollar for 10,000-thing fleets). Large fleet optimization: --concurrency flag tunes asyncio semaphore size; default 10 concurrent API calls safe for standard account limits. Fleet of 10,000 things with all five rules completes in under 8 minutes on standard account API quotas.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (delivered): PolicyWildcardRule, CertAgeRule, JSON/Markdown report output, IAM role separation, moto test suite, PyPI packaging, CloudWatch shipping with sanitisation. Phase 2 (delivered): AuditLoggingRule, RemediationEngine with --dry-run and --confirm, machine-readable JSON diff to --output-diff, ISS-001 resolved. Phase 3 current: ShadowExposureRule implementation, entry_points plugin loader (RuleRegistry), --list-rules command, CONTRIBUTING.md plugin authoring guide, NIST SP 800-213 control ID tagging on findings. Phase 4 (planned): SARIF output format for GitHub Advanced Security, Azure IoT Hub adapter, plugin registry documentation site.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 strict mypy. ruff for linting with all rules enabled. All boto3 calls mocked with moto in tests; no real AWS credentials in CI. New AuditRule subclasses must implement check() -> list[Finding], declare rule_id, severity_label, and boto3_permissions list. Rules registered via pyproject.toml entry_points group iotaudit.rules. All findings must include NIST SP 800-213 control reference in detail field. sanitize_for_logging() must be called on any dict before CloudWatch PutLogEvents; enforced by linter custom rule.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: AWS GetThingShadow rate limit throttling for fleets with thousands of named shadows. Mitigation: ShadowExposureRule uses semaphore(20) with exponential back-off; --concurrency flag lets operators reduce further for restricted accounts. Risk: third-party plugin packages introducing malicious audit logic in CI runners. Mitigation: internal code review gate before plugin installation; --list-rules shows source package for auditability. Tradeoff: stateless architecture with no database means no historical finding trend tracking natively; teams must integrate CloudWatch Insights queries for drift analysis. Tradeoff: entry_points plugin discovery adds startup latency for environments with many installed packages; acceptable for a tool invoked once per CI pipeline run.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: entry_points group name settled as iotaudit.rules for consistency with other Python audit tooling conventions. ShadowExposureRule severity set to high (not critical) because shadow exposure is an information-disclosure risk rather than a direct compromise vector. SARIF output deferred to Phase 4 after evaluating GitHub Advanced Security integration requirements with security team.

metadata.notes: IoT fleet security audit CLI; round 3 adds entry_points plugin loader and shadow document exposure check.

reasoner_reviews.product: The pipeline configurator is the key differentiating interactive element. It needs to demonstrate real anomaly detection concepts (sensor type, anomaly category, timeline simulation) without requiring actual ML inference, keeping it purely client-side. The use-case cards must reference specific industry pain points, not generic descriptions.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Next.js static generation with ISR is appropriate but for a landing page that changes infrequently, full static export is simpler and avoids any server-side runtime on Vercel. The pipeline configurator state machine should be a useReducer hook, not useState, to cleanly handle the multi-step selection flow.
reasoner_reviews.security: Content Security Policy must explicitly allowlist PostHog and HubSpot domains. The serverless function for trial signup must validate input (email format, company name length) before forwarding to HubSpot to prevent garbage lead data and API abuse.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Framer Motion adds approximately 30KB to the bundle. Using motion.div only on above-the-fold hero animation and lazy-importing the configurator section will keep FCP under 1.5 seconds. PostHog in cookieless mode can load immediately without consent banner delay.

frontend.app_structure: Next.js 14 app directory with single page (app/page.tsx) composed of section components: HeroSection, UseCaseCards, PipelineConfigurator, TestimonialStrip, PricingOverview, TrialSignupForm. All components are server components except PipelineConfigurator and TrialSignupForm which use client directives for interactivity.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Single-page layout with smooth scroll anchors: #hero, #use-cases, #configurator, #pricing, #signup. Navigation sticky header with CTA button scrolling to #signup. Configurator section is a 3-step flow: select sensor type (vibration/temperature/pressure/flow), select anomaly category (bearing failure/thermal runaway/cavitation/blockage), view simulated anomaly timeline chart.
frontend.state_management: useReducer in PipelineConfigurator for 3-step selection state. TrialSignupForm uses react-hook-form with zod validation schema. No global state management needed for single landing page.
frontend.performance_notes: Lazy import PipelineConfigurator and chart library (Recharts) via next/dynamic with loading skeleton. Hero animation via Framer Motion loaded eagerly but only above-the-fold. All images in next/image with priority flag on hero. Font subset loaded via next/font/google.
frontend.frontend_risks: Risk: pipeline configurator chart rendering slow on older corporate laptops. Mitigation: use Recharts LineChart (lightweight) over Chart.js; pre-compute all 12 timeline datasets (3 sensor types x 4 anomaly categories) as static JSON at build time.

security.auth_design: No authentication. Trial signup serverless function validates email with zod, rate-limits by IP using Vercel KV (Redis) to prevent form spam, returns 429 if more than 3 submissions per IP per hour.
security.abuse_prevention: Honeypot hidden field in trial signup form to catch bot submissions. Vercel KV rate limiting on POST /api/trial-signup. Input validation with zod schema before HubSpot API call.
security.privacy_controls: Cookie consent banner (cookie-consent library) blocks PostHog pageview tracking until accepted. PostHog loaded in cookieless mode for pre-consent analytics. Privacy policy link in footer. GDPR-compliant data processing statement in trial signup form.

devops.deployment_topology: Vercel project connected to GitHub repo. Production deployment on push to main. Preview deployment on every pull request with unique URL. Custom domain with Vercel SSL.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ESLint, TypeScript type check, next build to catch build errors. Lighthouse CI action on preview deploys to catch performance regressions before merge to main.
devops.observability_stack: PostHog events: page_viewed, configurator_step_completed (properties: sensor_type, anomaly_category), signup_form_submitted, signup_form_success. Vercel Analytics for Core Web Vitals (LCP, CLS, INP) per deployment. Vercel Functions logs for serverless function errors.

ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan proposed computing chart data client-side on each selection. Round 2 fixed this by pre-computing all 12 datasets as static JSON imported at build time.

history.note: Chart computation client-side causing jank on selection
Static JSON datasets generated at build time

ISS-002.title: Trial signup form missing rate limiting allowing spam submissions
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 had no rate limiting on the serverless function. Round 2 added Vercel KV IP-based rate limiting.

history.note: Vercel KV rate limit: 3 per IP per hour
