target_output.title: ML Anomaly Detection SaaS Landing Page
target_output.executive_summary: A Next.js 14 static landing page deployed on Vercel Edge Network targeting operations managers evaluating industrial IoT anomaly detection SaaS. Features an interactive pipeline configurator with pre-computed client-side anomaly timeline simulations across 12 sensor-anomaly combinations, a free-trial signup form with Vercel KV rate limiting and HubSpot CRM lead forwarding, and PostHog analytics in cookieless pre-consent mode with GDPR-compliant cookie banner. GitHub Actions enforces ESLint, TypeScript, and Lighthouse mobile performance score of 90+ on every pull request.
target_output.architecture_overview: Fully static Next.js 14 site exported to CDN with no server-side runtime. Single page composed of server components (HeroSection, UseCaseCards, TestimonialStrip, PricingOverview) and two client components (PipelineConfigurator, TrialSignupForm). PipelineConfigurator uses useReducer for 3-step selection state and renders pre-computed anomaly timeline JSON datasets via Recharts with no runtime ML inference. Trial signup posts to a Next.js API route serverless function that checks Vercel KV rate limit (3 per IP per hour), validates with zod, and forwards to HubSpot Contacts API. PostHog loads in cookieless mode before consent and switches to full tracking on cookie acceptance. Lighthouse CI runs against Vercel preview URLs on every pull request.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Next.js 14 app directory with TypeScript, Tailwind CSS 3, Framer Motion (hero animation only), Recharts for configurator timeline chart, react-hook-form with zod for trial signup. Analytics: PostHog with cookieless mode, Vercel Analytics for Web Vitals. Serverless: Next.js API route on Vercel Functions runtime. Rate limiting: Vercel KV (Redis). CRM: HubSpot Contacts API v3. CI: GitHub Actions with treosh/lighthouse-ci-action, lighthouserc.json with mobile performance threshold 0.90. Hosting: Vercel Edge Network with automatic HTTPS.
target_output.functional_feature_map: HeroSection: animated headline with Framer Motion, primary CTA scrolling to #signup, sub-headline with industry-specific value proposition for industrial operations managers. UseCaseCards: three cards for automotive (vibration bearing failure detection), food processing (thermal anomaly in pasteurization), semiconductor (pressure anomaly in CVD chamber); each card links to a configurator preset. PipelineConfigurator: 3-step selector (sensor type: vibration/temperature/pressure/flow; anomaly category: bearing_failure/thermal_runaway/cavitation/blockage); renders pre-computed Recharts LineChart from 12 static JSON datasets showing normal vs. anomalous sensor traces. PricingOverview: three tiers (Starter 10 sensors, Growth 100 sensors, Enterprise custom). TrialSignupForm: company name, email, sensor count estimate, use case dropdown; zod validation; HubSpot lead creation with onboarding link in confirmation response.
target_output.system_components: Next.js app directory (app/page.tsx, app/api/trial-signup/route.ts). HeroSection server component with Framer Motion above-the-fold animation. UseCaseCards server component (static content). PipelineConfigurator client component (useReducer, 12 pre-computed JSON datasets imported at build time, Recharts LineChart lazy-imported via next/dynamic). TrialSignupForm client component (react-hook-form, zod, fetch to /api/trial-signup). API route /api/trial-signup (Vercel Function: Vercel KV rate limit check, zod validation, HubSpot API call). PostHog provider with cookieless initial mode. Cookie consent banner component. lighthouserc.json with performance threshold 0.90 mobile. GitHub Actions workflow with Lighthouse CI step.
target_output.workflows: Pipeline Configurator Interaction: Visitor arrives at #configurator section. PipelineConfigurator renders Step 1 (sensor type buttons). On selection, useReducer dispatches SELECT_SENSOR action, renders Step 2 (anomaly category buttons). On selection, dispatches SELECT_ANOMALY, looks up pre-computed dataset from configuratorData[sensorType][anomalyCategory] static JSON import, passes data array to Recharts LineChart. Chart renders two lines: normal trace (flat with minor variance) and anomalous trace (spike pattern specific to anomaly type). Trial Signup Flow: Visitor fills TrialSignupForm, react-hook-form validates with zod schema (email format, company minimum 2 chars, sensor_count positive integer). On submit, POST to /api/trial-signup with JSON body. API route checks Vercel KV key rl:ip:{clientIp} (INCR + EXPIRE 3600); returns 429 if count exceeds 3. Calls HubSpot Contacts API POST /crm/v3/objects/contacts with email, company, sensor_estimate, use_case properties. Returns 200 with unique onboarding_link from HubSpot response. Confirmation message shown inline.
target_output.data_model: No persistent data model on this platform. Client-side state only: ConfiguratorState {sensorType: 'vibration' | 'temperature' | 'pressure' | 'flow' | null, anomalyCategory: 'bearing_failure' | 'thermal_runaway' | 'cavitation' | 'blockage' | null, step: 1 | 2 | 3}. Static JSON structure: configuratorData[sensorType][anomalyCategory]: Array<{t: number, normal: number, anomalous: number}> with 60 data points representing a 60-minute sensor trace. SignupPayload {email: string, company: string, sensor_count: number, use_case: string} validated by zod schema before HubSpot API call.
target_output.api_design: Single API endpoint: POST /api/trial-signup. Request body: {email: string, company: string, sensor_count: number, use_case: string}. Response 200: {success: true, onboarding_link: string, message: string}. Response 400: {error: string, fields: Record<string, string>} for zod validation failures. Response 429: {error: 'Too many submissions. Please try again later.'} for rate limit. Response 500: {error: 'Submission failed. Please try again.'} for HubSpot API errors. HubSpot API key stored in HUBSPOT_API_KEY Vercel environment variable; never exposed to client bundle.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Content Security Policy headers set in next.config.js: script-src allowlists app.posthog.com and js.hubspot.com; connect-src allowlists PostHog and HubSpot API domains; no inline scripts except Next.js framework chunks. HTTPS enforced by Vercel; no HTTP fallback. HUBSPOT_API_KEY in Vercel environment variable only; not exposed in NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix. Vercel KV rate limiting (3 POST per IP per hour) on trial signup to prevent lead spam. Honeypot field (hidden CSS, not submitted) in signup form to catch bot submissions. Cookie consent banner blocks PostHog identify and pageview events until user accepts; PostHog cookieless mode (distinct_id derived from page URL hash) active before consent for aggregate analytics. GDPR-compliant privacy policy linked in footer.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Vercel project connected to GitHub repository. Production deployment triggered on push to main; Vercel builds next export and deploys to Edge Network with CDN invalidation. Preview deployment on every pull request with unique vercel.app URL for stakeholder review. GitHub Actions pull_request workflow: ESLint check (next lint), TypeScript type check (tsc --noEmit), next build smoke test. Lighthouse CI step using treosh/lighthouse-ci-action: runs against Vercel preview URL (extracted from vercel-action output), lighthouserc.json asserts categories.performance >= 0.90 and categories.accessibility >= 0.85 on mobile preset. Lighthouse HTML report uploaded as GitHub Actions artifact. PR blocked if Lighthouse thresholds not met. Content updates: marketing team pushes Markdown/TSX changes directly to feature branches; preview URL shared in PR for non-technical review.
target_output.observability: PostHog events: page_viewed (url, referrer), configurator_step_completed (step, sensor_type, anomaly_category), configurator_completed (sensor_type, anomaly_category, time_to_complete_ms), signup_form_submitted, signup_form_success (use_case, sensor_count_bucket), signup_form_error (error_type). Vercel Analytics: Core Web Vitals (LCP, CLS, INP) per deployment for regression detection. Vercel Functions logs: trial-signup invocations with status code and HubSpot response code (not email or company name). Lighthouse CI artifacts in GitHub Actions for per-PR performance tracking. PostHog funnel analysis: page_viewed -> configurator_completed -> signup_form_success for conversion rate optimisation.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Vercel Hobby or Pro plan; landing page with low serverless function invocations stays within Hobby tier limits. Vercel KV included in Pro plan; minimal storage for rate limit keys (TTL 1 hour). PostHog Cloud free tier covers up to 1 million events per month. HubSpot Starter CRM for lead management. Total estimated cost under 50 USD per month at expected traffic volumes for a B2B landing page. No autoscaling concern; Vercel Edge Network handles traffic spikes without configuration.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (delivered): Hero, UseCaseCards, PipelineConfigurator with 12 pre-computed datasets, TrialSignupForm with HubSpot integration, PostHog cookieless analytics, Vercel KV rate limiting, CSP headers, Vercel deployment. Phase 2 (delivered): TestimonialStrip, PricingOverview, cookie consent banner with GDPR compliance, ISS-001 and ISS-002 resolved. Phase 3 current: Lighthouse CI integration in GitHub Actions (ISS-003 fix), lighthouserc.json thresholds, accessibility audit to reach 0.85 baseline. Phase 4 (planned): German and Japanese localisation via next-intl, ROI calculator interactive tool, video demo embeds with lazy loading, SARIF-compatible accessibility report for engineering review.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode. ESLint with Next.js recommended config and @typescript-eslint/strict. All images in next/image with explicit width and height to prevent CLS. Framer Motion imported only in HeroSection; never add motion.div to below-the-fold sections without Lighthouse CI verification. Pre-computed configurator JSON datasets live in src/data/configuratorData.ts; adding a new sensor or anomaly type requires updating this static file and regenerating all affected datasets. HUBSPOT_API_KEY must never appear in any NEXT_PUBLIC_ variable. Lighthouse CI gate must not be bypassed on main branch PRs.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: HubSpot API downtime drops trial signup submissions silently. Mitigation: API route returns success to user but logs error to Vercel Functions; marketing team monitors HubSpot contact creation rate daily. Risk: Vercel KV rate limit over-triggers for office IP addresses where multiple colleagues submit the form. Mitigation: rate limit key can be scoped to email+IP hash instead of IP only; configurable via environment variable. Tradeoff: pre-computed static JSON for 12 configurator states trades flexibility for performance; adding a 5th sensor type requires a code change rather than a CMS update, which is acceptable for this phase.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Lighthouse CI threshold set to 0.90 mobile performance after testing Next.js baseline with Framer Motion and Recharts lazy-loaded. Vercel KV chosen over upstash for rate limiting because it is natively integrated in Vercel Pro plan with no additional configuration. PostHog cookieless mode selected over server-side analytics to avoid requiring a backend for pure analytics needs.

metadata.notes: B2B IoT anomaly detection SaaS landing page; round 3 adds Lighthouse CI performance gate to close final low-severity issue.

project_goal.value: Self-service API management portal for third-party developers integrating with a smart home platform, allowing them to register applications, manage OAuth scopes, inspect device data access grants, and monitor API usage metrics
project_goal.rationale: Platform growth requires a self-service developer onboarding experience to reduce manual provisioning overhead

target_users.value: Third-party developers building smart home integrations and internal platform admins reviewing scope approval requests
target_users.rationale: Two distinct user groups with different permission levels and use cases

project_class.rationale: Full web application with authenticated portal, admin dashboard, and public API registry

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, analytics, public_api, admin_panel
capabilities.rationale: Developer portal requires frontend dashboard, REST API backend, OAuth 2.0 auth, usage analytics, and admin approval workflows

complexity_level.rationale: Standard web app with OAuth management and usage aggregation; no ML or real-time requirements

risk_level.rationale: Manages OAuth client credentials and device data access grants; compromise could expose homeowner device data to unauthorized apps

data_sensitivity.rationale: Developer apps access end-user smart home device data; portal stores OAuth grants linking apps to homeowner accounts

external_exposure.rationale: Developer portal must be publicly accessible for third-party developer self-registration

access_model.value: Auth0-based authentication for developer accounts; RBAC with developer and platform_admin roles; OAuth 2.0 client credentials for app-to-API authentication
access_model.rationale: Separates developer identity (Auth0) from app identity (OAuth client credentials)

feature_scope.value: Developer registration and app registration, OAuth scope selection and approval workflow, device data access grant inspection, API key management, usage metrics dashboard per client_id, platform admin scope approval and developer suspension, rate limit configuration
feature_scope.rationale: Full developer lifecycle management from registration through monitoring

mvp_scope.value: Developer registration, app registration with OAuth scope request, admin scope approval, API key issuance, and basic 24-hour usage metrics per client_id
mvp_scope.rationale: Gets first third-party integrations onboarded; advanced analytics and rate limit UI in subsequent release

security_baseline.value: Auth0 with MFA optional for developers; OAuth 2.0 client credentials with PKCE for web app flows; API keys stored as hashed values in database; scope allowlisting enforced server-side
security_baseline.rationale: OAuth 2.0 RFC 9700 security best practices; hashed API keys prevent exposure from database read

frontend_stack.value: React 18 with TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, React Query
frontend_stack.rationale: Team expertise; React Query for API data fetching with caching

backend_stack.value: Node.js 20 with Fastify, Prisma ORM
backend_stack.rationale: Fastify for high-throughput API; Prisma for type-safe PostgreSQL access

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for application data; Redis 7 for API usage counters and rate limiting
data_platform.rationale: Relational model for apps and grants; Redis for high-frequency usage counter increments

hosting_target.value: GCP Cloud Run for backend, Cloud CDN for frontend, Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL
hosting_target.rationale: Serverless containers with managed database; GCP-native stack for low operational overhead

privacy_retention_policy.value: OAuth grants and API usage logs retained 12 months; developer accounts retained until deletion request; GDPR right-to-erasure for developer accounts
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR compliance for EU-based developers registering on the platform

future_scope.value: Webhook subscription management, SDK documentation portal, sandbox environment with synthetic device simulator
future_scope.rationale: Q3 roadmap items for deeper developer experience

constraints.value: API usage aggregation must handle 50,000 API calls per minute across all registered apps; usage dashboard must reflect data no older than 5 minutes
constraints.rationale: Platform scale requirement; developers expect near-real-time usage visibility

observability_baseline.value: Google Cloud Operations Suite for backend logs and traces; custom Prometheus metrics for OAuth token issuance rate and scope approval latency
observability_baseline.rationale: GCP-native observability with custom business metrics for portal health

execution_preference.value: Agile sprints with 2-week iterations; Cloud Build CI/CD with automated staging deploys
execution_preference.rationale: Team delivery cadence; Cloud Build integrates natively with GCP Cloud Run

compliance_context.value: GDPR for EU developer personal data and end-user device data accessed via registered apps; OAuth 2.0 RFC 9700 security best practices for authorization server implementation
compliance_context.rationale: personal data_sensitivity and medium risk_level require compliance framing

project.goal: Self-service developer portal for smart home platform API management

backend.api: RESTful with OpenAPI 3.0

security.oauth: OAuth 2.0 client credentials with scope allowlisting

data.cache: Redis 7 for usage counters and rate limiting

devops.hosting: Cloud Run for backend, Cloud CDN for frontend

reasoner_reviews.product: The MVP scope is achievable for Q2. The scope approval workflow is a critical path item: if the admin approval UI is not shipped in MVP, third-party developers cannot go live. The admin panel must be included in MVP despite being listed as an advanced feature.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Redis INCR for usage counters with a rolling window aggregation job is the right pattern. However, the 5-minute freshness requirement means the aggregation must be a short-interval cron or streaming aggregation, not a nightly batch.
reasoner_reviews.security: OAuth client secrets must be shown only once at registration and stored as bcrypt hashes; this pattern needs explicit design in the data model and API response. Scope allowlisting must be enforced at the authorization server level, not only at the developer portal UI.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 50,000 API calls per minute generates 3 million Redis INCR operations per minute across client IDs. A dedicated Redis instance with pipeline batching is needed; shared Redis with session data would be overloaded.

frontend.app_structure: React SPA with React Router v6. Route groups: /dashboard (usage overview), /apps (app list and registration), /apps/:id (app detail with scopes and grants), /apps/:id/keys (API key management), /admin (admin panel for scope approvals and developer management).
frontend.pages_and_flows: Developer onboarding: register account via Auth0, create first app, request OAuth scopes, view pending approval status. App management: list registered apps, view per-app usage chart, inspect device grants per app. Admin: scope approval queue, developer suspension, rate limit override.
frontend.state_management: React Query for all server state (apps, usage metrics, pending approvals). No global client state manager needed.
frontend.frontend_risks: Usage metrics chart polling every 30 seconds per app. If a developer has 10 apps open, that is 10 concurrent polling queries. Should use a single aggregated query per dashboard view instead.

backend.service_design: Fastify with domain modules: apps (registration, key management), oauth (scope management, grant inspection), usage (metrics aggregation from Redis), admin (approval workflow, developer management).
backend.api_patterns: RESTful API at /api/v1 with OpenAPI 3.0. Auth: Auth0 JWT verified at Fastify hook. OAuth client_secret shown only on POST /apps/:id/keys response, stored as bcrypt hash.
backend.failure_handling: Redis connection failure falls back to PostgreSQL for usage reads with cache miss warning in response headers. Auth0 token validation failure returns 401. Prisma query errors mapped to structured error responses.

security.auth_design: Auth0 Universal Login for developer accounts. Auth0 Management API for user provisioning and suspension. RBAC via Auth0 roles: developer and platform_admin.
security.authorization_model: Row-level security: developers can only read/write their own apps and API keys. platform_admin can read all developers and approve/reject scope requests. Enforced at Fastify route handlers.
security.secrets_management: OAuth client_secret generated as 32-byte random hex at key creation, shown once in POST response, stored as bcrypt hash. API_KEY column stores SHA-256 hash.

data.entities: developer_accounts (id UUID, auth0_sub, email, company, status ENUM active/suspended, created_at). registered_apps (id UUID, developer_id FK, app_name, description, redirect_uris TEXT[], status ENUM pending/active/suspended, created_at). oauth_clients (id UUID, app_id FK, client_id VARCHAR UNIQUE, client_secret_hash VARCHAR, scopes_requested TEXT[], scopes_approved TEXT[], approval_status ENUM pending/approved/rejected, approved_by FK). device_grants (id UUID, oauth_client_id FK, homeowner_id UUID, device_id UUID, scopes_granted TEXT[], granted_at, revoked_at). api_usage_counters (Redis hash: key=client_id, field=minute_bucket UNIX_TS, value=call_count). usage_snapshots (id UUID, client_id FK, bucket_start TIMESTAMPTZ, bucket_duration_minutes INT, call_count INT, error_count INT).
data.schema_notes: API usage counters in Redis with 5-minute bucket keys; snapshot job runs every 5 minutes writing aggregated counts to usage_snapshots in PostgreSQL for persistent history.
data.retention_and_deletion: usage_snapshots older than 12 months deleted by monthly batch job. device_grants soft-deleted with revoked_at timestamp.

devops.deployment_topology: GCP Cloud Run for Fastify backend (min 1 instance, max 10, CPU target 60%). Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 with private IP. Cloud Memorystore Redis. Cloud CDN serving React SPA from Cloud Storage bucket.
devops.ci_cd_design: Cloud Build: ESLint, TypeScript check, Jest unit tests, Docker build, deploy to Cloud Run staging on main push, manual approval gate to production.
devops.observability_stack: Google Cloud Logging with structured JSON. Cloud Trace for request tracing. Custom Prometheus metrics via prom-client: oauth_token_issued_total, scope_approval_latency_seconds, api_key_created_total.

target_output.thinking_summary: First-pass plan for a smart home developer API portal. Key decisions: Auth0 for developer identity with RBAC separating developer from platform_admin roles; Redis for high-frequency usage counter increments with a 5-minute snapshot job writing to PostgreSQL for persistent history; OAuth client secrets shown once and bcrypt-hashed in storage. The weak quality distribution target means a couple of sections will be thinner than ideal. Scope approval workflow is the critical path for MVP.
target_output.title: Smart Home Platform Developer API Portal
target_output.executive_summary: A React 18 and Fastify web application on GCP providing third-party developers with self-service registration, OAuth scope management, device grant inspection, and API usage dashboards for smart home platform integrations. Auth0 handles developer identity with developer and platform_admin RBAC roles. Redis counters aggregate API usage at 5-minute buckets for near-real-time dashboards. OAuth client secrets are generated once and bcrypt-hashed in Cloud SQL.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-tier web application: React 18 SPA served from Cloud CDN, Fastify REST API on Cloud Run, Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 with Cloud Memorystore Redis. Developer identity managed by Auth0 with JWT verification at Fastify request hooks. OAuth client credential management (client_id, hashed client_secret, scope allowlisting) handled by the oauth Fastify module. Usage aggregation reads Redis INCR counters via a 5-minute snapshot cron job writing to PostgreSQL usage_snapshots for dashboard queries. Admin scope approval workflow routes pending oauth_clients to the platform_admin queue in the admin Fastify module.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, React Query, React Router v6. Backend: Node.js 20, Fastify 4, Prisma ORM, Auth0 JWT plugin. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL. Cache: Redis 7 on Cloud Memorystore. Auth: Auth0 Universal Login with RBAC roles. CI/CD: Cloud Build with Cloud Run deploy. Observability: Google Cloud Logging, Cloud Trace, prom-client metrics.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Developer Registration: Auth0 Universal Login, email verification, company profile setup. App Registration: create registered_apps record, generate oauth_clients with client_id and one-time client_secret display, request scope subset from platform allowlist. Scope Approval Workflow: developer submits scope request, platform_admin reviews in approval queue, approved/rejected status updates oauth_clients.scopes_approved. Device Grant Inspection: developers view device_grants table showing which homeowner devices granted access to their app. API Usage Dashboard: Recharts bar chart of call counts per 5-minute bucket from usage_snapshots, last 24 hours, refreshed every 60 seconds. Admin Panel: pending scope approvals queue, developer suspension, rate limit override per client_id.
target_output.system_components: React SPA (Cloud CDN, Cloud Storage), Fastify API (Cloud Run, 4 domain modules), Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 (apps, oauth_clients, device_grants, usage_snapshots), Cloud Memorystore Redis 7 (usage counters, rate limit state), Auth0 tenant (developer identity, RBAC roles), 5-minute snapshot Cloud Scheduler job, Cloud Build CI pipeline.
target_output.workflows: App Registration and Scope Request: Developer logs in via Auth0, creates a registered_app record, submits OAuth scope request selecting from platform-approved scope list (device.read, device.control, device.history). Fastify creates oauth_clients record with status=pending and generates client_id and client_secret (displayed once in response body, stored as bcrypt hash). Email notification sent to platform_admin queue. API Usage Aggregation: Platform edge gateway increments Redis INCR key {client_id}:{minute_bucket} on each API call. Cloud Scheduler triggers snapshot function every 5 minutes; function reads and resets Redis counters per client_id, writes summed counts to usage_snapshots in PostgreSQL. Developer dashboard React Query polls GET /api/v1/usage/me every 60 seconds for fresh data.
target_output.data_model: developer_accounts (id UUID PK, auth0_sub VARCHAR UNIQUE, email VARCHAR, company VARCHAR, status ENUM active/suspended, created_at). registered_apps (id UUID PK, developer_id FK developer_accounts, app_name VARCHAR, description TEXT, redirect_uris TEXT[], status ENUM pending/active/suspended, created_at). oauth_clients (id UUID PK, app_id FK registered_apps, client_id VARCHAR UNIQUE, client_secret_hash VARCHAR, scopes_requested TEXT[], scopes_approved TEXT[], approval_status ENUM pending/approved/rejected, approved_by FK developer_accounts, approved_at TIMESTAMPTZ). device_grants (id UUID PK, oauth_client_id FK oauth_clients, homeowner_id UUID, device_id UUID, scopes_granted TEXT[], granted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, revoked_at TIMESTAMPTZ). usage_snapshots (id UUID PK, client_id FK oauth_clients, bucket_start TIMESTAMPTZ, bucket_duration_minutes INT, call_count INT, error_count INT). api_keys (id UUID PK, oauth_client_id FK, key_hash CHAR(64), label VARCHAR, last_used_at TIMESTAMPTZ, revoked_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: RESTful API at /api/v1 with Auth0 JWT auth. POST /api/v1/apps (register app). GET /api/v1/apps (list developer's apps). POST /api/v1/apps/:id/scopes (request scopes). GET /api/v1/apps/:id/grants (list device_grants). POST /api/v1/apps/:id/keys (generate API key, one-time secret in response). GET /api/v1/usage/me (usage_snapshots last 24h). GET /api/v1/admin/approvals (platform_admin only, pending scope requests). PATCH /api/v1/admin/approvals/:id (approve or reject). DELETE /api/v1/admin/developers/:id (suspend).
target_output.security_and_compliance: Auth0 Universal Login with optional MFA for developer accounts. RBAC: developer role restricted to own resources; platform_admin role required for /admin routes enforced at Fastify hook. OAuth client_secret generated as crypto.randomBytes(32).toString('hex'), returned once in POST /apps/:id/keys response, stored as bcrypt hash (cost factor 12). API key stored as SHA-256 hash in api_keys table. Redis rate limiting: client-level rate limit checked per request using sliding window counter. GDPR: developer account deletion endpoint removes developer_accounts record and anonymises email in usage_snapshots; device_grants soft-deleted. OAuth RFC 9700 compliance: PKCE required for authorization code flows; scope allowlist enforced server-side.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Cloud Run backend: min-instances 1, max 10, CPU target 60%, memory 512MB. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16: private IP, automated backups, point-in-time recovery. Cloud Memorystore Redis 7: 1GB basic tier. Cloud CDN with Cloud Storage bucket for React SPA build artifacts. Cloud Build pipeline: lint, type check, Jest tests, Docker build, push to Artifact Registry, deploy Cloud Run revision with traffic at 0% for canary check before 100% rollout.
target_output.observability: Google Cloud Logging with structured JSON from Fastify. Cloud Trace for end-to-end request tracing. prom-client metrics scraped by Cloud Monitoring: oauth_tokens_issued_total, scope_approvals_total (label: outcome), api_keys_created_total, usage_snapshot_lag_seconds (time since last snapshot run).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Cloud Run scales to zero outside business hours; estimated 200 USD/month at moderate developer traffic. Cloud SQL db-g1-small sufficient for MVP. Redis 1GB handles 50,000 INCR/minute comfortably. Cloud CDN reduces egress cost for SPA assets.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): developer registration, app registration, OAuth scope request and admin approval, client_secret one-time display, API key generation, basic 24-hour usage chart. Phase 2 (4 weeks): device grant inspection UI, rate limit override admin panel, developer suspension workflow, GDPR deletion endpoint. Phase 3 (4 weeks): webhook subscription management, advanced usage analytics with 30-day history, sandbox environment with synthetic device simulator.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode backend and frontend. Fastify schema validation on all request bodies using JSON Schema. Prisma migrations in CI pipeline. OAuth client_secret never logged; log redaction middleware strips Authorization headers and secret fields from Cloud Logging output. React Query stale time 30 seconds for usage data; 5 minutes for app list.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Redis Memorystore failure causes usage counter data loss for the current 5-minute window. Mitigation: counters are best-effort telemetry; gaps acceptable for developer dashboards; critical usage enforcement uses PostgreSQL usage_snapshots. Risk: Auth0 downtime blocks developer logins. Mitigation: Cloud Run serves cached Auth0 public keys for JWT verification; short outages transparent to authenticated sessions. Tradeoff: usage aggregation via Redis counters and 5-minute snapshot cron over a streaming solution trades 5-minute latency for operational simplicity at the expected scale.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Auth0 chosen over self-hosted Keycloak for reduced operational overhead at this scale. Redis chosen over PostgreSQL advisory locks for usage counters due to INCR atomicity and throughput. OAuth scope allowlist stored in PostgreSQL platform_config table managed by platform_admin; not hardcoded.

metadata.notes: Smart home developer API portal; first pass with some thinner sections reflecting weak quality distribution target.

project_goal.value: AI system that optimizes energy procurement and load scheduling for large commercial buildings equipped with smart meters and HVAC IoT sensors, using predictive occupancy and weather models to shift flexible loads to off-peak tariff windows and reduce energy spend by 20-35%
project_goal.rationale: Energy cost reduction is a primary ROI driver for commercial building operators; 20-35% reduction is achievable based on pilot results

target_users.value: Building energy managers, facilities directors, and C-suite stakeholders reviewing energy savings reports
target_users.rationale: Multiple audience levels from operational (daily schedule review) to strategic (monthly savings reports)

project_class.rationale: Core value is ML-based demand forecasting and MILP optimization; AI/ML is the primary architectural concern

capabilities.value: backend, data, auth, ai_llm, analytics, integrations, admin_panel, batch_jobs
capabilities.rationale: ML inference pipeline, MILP solver, BMS API integration, savings analytics dashboard, LLM-powered natural language energy reports

complexity_level.rationale: LSTM demand forecasting, MILP optimization solver, real-time BMS API dispatch, multi-building portfolio management

risk_level.rationale: System dispatches HVAC schedules to live building systems; incorrect schedules could affect occupant comfort but not safety-critical systems

data_sensitivity.rationale: Energy procurement contracts, tariff schedules, and consumption patterns are commercially sensitive building operator data

external_exposure.rationale: Portal accessible to credentialed building operators only; not publicly discoverable

access_model.value: Azure AD with OIDC; RBAC with energy_manager, facilities_director, building_admin, and platform_operator roles; multi-tenant isolation by building portfolio
access_model.rationale: Enterprise building operators have existing Azure AD tenants; OIDC federation avoids separate credential management

feature_scope.value: Hourly LSTM demand forecasting per building zone, MILP-based load scheduling for HVAC pre-cooling, EV charging, and battery dispatch, tariff schedule ingestion and off-peak window detection, BMS API dispatch of optimization schedules, projected vs. actual savings tracking, LLM-generated natural language energy performance reports, admin portal for building configuration and tariff management
feature_scope.rationale: Full optimization cycle from forecast through dispatch and savings attribution

mvp_scope.value: Single building, LSTM demand forecasting for HVAC zones, MILP HVAC pre-cooling schedule optimization, BMS API dispatch via REST, savings dashboard showing projected vs. actual consumption
mvp_scope.rationale: Validate the core ML-to-dispatch loop on one building before multi-building portfolio rollout

security_baseline.value: Azure AD OIDC with MFA enforced; API keys for BMS integration stored in Azure Key Vault; ClickHouse data encrypted at rest; all inter-service communication via mTLS on AKS
security_baseline.rationale: Confidential energy procurement data requires encryption at rest and in transit; BMS API keys require secrets management

frontend_stack.value: None - API and admin panel only; admin UI served from FastAPI backend with Jinja2 templates
frontend_stack.rationale: ai_system project class; energy managers access savings dashboards via API-delivered data; no standalone SPA required for MVP

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI; OR-Tools CP-SAT solver for MILP optimization; PyTorch for LSTM forecasting model inference
backend_stack.rationale: FastAPI for async API serving; OR-Tools is the mature open-source MILP solver; PyTorch for LSTM model serving

data_platform.value: ClickHouse for time-series energy telemetry; PostgreSQL for operational data (buildings, tariffs, schedules, savings records); Redis for forecast cache
data_platform.rationale: ClickHouse handles billion-row sensor telemetry with fast aggregations; PostgreSQL for relational operational data

hosting_target.value: AKS (Azure Kubernetes Service) on Azure; Azure DevOps for CI/CD
hosting_target.rationale: Kubernetes for ML inference and optimizer workloads; Azure-native stack aligns with enterprise tenant Azure AD

privacy_retention_policy.value: Energy telemetry retained 24 months in ClickHouse hot storage, archived to Azure Blob Storage cold tier after 24 months; GDPR for building occupancy inference data per EU EED reporting obligations
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: EU Energy Efficiency Directive requires 24-month consumption history for compliance reporting

future_scope.value: EV charging fleet scheduling, battery storage dispatch optimization, multi-building portfolio Pareto optimization, carbon intensity-aware scheduling using grid emissions API
future_scope.rationale: Expand flexible load types after HVAC pre-cooling MVP proves savings model

constraints.value: MILP solver must return optimization schedule within 60 seconds for a 48-hour planning horizon across 20 HVAC zones; BMS API dispatch must complete 2 hours before the first scheduled load shift; LSTM inference must complete in under 5 seconds per building
constraints.rationale: Building operators review schedules during morning shift; tight latency budgets for operational planning windows

observability_baseline.value: Grafana and Prometheus on AKS; custom metrics for LSTM forecast accuracy (MAPE), MILP solve time, BMS dispatch success rate, and projected vs. actual savings delta
observability_baseline.rationale: ML system quality requires custom metrics beyond infrastructure monitoring; forecast accuracy drift indicates model retraining need

execution_preference.value: Iterative delivery in 3-week sprints; MLOps pipeline for model versioning and canary deployment
execution_preference.rationale: ML model iteration requires separate release cadence from application code

compliance_context.value: GDPR for building occupancy inference data used in demand forecasting; EU Energy Efficiency Directive (EED) Article 8 energy audit data retention obligations; internal data classification for energy procurement strategy as commercially confidential
compliance_context.rationale: Confidential data_sensitivity and EU building operator customer base require explicit compliance framing

llm_integration.value: Azure OpenAI GPT-4o for natural language energy performance report generation; LLM takes structured savings summary JSON and produces a 2-paragraph plain-English summary for building management reporting; no RAG required; outputs reviewed by energy manager before distribution
llm_integration.rationale: LLM report generation reduces energy manager reporting time; structured input keeps hallucination risk low; human review gate before distribution

project.goal: Reduce commercial building energy costs 20-35% via ML demand forecasting and MILP load scheduling
project.target_users: Building energy managers and facilities directors

backend.ml: PyTorch LSTM inference, OR-Tools CP-SAT MILP solver

security.secrets: Azure Key Vault for BMS API keys

data.db: ClickHouse for telemetry, PostgreSQL for operational data

reasoner_reviews.product: The single-building MVP is the right risk reduction strategy before portfolio rollout. The LLM report generation adds a compelling differentiator for management-level buyers. Projected vs. actual savings tracking is critical for ROI validation and must be in MVP, not deferred.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: OR-Tools CP-SAT is well-suited for the HVAC pre-cooling MILP formulation with binary on/off variables for zone scheduling and continuous variables for pre-cooling temperature setpoints. The 60-second latency constraint for 20 zones over 48 hours (20 zones x 48 time slots = 960 decision variables) is achievable. The LSTM inference service should be a separate AKS deployment from the optimizer to allow independent scaling during the nightly retraining cycle.
reasoner_reviews.security: BMS API keys are the highest-risk secrets; Azure Key Vault with managed identity access from AKS pods is the correct pattern. mTLS between FastAPI, LSTM inference service, and optimizer service prevents internal service impersonation. The LLM integration must strip any building-identifying information from prompts before sending to Azure OpenAI to avoid leaking procurement strategy context.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 5-second LSTM inference latency for a building with 20 zones requires batched zone inference in a single model forward pass, not 20 sequential calls. The forecast must also run on a fixed hourly schedule, not on-demand, with results cached in Redis to decouple forecast latency from the API response path.

backend.service_design: Three AKS deployments: forecast-service (FastAPI + PyTorch LSTM, batched 20-zone inference, Redis cache write), optimizer-service (FastAPI + OR-Tools CP-SAT, MILP formulation with tariff windows as constraints, schedule output to PostgreSQL), dispatch-service (FastAPI, reads approved schedules, calls BMS REST API with retry logic, writes dispatch audit log). Main API gateway (FastAPI) handles auth and routes to services.
backend.api_patterns: Internal service mesh via mTLS. External API at /api/v1: GET /buildings/:id/forecast (returns cached LSTM prediction), POST /buildings/:id/optimize (triggers MILP solve, returns schedule), POST /buildings/:id/dispatch (submits approved schedule to BMS), GET /buildings/:id/savings (projected vs. actual delta).
backend.business_modules: TariffEngine: parses tariff schedule JSON (time-of-use rates, demand charge windows, off-peak periods), identifies optimization windows for the 48-hour horizon. SavingsAttributor: compares actual meter readings against baseline consumption forecast to compute realized savings per building per day.
backend.background_jobs: Hourly AKS CronJob triggers forecast-service for all buildings. Nightly AKS CronJob triggers LSTM model retraining on previous 30 days of meter readings. Daily SavingsAttributor job reads prior-day ClickHouse meter data and writes savings_records to PostgreSQL.
backend.scaling_notes: optimizer-service CPU-intensive during solve; HorizontalPodAutoscaler on CPU 70%. forecast-service scales on custom metric forecast_queue_depth. Redis forecast cache with 2-hour TTL prevents re-inference on API reads.

security.auth_design: Azure AD OIDC with FastAPI azure-identity library. JWT validated at API gateway. MFA enforced via Azure AD Conditional Access policy for all users.
security.authorization_model: RBAC: energy_manager can view forecasts, approve schedules, and view savings. facilities_director adds building config access. building_admin can manage tariff schedules. platform_operator has full access including model retraining triggers. Multi-tenant isolation: all database queries filter by building_portfolio_id from JWT claims.
security.secrets_management: BMS API keys stored in Azure Key Vault; accessed by dispatch-service via AKS workload identity (managed identity). Azure OpenAI API key in Key Vault. PostgreSQL and ClickHouse passwords in AKS secrets backed by Key Vault CSI driver.
security.security_risks: Risk: MILP optimizer produces an infeasible schedule that dispatch-service blindly sends to BMS. Mitigation: optimizer-service validates schedule feasibility before storing; dispatch-service checks schedule status=validated before dispatch. Risk: LLM prompt injection via building names containing special characters. Mitigation: building names sanitised and inserted into LLM prompt template as escaped strings; system prompt instructs model to ignore instruction-like content in data fields.

data.entities: buildings (id UUID, portfolio_id UUID, name, bms_api_endpoint, timezone, floor_area_sqm, created_at). energy_meters (id UUID, building_id FK, meter_type ENUM main/hvac_zone/ev_charger/battery, zone_id, external_meter_id). hvac_zones (id UUID, building_id FK, zone_name, floor, area_sqm, setpoint_min, setpoint_max). tariff_schedules (id UUID, building_id FK, supplier, valid_from DATE, valid_to DATE, schedule_json JSONB). load_forecasts (id UUID, building_id FK, forecast_horizon_hours INT, generated_at TIMESTAMPTZ, zone_forecasts_json JSONB, model_version VARCHAR). optimization_schedules (id UUID, building_id FK, planning_horizon_start TIMESTAMPTZ, planning_horizon_hours INT, status ENUM draft/validated/approved/dispatched, schedule_payload_json JSONB, projected_savings_kwh FLOAT, solver_duration_seconds FLOAT). savings_records (id UUID, building_id FK, date DATE, baseline_kwh FLOAT, actual_kwh FLOAT, realized_savings_kwh FLOAT, realized_savings_pct FLOAT). ClickHouse table: meter_readings (meter_id UUID, ts DATETIME, value_kwh FLOAT32, quality_flag UInt8) ENGINE=MergeTree PARTITION BY toYYYYMM(ts) ORDER BY (meter_id, ts).
data.storage_design: ClickHouse for meter_readings; MergeTree engine with monthly partitions and meter_id+ts sort key for fast range queries per meter. PostgreSQL for all relational operational data. Redis for forecast cache: key=building:{id}:forecast, TTL 2 hours.
data.analytics_events: savings_record created (daily, per building), optimization_schedule dispatched (per dispatch event), forecast_mape computed (hourly, per building, pushed to Prometheus gauge), milp_solve_duration (histogram per solve, labels: building_id, zone_count).

devops.deployment_topology: AKS cluster (Standard_D4s_v3 nodes): api-gateway deployment (2-4 replicas), forecast-service deployment (2 replicas, GPU node pool for PyTorch), optimizer-service deployment (2-4 replicas, CPU-optimized), dispatch-service deployment (1-2 replicas). Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server. ClickHouse on AKS StatefulSet (3 nodes). Azure Cache for Redis. Azure Key Vault with AKS workload identity.
devops.ci_cd_design: Azure DevOps pipelines: main branch triggers build, test, Docker push to Azure Container Registry, Helm chart deploy to staging AKS. Production deploy via manual approval gate in Azure DevOps release pipeline. ML model pipeline separate: nightly retraining job with Azure ML experiment tracking; model promoted to production via model registry after MAPE validation gate.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus with custom metrics: forecast_mape_gauge (label: building_id), milp_solve_duration_seconds histogram, bms_dispatch_success_total, savings_attribution_lag_seconds. Grafana dashboards: per-building forecast accuracy, optimizer throughput, dispatch success rate, realized savings trend. Azure Monitor Alerts on BMS dispatch failure rate > 5%.
devops.rollback_strategy: Helm rollback for application services. ML model rollback via model registry version pin in ConfigMap; previous model version re-deployed within 10 minutes. ClickHouse schema migrations via Flyway with backward-compatible column additions only.

target_output.thinking_summary: First-pass strong plan for a commercial building energy AI system. Three independent AKS services for forecast (PyTorch LSTM), optimization (OR-Tools MILP), and dispatch (BMS REST API) enables separate scaling and deployment cadences. ClickHouse for meter telemetry at scale with PostgreSQL for relational operational data is the right dual-database pattern. Azure OpenAI GPT-4o for natural language savings report generation with structured JSON input keeps hallucination risk manageable. The 60-second MILP latency constraint for 20 zones is achievable with OR-Tools CP-SAT given the problem size. Key risks are BMS dispatch failures and forecast model drift.
target_output.title: Commercial Building Energy Optimization AI System
target_output.executive_summary: An AI system on AKS that reduces commercial building energy costs 20-35% by running hourly PyTorch LSTM demand forecasts per HVAC zone, solving a 48-hour MILP load scheduling problem via OR-Tools CP-SAT to identify off-peak pre-cooling windows, and dispatching the approved schedule to the building management system via REST API. ClickHouse stores meter telemetry at scale; PostgreSQL manages operational state. Azure OpenAI GPT-4o generates plain-language energy performance reports from structured savings summaries. Multi-tenant RBAC via Azure AD OIDC with portfolio-level data isolation.
target_output.architecture_overview: Microservice architecture on AKS with four deployments: api-gateway (FastAPI, Azure AD JWT auth, routes to internal services), forecast-service (FastAPI + PyTorch LSTM, batched 20-zone inference, writes cached forecast to Redis), optimizer-service (FastAPI + OR-Tools CP-SAT, reads tariff schedule and forecast from PostgreSQL/Redis, solves MILP, writes optimization_schedule to PostgreSQL), dispatch-service (FastAPI, reads approved schedules, calls BMS REST API with retry logic, writes audit log). Hourly AKS CronJob triggers forecast-service for each building. Nightly CronJob retrains LSTM on 30 days of ClickHouse meter readings. Daily SavingsAttributor job computes realized savings from prior-day meter data. Azure OpenAI integration in report-generator module invoked on-demand from api-gateway. All inter-service communication via mTLS on AKS service mesh.
target_output.technology_stack: Runtime: Python 3.12. API framework: FastAPI with uvicorn workers. ML inference: PyTorch 2.3 LSTM model served in forecast-service. Optimization: OR-Tools 9.x CP-SAT solver in optimizer-service. LLM: Azure OpenAI GPT-4o via openai Python SDK. Time-series DB: ClickHouse (MergeTree engine, AKS StatefulSet). Relational DB: Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server 16. Cache: Azure Cache for Redis 7. Auth: Azure AD OIDC via azure-identity. Secrets: Azure Key Vault with AKS workload identity CSI driver. CI/CD: Azure DevOps with Helm chart deployments. ML versioning: Azure ML model registry. Observability: Prometheus, Grafana, Azure Monitor.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Demand Forecasting: hourly PyTorch LSTM inference for each HVAC zone in a building using 168-hour (7-day) input window of meter readings, weather API data, and calendar features (day-of-week, holiday flags); outputs 48-hour zone-level consumption forecast stored in load_forecasts and cached in Redis. Load Scheduling Optimization: TariffEngine parses tariff_schedules JSONB to identify off-peak windows and demand charge avoidance periods; OR-Tools CP-SAT formulates MILP with binary pre-cooling activation variables per zone per time slot and continuous setpoint variables; objective: minimize total tariff cost subject to thermal comfort constraints (zone_setpoint_min/max); outputs optimization_schedules with projected_savings_kwh. BMS Dispatch: energy manager reviews and approves optimization_schedule; dispatch-service calls building BMS REST API to set HVAC zone schedules for the planning horizon; audit log records dispatch timestamp and BMS response. Savings Attribution: SavingsAttributor reads prior-day actual meter readings from ClickHouse, compares against baseline forecast, writes savings_records with realized_savings_kwh and realized_savings_pct. LLM Reporting: report-generator module assembles structured savings_summary JSON (30-day savings_records, forecast accuracy, top-saving zones), calls Azure OpenAI GPT-4o with sanitised prompt, returns 2-paragraph plain-English summary for energy manager distribution.
target_output.system_components: api-gateway (FastAPI, Azure AD JWT, routes to 3 internal services, LLM report endpoint). forecast-service (FastAPI, PyTorch LSTM model loaded from Azure ML registry, batched zone inference, Redis cache write, Prometheus forecast_mape gauge). optimizer-service (FastAPI, OR-Tools CP-SAT, TariffEngine, schedule feasibility validator, HPA on CPU 70%). dispatch-service (FastAPI, BMS REST client with exponential retry, dispatch audit log, Prometheus bms_dispatch_success_total). SavingsAttributor CronJob (daily, ClickHouse reader, savings_records writer). LSTM Retraining CronJob (nightly, Azure ML experiment, model registry promotion with MAPE gate). ClickHouse StatefulSet (3 nodes, MergeTree meter_readings). PostgreSQL Flexible Server. Redis Cache. Azure Key Vault + CSI driver. Grafana + Prometheus AKS stack.
target_output.workflows: Hourly Forecast Cycle: AKS CronJob triggers forecast-service for each active building. forecast-service queries ClickHouse for 168 hours of meter readings per zone, calls Weather API for the building location, assembles feature tensor, runs LSTM forward pass for all 20 zones in a single batch, writes load_forecasts record to PostgreSQL with zone_forecasts_json, writes cache key building:{id}:forecast to Redis with 2-hour TTL. Total per-building target: under 5 seconds. Daily Optimization and Dispatch: AKS CronJob at 06:00 local building time triggers optimizer-service. TariffEngine reads tariff_schedules for the next 48 hours, identifies off-peak windows and demand charge periods. Optimizer reads cached LSTM forecast from Redis, formulates MILP: 20 zones x 48 hourly time slots = 960 binary pre-cooling variables plus 960 continuous setpoint variables. OR-Tools CP-SAT solves with 60-second time limit. Feasibility check validates all thermal constraints satisfied. optimization_schedule written with status=draft and projected_savings_kwh. Energy manager receives dashboard notification, reviews schedule, clicks Approve. dispatch-service reads approved schedule, calls BMS API endpoint for each HVAC zone setpoint, logs each dispatch call with HTTP status to audit log. Dispatch completes 2 hours before first scheduled load shift.
target_output.data_model: buildings (id UUID PK, portfolio_id UUID, name VARCHAR, bms_api_endpoint VARCHAR, timezone VARCHAR, floor_area_sqm FLOAT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). energy_meters (id UUID PK, building_id FK buildings, meter_type ENUM main/hvac_zone/ev_charger/battery, zone_id UUID, external_meter_id VARCHAR). hvac_zones (id UUID PK, building_id FK, zone_name VARCHAR, floor INT, area_sqm FLOAT, setpoint_min FLOAT, setpoint_max FLOAT). tariff_schedules (id UUID PK, building_id FK, supplier VARCHAR, valid_from DATE, valid_to DATE, schedule_json JSONB). load_forecasts (id UUID PK, building_id FK, forecast_horizon_hours INT, generated_at TIMESTAMPTZ, zone_forecasts_json JSONB, model_version VARCHAR, mape_pct FLOAT). optimization_schedules (id UUID PK, building_id FK, planning_horizon_start TIMESTAMPTZ, planning_horizon_hours INT, status ENUM draft/validated/approved/dispatched, schedule_payload_json JSONB, projected_savings_kwh FLOAT, solver_duration_seconds FLOAT, approved_by VARCHAR, approved_at TIMESTAMPTZ). savings_records (id UUID PK, building_id FK, date DATE, baseline_kwh FLOAT, actual_kwh FLOAT, realized_savings_kwh FLOAT, realized_savings_pct FLOAT). ClickHouse: meter_readings (meter_id UUID, ts DateTime, value_kwh Float32, quality_flag UInt8) ENGINE=MergeTree PARTITION BY toYYYYMM(ts) ORDER BY (meter_id, ts) SETTINGS index_granularity=8192.
target_output.api_design: FastAPI REST at /api/v1. GET /api/v1/buildings/:id/forecast - returns cached LSTM forecast from Redis (or triggers on-demand if cache miss). POST /api/v1/buildings/:id/optimize - triggers MILP solve, returns optimization_schedule with projected_savings_kwh. GET /api/v1/buildings/:id/schedules - list optimization_schedules with status. PATCH /api/v1/buildings/:id/schedules/:sid/approve - energy manager approves schedule for dispatch. POST /api/v1/buildings/:id/schedules/:sid/dispatch - dispatch-service dispatches to BMS. GET /api/v1/buildings/:id/savings?from=DATE&to=DATE - savings_records aggregated over date range. POST /api/v1/buildings/:id/reports/generate - calls Azure OpenAI with 30-day savings summary, returns plain-language report text. GET /api/v1/admin/buildings - platform_operator only, list all buildings. Auth: Azure AD JWT at api-gateway; all queries filter by portfolio_id from token claims.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Azure AD OIDC with MFA via Conditional Access policy for all user roles. JWT validated at api-gateway using azure-identity public key fetch. RBAC enforced at route level: energy_manager can view and approve; facilities_director adds config write; platform_operator has admin access. Multi-tenant isolation: portfolio_id from JWT claim injected as WHERE filter on all PostgreSQL and ClickHouse queries; ORM-level filter prevents cross-portfolio data access. BMS API keys and Azure OpenAI API key stored in Azure Key Vault; accessed by pods via AKS workload identity (managed identity) and Key Vault CSI driver; never in environment variables. mTLS between all AKS services via service mesh (Linkerd). LLM prompt construction sanitises building names and strips procurement strategy fields before sending to Azure OpenAI; system prompt instructs model to treat all input as data not instructions. GDPR: building occupancy inference data (used as LSTM feature) subject to data minimisation; raw occupancy sensor data deleted after 7 days; aggregated occupancy features retained in load_forecasts. EU EED Article 8 compliance: 24-month meter_readings retention in ClickHouse.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: AKS cluster in Azure West Europe (eu-based building operators): node pools: system (Standard_D2s_v3), cpu-workers (Standard_D4s_v3 for optimizer), gpu-workers (Standard_NC4as_T4_v3 for PyTorch inference). Deployments: api-gateway (2-4 replicas, HPA on request rate), forecast-service (2 replicas, GPU nodeSelector), optimizer-service (2-4 replicas, HPA CPU 70%), dispatch-service (1-2 replicas). ClickHouse StatefulSet (3 nodes, Standard_L4s for NVMe storage). Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server (General Purpose, 4 vCores). Azure Cache for Redis Basic C2. Azure Container Registry for Docker images. Azure DevOps pipeline: ruff lint, mypy, pytest unit tests (mock OR-Tools and PyTorch), Docker build, Helm chart package, deploy to staging AKS on main merge, manual approval gate to production. ML pipeline: Azure ML nightly retraining job, MAPE < 10% promotion gate, model version tagged in Azure ML registry, ConfigMap updated in production cluster to point forecast-service to new model version.
target_output.observability: Prometheus custom metrics: forecast_mape_gauge{building_id} updated hourly by forecast-service after each inference run, milp_solve_duration_seconds histogram{building_id, zone_count}, bms_dispatch_success_total{building_id, outcome} counter, savings_attribution_lag_seconds gauge. Grafana dashboards: per-building forecast accuracy trend (MAPE over 30 days), optimizer solve time distribution, BMS dispatch success rate, realized vs. projected savings. Azure Monitor Alert rules: bms_dispatch_failure_rate > 5% over 1 hour, forecast_mape > 15% for any building (triggers model review), optimizer solve timeout > 60s (triggers scaling review). Structured JSON logs from all services; Azure Log Analytics workspace for cross-service query.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: AKS GPU node pool scales to zero outside hourly forecast window (09:00-17:00 building local time); estimated 800 USD/month per building at full operation. ClickHouse on NVMe nodes handles 1 billion meter readings per building-year with fast aggregation for savings queries. Optimizer CPU nodes HPA prevents idle compute. Redis TTL 2 hours on forecast cache reduces redundant inference re-runs. PostgreSQL connection pooling via PgBouncer sidecar for optimizer and savings query load. Total estimated cost 2,500-4,000 USD/month for a 5-building portfolio.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): Single building deployment, LSTM forecast model for HVAC zones trained on synthetic data, OR-Tools CP-SAT MILP formulation for HVAC pre-cooling, BMS dispatch REST integration for one supported BMS vendor, savings dashboard with projected vs. actual delta, Azure AD auth with energy_manager and platform_operator roles, ClickHouse + PostgreSQL data layer, AKS deployment with Azure DevOps CI. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Multi-building portfolio support with portfolio_id tenant isolation, LLM report generation via Azure OpenAI, admin portal for building configuration and tariff schedule management, nightly LSTM retraining pipeline with Azure ML model registry. Phase 3 (6 weeks): EV charging fleet scheduling added to MILP formulation, battery storage dispatch optimization, carbon intensity-aware scheduling using grid emissions API, multi-building Pareto optimization for portfolio-level savings maximisation.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 strict mypy. ruff for linting. OR-Tools MILP formulation unit-tested with fixed tariff and forecast fixtures; solver output validated against known optimal for small toy instances. PyTorch model loaded from Azure ML registry at service startup; model version logged in every load_forecasts record for traceability. LLM prompt template version-controlled; prompt changes require PR review from energy domain expert. All ClickHouse queries parameterised; no string interpolation. Portfolio_id filter added as mandatory ORM-level default scope to prevent accidental cross-tenant query. BMS API client has circuit breaker (pybreaker) to prevent dispatch-service hammering an unresponsive BMS.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: BMS API returns success but schedule not applied due to BMS firmware bug. Mitigation: dispatch-service reads back HVAC setpoints 15 minutes after dispatch and logs discrepancy to audit log; alerts if > 20% of zones show mismatch. Risk: LSTM forecast MAPE degrades above 15% in summer due to heat wave distribution shift. Mitigation: Azure Monitor alert triggers model review; nightly retraining automatically incorporates recent anomalous weather; fallback to rule-based scheduling if MAPE exceeds 20%. Tradeoff: OR-Tools CP-SAT over Gurobi trades commercial license cost for open-source flexibility; CP-SAT is sufficient for the 960-variable problem size but Gurobi could be adopted for larger portfolios. Tradeoff: separate forecast and optimizer services over a monolith increases operational complexity but allows GPU/CPU node pool separation and independent retraining cadence.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Azure OpenAI chosen over self-hosted open-source LLM for report generation because occupancy data has no PII after aggregation, making Azure OpenAI data processing terms acceptable. OR-Tools CP-SAT chosen over linear relaxation because pre-cooling activation variables are inherently binary (zone is either pre-cooling or not). ClickHouse on AKS StatefulSet chosen over Azure Data Explorer to avoid proprietary lock-in; operational overhead acceptable given dedicated DevOps support.

metadata.notes: Commercial building energy optimization AI system with LSTM forecasting, OR-Tools MILP scheduling, and Azure OpenAI reporting; strong first-pass plan.
